- The Paralyzed CEO Hired A Single Dad As Her Driver — Then He Changed: Kind: captions Language: en The paralyzed CEO hired a single dad as her driver. Then he changed her entire life. Eleanor Whitmore was once the kind of woman New York could not ignore. At 32, she had built Whitmore Dynamics into one of the most feared medical technology companies on the East Coast. The press called her steel CEO. She ran on 4 hours of sleep, marathon medals, and iron discipline. Then one rain soaked night. Everything she had built, everything she was, was ripped from her in a single deafening crash. A year later, she sat alone inside a glasswalled mansion in silence, moving through her life on wheels and slowly deciding that perhaps she was already done. Then she hired a driver named Caleb Hunter, a broke single father who had nothing to offer but steadiness. And somehow, impossibly, he changed every single thing. If you believe one decent human being can pull another person back from the edge, stay until the very last word. New York had a particular cruelty reserved for those who fell from great heights. The city that once printed Eleanor Whitmore's face on the covers of business magazines did not pause out of sympathy. It simply moved on to the next story, the next name, the next empire being built or burned. For years before the accident, Eleanor had been the story everyone wanted to tell. She had taken her father's struggling biotech firm, stripped it down to its skeleton at the age of 26, and rebuilt it into Witmore Dynamics, a company that now employed over 3,000 people, and partnered with hospital networks across 14 states. She appeared at industry conferences with the kind of quiet authority that made senior men twice her age straighten their ties. She ran marathons on Sunday mornings, signed contracts worth nine figures on Monday afternoons, and slept dreamlessly in a penthouse apartment that she had designed herself down to the last fixture. Every inch of her life was intentional, controlled, and moving forward. Then came the night of November 14th. It had been a late corporate dinner, a routine one, nothing unusual. Her town car had been winding through the wet streets of lower Manhattan when the driver, Marcus, lost control on a rainslick turn. The car jumped a curb, struck a concrete barrier, and rolled. Marcus was killed on impact. Eleanor was pulled from the wreckage alive, but broken in ways the surgeons explained with clinical calm, and she understood only in the weeks that followed when she realized her legs would not move no matter how many times she willed them, too. The damage to her lower spine was severe. The doctors were honest without being cruel. Partial paralysis, they said, possibly permanent. Physical therapy could help, but she should prepare herself for a life that looked different from the one she had known. She had lain in that hospital bed and stared at the ceiling for a very long time. The months that followed were a different kind of wreckage. Her fiance, a charming architect named Derek, held her hand through the first three weeks of recovery. By the second month, he grew quieter. By the third, he left with a careful, measured explanation about how he was not built for this kind of life, as if her broken spine were a lifestyle choice he was politely declining. Eleanor did not cry when he left. She had already gone somewhere cold inside, a place where she felt very little and preferred it that way. The press found the story irresistible. Iron CEO falls Whitmore erys fighting for more than her company. The headlines were sympathetic on the surface and voracious underneath. Photographers stationed themselves outside the rehabilitation center. Her board of directors held closed door meetings she was not invited to. Sebastian Hail, her CFO, a man who had always worn his ambition like a second skin, began making quiet rounds among the board members, planting seeds of doubt. Was Eleanor still sharp enough? Was she still the right face for a company that sold health and vitality to the world? Could a woman in a wheelchair truly lead Whitmore Dynamics into its next phase? Ellaner knew what Sebastian was doing. She had always known exactly who Sebastian was, but knowing and having the strength to fight were two entirely different things. She had moved into the family mansion in Greenwich after leaving the hospital, a sprawling estate with wide corridors and a ground floor that had been retrofitted for her wheelchair. The house was beautiful and enormous and completely oppressively silent. Her assistant Amelia came every morning. A rotating team of nurses managed her medication schedule. A physical therapist named Lucas Bennett visited three times a week and reported carefully that Eleanor's progress was slower than it should be for someone of her age and physical condition. He did not say what he was really thinking. He did not have to. The truth was simple. Eleanor Whitmore had stopped wanting to get better. She sat at her desk for 12 and 14 hours a day because work was the only language she still trusted. She ate whatever Amelia left for her without tasting it. She had not opened the curtains in her bedroom since moving in. The wall of her dressing room held six pairs of running shoes she would never put on again, and she had spent one terrible Tuesday evening putting every one of her old marathon medals into a cardboard box and sending it to storage. She did not want to look at them. She did not want to be reminded of who she had been. Her driver of six months, a stiff, humorless man named Gerald, handed in his resignation on a Friday afternoon without much explanation. He had simply reached his limit. Eleanor had not been easy. She had been, by any honest measure, nearly impossible. She thanked him with the same distant politeness she reserved for everyone now, and told Amelia to arrange a replacement. She did not expect it to matter. She expected nothing to matter. She was wrong. Caleb Hunter had not expected this particular Tuesday to be the day his luck turned. He had stopped expecting luck somewhere around the time the hospital called to say his wife Rachel was gone 4 years earlier on a gray March morning. Their daughter Grace asleep in her car seat in the parking garage downstairs while he stood in a hallway and stopped breathing for a moment that felt like it lasted several years. He was 34 now. He lived in a two-bedroom apartment in Bridgeport with Grace, who was seven and had her mother's eyes and a stubbornness that Caleb privately believed she had gotten from him. He had done long haul trucking for 2 years after Rachel died because it paid well and the road gave him something to do with the grief. When Grace turned five, he quit. He could not keep leaving her. He picked up whatever work he could find, delivering packages, driving for private car services. One month of night, security at a warehouse that smelled like chemicals and regret. The money was always two weeks behind where it needed to be. He was three months behind on rent. His landlord, a broad shouldered man named Frank, who had been patient longer than most people would have been, had left a final notice on his door the previous Wednesday. More pressing than the rent, though, was the letter from the law firm of Harrove and Welsh attorneys representing the family of his late wife Rachel, specifically her parents, who had filed for partial custody of Grace. The argument in the filing was precisely what he feared. financial instability, no fixed routine, inadequate provision, the kind of language that sounded reasonable on paper and felt like a knife in the chest when you read it at 2:00 in the morning at a kitchen table in a too small apartment. Grace was everything. Caleb was not a man who used words like everything lightly. He used very few words lightly, but that particular word was accurate. Without grace, the last four years made no sense at all. He was not going to lose her. He had decided that with the same quiet finality he applied to most decisions. No drama, no announcement, just a line drawn somewhere deep and permanent. He had seen the listing for the personal driver position at Whitmore Dynamics on a job board. The salary was nearly three times what he had been making. The requirements were extensive, clean record, professional references, physical fitness, discretion, availability at all hours. The listing also noted that the role required above average emotional resilience and the ability to work closely with an individual who had specific accessibility needs. He read that line twice. Then he called the number and scheduled an interview. The morning of the interview, he laid out his best shirt, a white button-down he had owned for 5 years that was clean and pressed and showed no visible wear if he did not look too too carefully. He drove Grace to school, told her he had an important meeting, and she looked up at him with those enormous brown eyes and said, "You always make things better, Dad. It's kind of your thing." He had not known what to say to that. So, he kissed her forehead and got back in the car. The lobby of the building where the interviews were held was the kind of space designed to make you feel small. Marble floors, a ceiling that went up three stories, young professionals in tailored clothes moving with the particular urgency of people who were paid very well to seem busy. Caleb sat in one of eight chairs arranged in a row outside a conference room. The other candidates wore suits. He wore his pressed white shirt and a pair of clean dark trousers. He did not feel embarrassed by the difference. He felt nothing in particular. He was there for the job, not the room. Amelia Brooks conducted the first round of interviews herself. She was efficient and had the particular quality of someone who had worked for a difficult person long enough that nothing surprised her anymore. She moved through the candidates quickly, checking things off a mental list. When she reached Caleb, she asked the standard questions and then studied him a moment longer than she had studied the others. He was not trying to impress her. Every other candidate had been performing adjusting posture, modulating tone, watching her face for cues. Caleb just sat there and answered as if he were recounting facts about someone else's life. When she asked how he handled stress, he thought about it for an actual moment before answering. I don't panic. That's about the most I can promise. Eleanor arrived near the end of the session, not to conduct the interview herself, but to observe. She appeared in the doorway of the conference room in her wheelchair, and the shift in the room's energy was immediate. The remaining candidates straightened up. She noticed it and appeared entirely unmoved by it. She scanned the row of chairs with the expression of someone searching for something useful in a drawer full of things that were not. Her eyes settled on Caleb for a few seconds. He met the look without adjustment. She turned to Amelia and said, "That one." And wheeled back out of the room. Amelia looked mildly surprised. Caleb looked at neither of them. He was already thinking about the paperwork he would need to sign that afternoon. As he stood to follow Amelia out, she caught his arm briefly and said, "Just so you know, three people have quit this job in the past 8 months." He considered that for a moment. Then he said, "That's useful to know." He took the job. The first morning, Caleb arrived at the mansion. He pulled the Black Company sedan into the circular drive at 7:58, 2 minutes early. Eleanor's previous drivers had apparently made a habit of arriving at exactly 8, which she found predictable in an irritating way. She noticed the 2 minutes without commenting on them. The morning routine was established without ceremony. He opened the rear passenger door before she reached it. He lifted the folded wheelchair into the trunk with the ease of someone who had looked up the exact procedure the night before and practiced it. He did not offer to help her into the car unless she indicated she needed it. He did not narrate his movements. He drove with both hands on the wheel, the radio off unless she requested it, and spoke only when she asked him something directly. Eleanor spent the first three days waiting for him to do something that annoyed her. He did not. He was not obsequious. He was not hovering. He did not ask how she was feeling or deliver careful smiles intended to show her how deeply he understood her situation. He treated her the way one reasonably competent person treats another reasonably competent person, as if the logistics of her disability were simply a set of conditions to be navigated, and the rest of her was none of his particular to business. It was disorienting. She had grown accustomed to people treating her as either fragile or tragic, and she had responded to both with the same flat hostility. Caleb gave her nothing to push against. He was simply there, reliable and quiet. And she found herself watching him in the rear view mirror with an attention she had not intended to give him. The first real friction came on a Thursday. She had a meeting at a medical technology conference center on the west side and the accessible entrance ran down a steep ramp that had not been properly maintained. One of the wheels caught in a gap in the concrete and the chair lurched sideways. Caleb was beside her in two steps, stabilizing without grabbing, adjusting the angle so she could navigate forward. She did not thank him, which was habit. He did not look wounded, which was unusual. As she rolled through the door, he said without particular emphasis. You're not weak. That ramp is just poorly designed. She stopped for half a second. Then she went inside. She thought about the sentence for the rest of the meeting. Not because it was profound, but because no one had said anything like it to her in a very long time. Everyone around her spoke about her limitations with the gentle tragedy of a funeral they were already attending. Caleb had simply identified a problem with the ramp. As if she were fine, and the ramp was the one that needed fixing. The shift was so small, she might have missed it if she had not been paying close attention. 10 days into the job, Caleb asked apologetically but without visible distress whether he might bring his daughter along for a late evening pickup. His babysitter had canled and the alternative was leaving Grace alone for 3 hours which he was not going to do. Ellaner said nothing for a long moment. Then she said one time Grace arrived wearing a purple jacket with a broken zipper and carrying a paperback book about deep sea creatures. She climbed into the front seat with a focused seriousness that Eleanor noticed immediately. The child did not stare. She said hello to Eleanor with the directness of someone who had been raised to look people in the eye and then opened her book. Elellanor watched her for a few miles and said nothing. It was Grace who broke the silence. She looked up from a page about angler fish and said with genuine curiosity and zero awareness of the sensitivity involved, "Do you remember what it feels like to run really fast?" The inside of the car went completely still. Eleanor felt the question land somewhere behind her ribs, not as pain exactly, but as pressure. Caleb kept his eyes on the road. Then Grace said as naturally as if she were finishing a thought about the weather. Because if you can't run anymore, you can still go look at things. That's what my dad says. Moving isn't the point. Seeing is the point. Eleanor turned to look out the window. The city moved past the glass in columns of light, and she did not speak for a long time. When they reached the mansion, she got out without a word. Caleb helped with the chair and she moved toward the door and then stopped. She said without turning around. Good night, Grace. Good night, Grace said cheerfully and went back to her fish. That night, for the first time since November, Eleanor reached over and pulled the bedroom curtain back. She sat in the dark and looked out at the city. the lights, the motion, the impossible indifferent beauty of a place that had not stopped for her, and she felt something very small and very stubborn stir somewhere in her chest. She did not have a name for it yet. But she did not close the curtain. Spring came slowly to Greenwich, the way it always did, cautious at first, then committed. The trees along the mansion's drive went from bare to budded in what felt like a single week, and Eleanor, who had spent the previous autumn and winter entirely inside her own head, began to notice them. Caleb had started suggesting small deviations from the direct route between home and the office. Not dramatically, just a drive through the park road where the cherry trees were flowering or a stop at a bakery on the way back from a meeting because he had noticed Eleanor had not eaten by noon and the kitchen staff reported she was not eating in the evenings either. He didn't make announcements about these things. He just did them and let her decide. She always decided to go along. She didn't examine why. Their conversations evolved the same way in small unforced increments. She asked him one afternoon why he had quit longhaul driving and he told her in the same even tone he used for everything. He couldn't keep being away from Grace. She asked whether it had been hard to give up the income. He said it had been very hard and he would make the same decision again. She looked at him for a long moment. Then she went back to her tablet and they didn't speak for another 20 minutes. But something in the silence was different than it had been before. He didn't ask her about the accident. He didn't ask about Derek. He didn't ask about the board or the company or whether she was afraid. These were the things every other person in her life orbited around carefully or clumsily but constantly. Caleb seemed to take the position that her catastrophes were hers to bring up if she chose to, and that in the meantime they could talk about other things, the weather, Grace's ongoing obsession with bioluminescent creatures, the absurdity of certain highway on-ramps, whether coffee from the corner cart downtown was genuinely better, or whether she only thought so because she was outside when she drank it. She began to laugh. Not performatively, not politely. Actually, genuinely laugh. Once at something Grace said, once at something Caleb said about a parking enforcement officer and a very specific cone placement situation. The laughs were small and brief, and she was almost startled by them both times. Amelia noticed. Lucas Bennett noticed. Her mother, Hannah Whitmore, called from her home in Boston and said Eleanor sounded different. Hannah didn't say better because she was cautious, but she said different. Grace had begun drawing pictures and leaving them on the back seat of the sedan for Elellanor to find. Marine animals mostly with elaborate names Grace invented. One afternoon, there was a drawing of a woman in a wheelchair looking up at the sky with a crown on her head made of what appeared to be starfish written at the bottom in uneven block letters. For the pretty wheelchair lady. Eleanor held the drawing for a long time without saying anything. She was in the back seat alone. She allowed herself to cry for about 45 seconds, which was the longest she had cried since leaving the hospital. Then she folded the drawing carefully and put it in the inside pocket of her jacket. It was Caleb who told her about Rachel, about his wife, not as a bid for sympathy, but as a piece of information he apparently decided she had earned the right to know. He told her simply, she had been ill, a rare autoimmune condition that progressed faster than anyone predicted, and she had been 30, one years old, and Grace had been three. He had spent several months afterward in a state that he described with characteristic understatement as not functional. He had pulled out of it because he had to, because Grace needed him to, not because he had figured anything out or healed in any particular way, but because someone needed him to be present and he had decided to be present. Ellaner asked how he had gotten through it. He looked at the road for a moment. Then he said, "Most days I didn't live because I wanted to. I lived because she needed breakfast." Eleanor sat with that for a long time. "That's an honest answer," she said finally. "It's the only one I've got." She started showing up to her physical therapy sessions with Lucas with something she hadn't brought before. Attention. Real attention. She pushed harder in the sessions, asked better questions, stopped treating the exercises as obligations to be discharged, and started treating them as problems to be solved. Lucas called it a behavioral shift. He was careful not to say too much about where he thought it had come from. By the end of April, Eleanor Whitmore was not healed. She was still in the wheelchair. She still had mornings that were very dark and very quiet. But she was moving towards something, not away from it. And the difference between those two directions was everything. Sebastian Hail had a gift for reading rooms, and the room had changed. He noticed it first in a board prep meeting in midappril. Eleanor sat at the head of the table with a sharpness in her eyes that had been absent for months. She cut off two digressions, caught a discrepancy in the quarterly projections that three other people had missed, and left the room with the purposeful silence that used to precede her largest moves. Sebastian drove home that night with his jaw tight. He had spent 8 months cultivating a particular narrative with the board carefully, patiently. The narrative was that Eleanor was not recovering, that her judgment had been compromised, that Whitmore dynamics needed stable leadership, and stable leadership looked very much like Sebastian Hail. He had three board members who were sympathetic, one actively in his corner and a full proposal for a structured leadership transition, sitting in an encrypted folder on his personal laptop. He had been waiting for Eleanor to collapse under her own weight. She was inconveniently not collapsing. He started with the driver. A few discreet inquiries through a private research service turned up the relevant facts quickly. Caleb Hunter was financially exposed significantly behind on rent. Facing a custody challenge from his late wife's family, no substantial savings, no safety net, there was leverage there. Sebastian was good at leverage. He arranged a meeting through an intermediary. a brief untraceable coffee at a neutral location and made the offer with the directness he normally reserved for acquisition talks, a significant sum of money in exchange for a resignation. No explanation required. Caleb listened to the entire proposal without expression. Then he said no. Sebastian had not expected that which irritated him more than the refusal itself. Men in Caleb's position said yes. The math was straightforward. He tried again, more explicitly, making clear that refusing was not in Caleb's interest. Caleb said, "I heard you the first time." He stood up and left. Sebastian moved to plan two. He began circulating a story, not directly, never directly, through the careful architecture of suggestion and implication that was his native language. The story was that Caleb Hunter had cultivated a relationship with Eleanor for strategic personal gain. That his influence over her judgment was a liability. The board should consider that a vulnerable CEO becoming emotionally dependent on an unvetted employee was a governance concern. The story found ground. Two board members raised questions. One called for an independent review. Hannah Whitmore flew down from Boston and had a long uncomfortable phone conversation with Eleanor urging caution. The press got a thread of the story and published a piece with a photograph of Caleb and Grace outside the mansion framed through careful cropping to appear more intimate than it was. Eleanor saw the piece on a Tuesday evening and felt a cold, specific fury settle over her. Caleb arrived the next morning and knew immediately that something had shifted. Eleanor was still and controlled in the way that meant she was holding something very tight. She looked at him directly when he came in and said without preamble, "Are you planning to quit?" He said, "I've been thinking about it." The directness of it, the fact that he didn't deny it cut through something she had not expected to feel. She was quiet for a moment. Then she said very carefully, "Why? because the noise around this is getting in your way," he said. "And you don't need me making things harder for you." Eleanor looked at him for a long time. Something was happening behind her eyes, something she was not accustomed to and did not immediately know what to do with. When she spoke again, her voice was steady, but only just. "You are the only person in my life right now," she said, "who makes me feel like I'm not already dead." The words landed between them in the quiet house, and neither of them moved for a moment. He did not say anything immediately. He stood there with the car keys still in his hand, and whatever he was deciding, he decided it without involving her in the process. Then he said, "Okay, I'll stay." He set the keys on the table and went to bring the car around. The thing that unraveled Sebastian Hail's careful construction began not with a dramatic discovery, but with a small practical observation Caleb made on a Wednesday morning in May. He had been reviewing the sedan's service records, a habit from his years on the road, where you learned quickly that ignoring small mechanical anomalies was the fastest way to end up broken down on the shoulder of Interstate 90 in January. He noticed that the vehicle file for Eleanor's car from the night of the accident contained a service notation that had been amended twice small changes. The kind of administrative adjustments that could mean nothing or could mean precisely one specific thing. The original inspection had flagged an anomaly in the braking system before the car went out that evening. The amended version did not mention it. He said nothing about it to Eleanor. He called Owen Price instead. Owen Price was Whitmore Dynamics's chief legal counsel, a precise, cautious man in his late 50s who had served the company for over a decade and who had one distinguishing quality that made him valuable above all others. He was not afraid of the truth when he found it. Caleb laid out what he had observed quietly and specifically, and Owen listened without interrupting. Owen made two calls that afternoon. What came back to him over the following 10 days was not comfortable. There had been a conversation between a service contractor who maintained the company fleet and someone with a direct line to the company's internal scheduling system. The conversation had happened 48 hours before Eleanor's accident. The service log had been altered after the crash quietly, professionally by someone with both the access and the motivation to make a paper trail disappear. Owen gave Caleb the name of a former city traffic investigator he trusted. And Caleb used a contact from his long haul days, a man who had spent years logging roadside incidents to identify which traffic monitoring systems covered the route of the accident. What the footage showed was not ambiguous. The car had experienced brake failure, not as a result of the rainy conditions, not as a result of driver error. The brake line had been tampered with. The failure was engineered. The name at the end of every thread at the bottom of the access logs. In the contractor's call history, behind the series of shell messages moving through the company's internal communication system was Sebastian Hail. Caleb told Eleanor on a Sunday evening. He sat across from her in the main sitting room of the mansion and explained what he and Owen had found in the same quiet, factual tone he used for everything. He did not editorialize. He did not soften it. Eleanor did not speak for almost 3 minutes after he finished. She was very still. The kind of stillness that is not peace, but the opposite, the absolute compression of something massive before it moves. She had known Sebastian was ambitious. She had known he was calculating. She had not known, had not let herself consider that he was this. Marcus was dead because of this. She was in this wheelchair because of this. Not bad luck, not weather, not the simple terrible randomness of a wet road at night. Because Sebastian Hail had decided that her disability was cheaper to manufacture than her removal. She looked at Caleb and said with a quietness that was more frightening than anger. How long have you known? 2 weeks, he said. I wanted to be certain before I told you. She nodded slowly. That week, she received a message from Sebastian through official channels requesting her attendance at an emergency session of the board of directors scheduled for the following Friday. The framing was standard organizational review, forward planning, but the subtext was clear. He had support. He had a proposal. He intended to walk out of that room with her resignation. He had spent 11 months engineering this moment. She replied that she would be there. Three days later, she and Owen walked the federal agents through everything. The night before the board meeting, Grace drew a picture of a lion. She left it on the backseat of the car with a note that said in her irregular printing, "You are the lion." Caleb found it before Eleanor got in and said nothing, just placed it on the seat beside her. Eleanor looked at it for the full drive into Manhattan and said nothing either, but she held it the entire time. The boardroom on the 42nd floor of Whitmore Dynamics headquarters had a wall of windows that looked out over the city skyline. And on a clear Friday morning in late May, the light came in horizontal and brilliant, turning the long conference table into something that looked almost ceremonial. Sebastian Hail arrived early. He took the chair to the right of center, not the head, because that was nominally Eleanor's, but close enough to make a point. He looked comfortable. He had prepared for this. the way he prepared for everything thoroughly with redundancy with contingency plans for each plausible version of what might happen. What he had not planned for was Eleanor Whitmore walking, wheeling into that room looking like herself. She came through the double doors at 9:00 with Amelia behind her and Caleb three steps back, wearing his usual expression of composed absence. Eleanor wore a dark navy suit she had not put on since before the accident. Her hair was up. Her hands were steady on the wheels. Sebastian watched her cross the room and felt for the first time in a long time something he did not immediately recognize as doubt. She greeted the board members by name. She sat at the head of the table without help and set a single folder in front of her. Sebastian opened his remarks with practiced smoothness. He thanked everyone for attending. He acknowledged the company's exceptional quarter. And then he moved with the rehearsed sadness of someone who had practiced it into his central argument. The question of leadership stability, the question of Eleanor's capacity, given the circumstances of the past year, to shepherd the company through its next phase. He did not say she was incapable. He said the company deserved certainty. He said this with genuine sounding compassion. Three board members nodded. One looked at his phone. Elellanar waited until he was done. Then she said, "Thank you, Sebastian." She opened the folder. The first document she placed on the table was a certified copy of the original vehicle service record, the unaltered version, recovered through Owen Price's legal process, she explained without raising her voice, what it showed. The second document was the communication log from the contractor. The third was a transcript of the relevant calls. The fourth was a forensic analysis of the brake line prepared by the independent investigator, confirming that the failure was not mechanical wear, but deliberate intervention. She let each document sit for the 30 seconds it took the people in the room to understand it. Then she said, "The accident that put me in this chair was not an accident. The room was so quiet that the ventilation system was audible. Sebastian had gone very still. His face had the specific blankness of someone whose internal processing has briefly exceeded its capacity. Eleanor looked at him directly. "You made a miscalculation," she said. "You assumed that breaking me physically would break me entirely. You assumed that the people around me would all behave the way you do, that everything has a price and everyone can be leveraged." She paused. "One person in my life did not work that way, and that turned out to matter quite a lot. The room's attention moved briefly and collectively to Caleb, who was standing near the back wall in the practice non-presence of someone who drove people places for a living and had decided that this particular moment was not his. Sebastian tried to speak. He managed half a sentence about documentation providence before Owen Price interrupted him with the specific courtesy of someone who has spent decades saying unpleasant things in the most legally precise manner possible. The two agents who had been waiting in the building's lobby came upstairs at Owen's signal. Sebastian did not make a scene. He was the kind of man who had always believed until the moment evidence contradicted him that things would go the way he arranged them. He left the room between the two agents with the particular stunned stillness of someone experiencing a reality that had not been in his plan. The charges were extensive. fraud, financial misconduct, and conspiracy to cause grievous harm. The case would take 18 months to resolve. Sebastian would not return to the building. The room remained quiet for a moment after the doors closed behind him. Then Eleanor looked around the table at the board members who had been cultivated over the past year into Sebastian's quiet coalition. She did not look triumphant. She looked tired and clear and entirely present. I want to talk about the next two years. She said, "If anyone has concerns about my capacity to lead this company, I'd like to hear them now rather than in a series of private meetings." Nobody spoke. She opened a second folder and began the meeting. Caleb left quietly 20 minutes in. He went downstairs and sat in the car and ate the sandwich he had packed that morning. He texted Grace's school to confirm her afternoon pickup. Then he leaned back in the seat and looked at the ceiling. He felt something that took him a moment to identify a quiet specific satisfaction that lived somewhere between pride and relief. He thought about Rachel for a few minutes. The way he sometimes allowed himself to in idle moments. He thought she would have found Eleanor wit more interesting. She had always liked people who were difficult in ways that were worth the effort. He was still thinking about this when Eleanor came downstairs 2 hours later. The summer that followed was slower and lighter than any Eleanor had lived in a long time. The investigation proceeded efficiently. Whitmore dynamics stabilized quickly once the board was clear of Sebastian's influence, and it became apparent through the forensic accounting that he had systematically misrepresented the company's projected liabilities to erode the board's confidence in Eleanor. With that fiction dismantled, the company's actual position was significantly stronger than the board had known. She rebuilt the executive team carefully. She brought in a new CFO from Boston, a woman named Patricia Landis, with the kind of precise, unscentimental intelligence Eleanor recognized as similar to her own. She restructured two underperforming divisions and accepted three speaking invitations she had turned down over the past year. She also continued physical therapy with Lucas Bennett. But something had fundamentally changed in that relationship, too. She had stopped going to prove something to other people and started going because she genuinely wanted to know what her body was still capable of. The progress was slow and real and uncertain in exactly the way that true recovery is uncertain. Not a straight line, not a narrative arc, just incremental stubborn biological fact. By August, she could stand with support. Not walk, not yet, and possibly not ever in the way she once had, but stand upright with the parallel bars in Lucas's therapy room for 23 seconds the first time and 41 the second time. And she did not count after that because she had stopped caring about the number and started caring about the feeling. The feeling was extraordinary. The company held its annual internal leadership summit in late September. 300 employees, the full executive team, the board. It was the first one Eleanor had attended since the accident. She had given a brief video address the previous year, which she had recorded in the mansion study with the curtains drawn behind her, and which she had been unable to watch back afterward. This year, she attended in person. She had not planned what happened at the end of her opening remarks. She had written a speech, focused, strategic, forwardlooking. She delivered it cleanly. And then at the end, as she was reaching for the wheels of her chair to push back from the speaker's table, something made her stop. She put her hands on the table's edge instead. Lucas was in the back of the room. He had been invited, technically as a plus one of Amelia's, and he stood up slightly when he saw what Eleanor was doing. She pushed herself upright, both hands on the table, arms trembling with the effort, the room going completely silent around her. She stood for a moment that lasted by the clock on the wall 11 seconds by any other measure, considerably longer. Grace was in the side hall, technically not allowed in the conference space, but present because Caleb had been unable to find a sitter, and Amelia had decided that this was a special circumstance. She was watching through the glass panel and the door. When Eleanor stood, Grace pushed the door open and ran across the room with the absolute disregard for protocol. That is the exclusive privilege of seven-year-olds in moments of genuine feeling and flung her arms around Eleanor's waist and said loudly and clearly into the suddenly very quiet room. I knew you could do it. I knew the whole entire time. Eleanor sat back down. She put one hand on Grace's hair and held her for a moment. She did not cry. She was not going to cry in front of 300 people. And she did not. But her eyes were bright and her hands were not steady. And across the room, Caleb, who was leaning against the far wall with his arms crossed, looked at the floor for a moment in the way that people look at the floor when they are keeping something in. Afterward, in the parking garage, Eleanor sat in the back of the sedan while Caleb loaded the chair. The summit crowd was filtering out around them, voices and footsteps in the distant hum of the elevator. Grace had fallen asleep on the seat beside Eleanor with her cheek against Eleanor's arm. One hand still faintly gripping Eleanor's sleeve. "She's out," Caleb said, glancing back as he got into the driver's seat. "I noticed," Eleanor said. She looked down at Grace for a moment. Then she said, "I want to tell you something. He waited. You didn't fix my legs." She said, "I want you to know that I understand that. You didn't come in here and solve the medical problem or change the physical reality. I'm still in this chair. I might always be in this chair." She paused. But somewhere between that conversation about the ramp and Grace's drawings and whatever it was you said about breakfast, I started believing I was worth fixing. And I hadn't believed that for a long time. Maybe not since before the accident. Caleb was quiet for a moment. Outside a car moved past. I think he would have gotten there, he said. Maybe, she said, but I got there faster. They drove home through the September evening, the city warm and lit around them. Grace asleep between them in the back seat with her hands still curled around the sleeve of Eleanor's jacket. Caleb drove the way he always drove, steady, unhurried, both hands on the wheel. Eleanor watched the city move past the window. She thought about what she had said and about what he had not said in return and about the particular quality of silence that exists between two people who are not quite yet saying the most important thing. I'd like to know, she said, not looking at him. Whether you think about this differently than I do, he glanced at her in the mirror. Think about what? He said. She kept her eyes on the window. Whether this is just a job to you? The pause that followed was long enough to be an answer of its own. When he spoke, his voice was the same even register he used for everything. But she had learned over these months that evenness was not the same as absence. It stopped being just a job, he said, around the time you put Grace's drawing in your jacket. Eleanor looked at him then in the mirror. He met her eyes for just a moment before looking back at the road. She did not say anything else. Neither did he. But when they reached the mansion and he came around to help with the chair and their hands overlapped briefly on the armrest in the practiced choreography of a 100 assisted transfers. Neither of them moved away immediately. The night was quiet around them. Grace stirred slightly in the back seat. The stars were not visible over the city's light, but the sky was clear. Caleb said very quietly. I'm not going anywhere. Eleanor looked at him for a long moment. Then she said, "Good." 3 weeks later, on a clear Sunday afternoon in October, Caleb pushed Eleanor's wheelchair along the winding path that ran through Benny Park in Greenwich. The maples had turned, and the light was coming in sideways through orange and gold leaves, and the whole world had that particular quality of autumn Sundays that feels like the year taking a slow, clean breath before everything changes. Grace ran ahead of them on the path, arms out at her sides, narrating what appeared to be a complex adventure involving a crew of marine biologists and a previously undiscovered species of luminous crab. She stopped occasionally to report back on her findings, addressed primarily to Eleanor, who had developed the habit of responding to Grace's monologues with the seriousness they deserved. Eleanor had a cup of coffee from the cart near the park's east entrance. Caleb had his hands on the chair's handles. They were not talking, which was fine. They had gotten very good at silence, the productive kind, the kind that did not require filling. A woman walking a dog passed them and smiled at the three of them. The automatic warm smile people give to what looks like a family on a Sunday afternoon. Eleanor watched her go. She thought about how strange it was that a smile like that could mean so many things at once. how it could be both assumption and accident and somehow still perfectly correct. She had thought for most of the past year that her life had ended on November 14th. That whatever came after the accident was not really living but a kind of extended administrative process managing the wreckage of the person she had been. She had been willing to run out that clock with a kind of dignified exhaustion that she had mistaken for acceptance. What she understood now sitting in the autumn light with a cup of coffee and a man's steady hands on the back of her chair and a seven-year-old shouting new taxonomic names at the geese was that the accident had ended a version of her. The version that ran on control and isolation and the belief that self-sufficiency was the same thing as safety. That version had been fast and bright and real, and she did not mourn it without some complexity. But the version she was now slower, uncertain, incapable of pretending to be unaffected was not lesser. It was just different. And it was hers. And it was on balance more honest than anything she had been before. Grace came running back at full speed and grabbed the side of the wheelchair with both hands and said breathlessly, "There are actual swans. Two of them. We have to go look right now." Immediately. Eleanor said. Lee lead the way. Grace took off again. One arm extended behind her to make sure they were following. Caleb pushed the chair forward and they went to look at the swans. She had once believed the most terrifying thing in the world was losing control of her body. She had been wrong. The most terrifying thing was losing the belief that you were worth anything when that control was gone. a driver named Caleb Hunter, a quiet man with a child who loved sea creatures and a way of looking at broken ramps that had somehow rearranged her entire inner architecture had given that belief back to her slowly and without ceremony in the way that most real and lasting things are given. Not through grand gestures or declarations, but through breakfast, through 2 minutes early, through your not weak. The ramp is just poorly designed. Through staying, Elellanor had thought her story ended the night her car hit a concrete barrier on a rain soaked street in lower Manhattan. But stories rarely end where we think they do. Sometimes the ending is somewhere quieter than the fall, a Sunday in October, coffee in your hand, a seven-year-old shouting about swans she is very certain you need to see. Sometimes the miracle is not standing up again. Sometimes the miracle is simply finding a reason to keep going and understanding finally that the reason does not have to be enormous to be Enough.

  3. @JJVPYOU [1 likes]: Yes, Texas only has 4% of the land as public, but that still over 1 million acres of public lands. Also, you buy a hunting license and public land hunting endorsement, and you just go and hunt, year after year. No lottery. You hunt as many times as you  want, every year. No hoping you get selected on the lottery. Your hunting license includes a number of tags for deer (both for bucks and/or does.  You get 2 white tail antlerless tags, 3 white tail deer (buck or anterless), 1 mule deer antlerless and 1 mule deer buck or antlerless. Also 4 turkey tags. So every year you can harvest  5 white tail deer, 2 mule deer and 4 turkeys. In addition to the 1 million acres of public lands, you can also apply for drawn hunts, which are a lottery system that opens additional public lands not available otherwise.

Things to do:
1. Start a conversation with conversation sparkers.
2. Finding the sweet spot, the social zone, where people exit the bar. 
3. Angle in our body to the person we are talking to if we don't want our conversation to be interrupted by someone else. 
4. Stand with an eyeline of the host

Things not to do: 
1. Hovering at the boundary of the start zone.
2. Lingering near the bathroom.
3. Floating around the food table.
4. Faking your smile.

events as i could as many as i could afford and i spent lots of money over the years on networking events now what i found when it comes to conversation starters is that the more traditional ones really worked better for me they felt more natural to me these are proven conversation starters that people have been using for many years and there's a reason for that they're non-threatening and they make people feel comfortable you have to remember that when you're at a networking event when you're meeting people for the first time they're going to be wary of you they're going to be wondering who you are what do you want are you a threat to them or should they be friends with you and by using traditional conversation starters these are conversation starters that people know of that they expect to be asked at a networking event it makes them feel comfortable it doesn't intimidate them and it doesn't threaten them whereas if you ask innovative or original questions because you're afraid of sounding boring you can risk intimidating or putting the other person off in some way so let's look at some of the not awkward conversation starters you can use at networking events number one is use traditional these are fail safe conversation starters like what do you do or what line of work are you in people expect these questions at networking events and they work they help the other person relax they help them feel that you are a safe person to talk to now some people don't like to ask these questions they feel that they're boring everyone asks these questions at networking events so they want to ask something that's different that's more original that's innovative this is fine but in my opinion you are there to connect with people you are not there at a you connect with them because it makes them feel safe then in my mind that is a better question to ask at a networking event number two is work related conversation starters so the purpose of these types of conversation starters is to get to know more about that person's job or about their background so some things you could ask here are how long have you been working in xyz industry where did you do your studies what do you like best about your job these types of questions get people talking about their job and their background which is a really good way to get them to start a conversation they will walk away from that conversation from that moment with you with a positive impression of you this is much better than when people try to push their services on you when they try to tell you all about what they do and why you should buy their products or why you should buy their services this is a big deterrent at many networking events and many people who attend are very scared of coming across somebody like that so don't be like that i want you to focus on the other person make them feel special make them feel important by asking them questions about their background number three is event related conversation starters so you are at that event with that person you have that commonality with them why not leverage this and ask them about that event you can ask things like have you answer like yeah i come to this event every month i love it which can lead you to ask a question like oh what do you like so much about this event it's not hard to ask the right questions at a networking event to get the conversation started and they certainly don't have to be the most innovative or original questions that you need to ask don't worry about being boring don't worry about - How to Network: Networking Etiquette Tips for Professionals: Kind: captions Language: en business networking love it or hate it it is critical for your Career Success you need to know how to do it and you need to know how to do it well in this video you're going to learn how to improve your networking skill at networking events so you can come across as somebody who is poised confident and warm these tips will help you build your professional reputation and make valuable Connections in business it's up to you what you do with those connections afterwards if you like the sound of what you're going to learn then hit the like button subscribe to my channel for more content like this and let's get straight into it one of the most basic tips that most people Overlook when it comes to networking and going to networking events is to find out who is going to be there the purpose of this is to go into the networking event prepared and with a strategy so you know exactly who to talk to at networking events you have limited time you have limited time to meet people and to have conversations you don't want to waste your time talking to somebody who can have no impact whatsoever ever on your career so you need to be a lot more strategic about who you talk to at networking events now I know what you're thinking you're probably saying to yourself well that's really shallow Cara you never know who you're going to meet at a networking event you should keep all opportunities open that is true to some extent but from my experience when you go to a networking event you go there with a purpose you go there to meet people who can connect you to a new job if you're a job hunting you go there to meet new clients if you aren't running your own business or if you're in an organization and you need to look for clients you go to those networking events with a purpose in mind if you fill your time the whole time you spend at that networking event talking to this person talking to that person it is very likely you will never achieve the purpose you had of going to that networking event you will walk away from that event not having achieved anything not having met anyone of significance of importance who can help you in your career here I know this through my experience going to many networking events as a business person that you really need to have strategy you need to be prepared you need to find out who is going to be there it doesn't mean you need to spend all of your time talking to people on your list who is going to be there leave some time to open yourself up to New Opportunities if you want but please do go with some type of preparation to at least meet two maybe three people who can help you in your career or help you achieve the goals that you want to achieve now how do you find out who is going to be at the event well a lot of the time the events are posted on Eventbrite on Facebook or even on LinkedIn they are posted on this on those platforms and you can actually see on the event page who is interested in going or who is actually going to be attending those events so take a look at the pages where those events are posted online if that fails you can always ask the event organizer they may or may not tell you who will be attending the event but there's no harm in asking right arriving early gives you a fantastic advantage to see who is entering the room and to start conversations with the people you want to talk to so stand near the door or near the bar both of these areas are great Vantage points to see who is entering in the room and to be able to approach them as soon as they enter another advantage of arriving early is that you are already there when people enter the room now when people enter the room you have to understand that they usually go through a period of feeling a bit nervous a bit stressed a bit uncomfortable because they don't know who to talk to they don't know what the first thing is that they should do when they enter the room you have the advantage of being able to approach them to start a conversation to introduce yourself to help them feel more comfortable and more relaxed at the event which of course will help you give a good first impression and help that person to remember you more if you are at a networking event looking for someone to talk to looking to enter an already established conversation you need to know the right way to do it sometimes groups will be very closed off people will be standing very close together it will be very difficult for you to enter that conversation and start to talk to someone other times groups will be very open and there'll be lots of opportunity for you to enter the group and to enter the conversation as well how can you tell the difference how do you know which is an easy group to approach or a difficult group to approach well you need to look at the body language of the people in the group if they are standing close together if there is not much space at all in between the people in the group then that means they are very closed to anyone else entering the group it's going to be very difficult for you to enter the group and to start talking to anyone in that conversation I recommend you avoid those types of groups on the other hand if there is a group where people have gaps in between them or if they're using very open body language maybe they are not directly facing the other person they're slightly angled away from the other person this is an indication that they are open they're opening up the group for other people to approach them for other people to enter the conversation and start talking when you see groups like this at networking events whether it's one person whether it's three people or more than three people this is your opportunity to approach them and to enter the conversation and meet new people you can learn a lot just by reading someone else's body language if you liked what you have learned so far in this video then please hit the like button subscribe to my channel for more videos like this every week and let's continue on using a business card case is one of my favorite tips to share with emerging leaders when it comes to networking events a business card case will absolutely make make you look polished and professional like no one else does if you're wondering what a business card case is it is a small pouch where you store your own business cards and also the business cards of other people this ensures that both your business cards and the other person's business cards remain neat organized and secure a business card case is something I use religiously for every networking event or a business function that I go to I have been using a business card case ever since I was working in Japan it is a tip that I picked up over there and I absolutely love how it can really make you look a lot more polished a lot more confident and a lot more organized as well which definitely helps you give a positive first impression when you meet people for the first time there are so many different types of business card cases out there it's really important you find one that matches both your personality and also your leadership brand I definitely recommend that you get a business card case but if you want want to start with some options that could be really helpful for you I have linked to some options in the description below I do need to let you know those links are affiliate links so if you do purchase from them I will earn a small commission at no cost to you the next tip for networking events is to avoid eating now I know at networking events there are some amazing food there and it is really tempting to wanna eat all the food that is served to you or presented to you or offered to you at the networking event but you need to go back to the purpose of why you are at the event you are there to meet people you are there to have conversations how can you have a conversation if you have a mouthful of food you won't look polite if you're talking with your mouth full you won't feel comfortable and you won't feel confident either I recommend you eat before the event that way you won't be tempted by all the delicious food that's offered to you and you can focus on your main purpose of going to the event which is to meet people when you start talking to people at a networking event I recommend you start the conversation by asking non-threatening questions what do I mean by non-threatening questions these are questions that are really easy to answer they're not too prying they're not too interrogative in nature you see when you meet someone at a networking event they will be nervous just like you they're likely to be stressed when you start a conversation with them they'll probably be very wary about who you are or what you want what your intentions are they might be worried that you're going to try to sell them something immediately or try to get them to say yes to some kind of request you need to help them relax you need to bring down their defense mechanism you need to get them comfortable talking to you and you do this by asking non-threatening questions have you been to this event before how did you find out about this event and the dreaded question what do you do you I know a lot of you are probably thinking that is so boring it is so typical to be asked that question at a networking event but it is extremely effective in getting the other person to talk to you and getting the other person to feel relaxed around you why because they expect you to ask them this kind of question if you start to ask some questions out of left field questions they don't expect they will start to become cautious they'll put up their defense mechanism they won't be able to relax with you and they probably won't want to continue the conversation with you either how can you meet people who can help you in your career if you're not able to talk to them if you're not able to have an in-depth conversation with them so you need to find ways to bring down their natural defense mechanism get them relaxed to get them to talk to you and you do this by asking non-threatening questions and the handshake you give conveys a lot about who you are as a professional I've had bad handshakes I'm sure you have had bad handshakes at some stage in your career as well they definitely make you notice there are definitely memorable but they don't help you to remember that person in a positive way you don't want people to have this reaction of you you don't want people to think this of you any less of you because you gave a bad handshake I want you to be able to connect with people I want you to be able to get people to like you at networking events this is how you can build a relationship with that person networking events are the first point of contact with someone if they don't like you at their first point of contact then you have no hope of developing a relationship with them so it's really important that you give a positive impression and you give a good handshake or the right handshake to convey the right impression there are many different types of handshakes there is the dead fish handshake where the other person has a slightly clammy hand and it feels a little moist there is the queen handshake where the other person offers you just their fingers instead of their entire hand to shake then there is the limp handshake where they offer you their entire hand but there is no strength to their shake you want to use a strong confident handshake so that is exactly the impression people form about you and here's how you do it you need to make sure you have web to web contact when you shake the other person's hand this is the first step you need to wrap your hand around the other person's hand firmly then you need to shake up and down two to three times then you can let go a handshake is generally the only physical contact you're going to have with someone at a networking event so you really want to make it count we haven't talked at all about body language but body language is extremely important for networking events it's extremely important to help you look more confident and look more poised and professional when you meet people I do have a two-part series on my Channel about body language which I will link to in the description below or you can click on this video right here if you want to watch the first part of this two-part series before you go if you did like this video hit the like button and subscribe to my channel for more content like this every week thank you so much for watching this video and I will see you in the next one

- Event Photography Tips and Tricks (6 Shot Types): Kind: captions Language: en in this video we're going to talk about your different shot types you have at your disposal as an event photographer you have to remember as an event photographer you want to tell a full story and these are the different shots you have at your disposal to do so the first one will be your establishing shot in cinema we're used to establishing shots just telling us where a scene takes place but when it comes to photography it's much more about scale if you're shooting a big corporate event in a massive hall you need to show scale by showing how many people are there having people interacting maybe you're at a conference and there's a lot of different booths and you want to capture all of them to show how many vendors participated and how many people were filling that hall if you're shooting a wedding people want that wide shot of all the different participants or guests at that wedding if it's the ceremony getting a wide shot of everyone not just the bride and groom but all the different people that are there that's really important additionally establishing shots are an anchor to all the images that follow it gives you an idea of where those images are so you're not in some sort of black hole where you have these close-up candids but you have no idea where those are actually shot it also gives you an idea of maybe what those people are interacting off of so let's talk about the next type of shot that without you're establishing shot would feel a little out of place and that would be your close-up candid close-up candidates are something I kind of specialize in if you take a look at my work there are these close images that kind of define an emotional feeling that a participant someone there has their ringing reminders of what it was like to be in that moment for people that were there or people that weren't there it gives them an insight into what people were feeling this is a really important shot it's not so much about anyone else although I do recommend giving some sort of context without taking away the isolation of getting in real close gives you so that could be shooting over someone's shoulder but at a very shallow depth-of-field that way the person you're shooting over is blurred out but you know that person that you're your subject you know they're reacting off of something it could be a funny joke it could be very serious it doesn't matter but you know they're reacting off of a different person I like to shoot the shots by shooting through things pretty often it gives a more candid feel it's already a candid but when you're able to frame your shot you're not only able to draw the eye in toward that subject if you're using two different things to frame or even sometimes I'll shoot through a wall so I used part of a wall in my shot to draw the eye into the subject I'm really trying to get that emotional moment from I tend to shoot these shots with telephotos it could be a 135 millimeter it could be a 70 to 200 or even an 85 millimeter I often use that 85 millimeter if I want to get even more shallow than what my 70 to 200 can do for me and I don't mind getting in close it could be really exciting as well getting in close and feeling unnoticed if you can be unnoticed at closer focal lengths and you're a good photographer so I really enjoy shooting that gives me a more shallow depth of field which helps me further isolate my subject the next type of shot would be your candid interactions this is typically about more than one person now you might have one person that is the focus but you're just giving a little bit more information about what they're reacting off of sometimes you have more than one subject matter maybe they're both equally important and so you need to shoot at appropriate apertures to get the proper depth of field remember if you're at 2.8 unless those people are perfectly lined up odds are you're not going to get both InFocus it really depends so another thing I want to say don't always shoot wide open guys shooting wide open is appropriate for isolating a subject but when you have more than one subject and they're not really one doesn't take priority over the other having one out of focus is doing you a disservice these shots are actually a bit more challenging than your kind of isolated candid emotional shot because you're having to shoot at wider apertures which takes more skill it's really easy to just zoom in close blur everything out to oblivion and then get these emotional highs but getting more than one person in focus and still having a pleasing composition can be a little bit more challenging what that means is you're gonna have to mind your rules of composition a bit more you are especially gonna have a challenge of balancing everything out if more things are in focus they're gonna pull your eye and so the idea is that if you have something here pulling your eyes attention too hard this is gonna be an empty space it won't work so you kind of find ways to balance the image the next type of shot to talk about would be posed shots I'm not talking about portraits I'm talking about where you actually approach people though and ask for a photograph this could be in front of a step-and-repeat but right now we're gonna talk about posed images when you see a small group of people and you approach them and asked to take their photograph it's not my favorite part of the job it's a piece of cake though and you shouldn't be intimidated to approach people these are shots you're often gonna get at a cocktail hour and that kind of thing when people are just mingling so what I like to do I go right up to them a small group of people maybe two or three or four I say hey do you mind if I get a shot of you guys and pretty much always they're gonna say absolutely now I really recommend also taking advantage of approaching them and asking for that shot because it's a good opportunity to get some candid interactions as well and the way to do this is simply ask them to look at each other everyone laughs and it's so goofy when you're standing next to someone for a post shot and then the photographer says hey look at each other you say oh can I get one of you guys looking at each other they always bust out laughing or smile it's fun it's easy and then if you're missing your interactions if you haven't really refined your timing yet you're able to sneak that type of work into your body of your collection of images another type of shot would be your portrait shot now these are shots that are actually a little more artistic let's call it I love to shoot these nowadays for myself I often bring a film camera to every job I go to and if there's someone notable I'll ask them just real quick hey Can I grab a shot of you this last month or two I've done this with Nancy Pelosi Adam Schiff and Jamie Lee Curtis and it just it's fun it changes it up for me because when I'm shooting an event I'm typically not working with anyone very extensively I'm just documenting but this is a good opportunity for you to build a portfolio enjoy enjoy it you never know Right. And I just think a lot of that has kind of sucked the joy out of hunting as I knew it when I was growing up. GUYS, REPLACE THOSE CHEAP STRAPS WITH ROPE OR CABLE AND CONNECTORS STRONG ENOUGH TO SUPPORT YOUR WEIGHT PLUS YOUR EQUIPMENT... AND NEVER DISCONNECT FROM THE TREE TIL YOU ARE AT THE GROUND.

- Cybersecurity Threat Hunting Explained: Kind: captions Language: en-US Boom. You've just been attacked. The bad guys in your system and you're hemorrhaging data. Now, this was not an isolated incident. There was a time that led up to this, and there was a time that happened after. The time that led up to it, going back and preceding this, we call left of boom. And the time after it when we trying to discover, that's the time that we call right of boom. So in this first phase, we're basically doing the bad guy is doing reconnaissance. He's looking at your systems. He's probing them. He's trying to figure out where the weak spots are, where the good data is, and the time after boom is when we want to be doing recovery. The problem is we don't always know when boom occurred at the time it occurred. There's a lag between boom and alarm. This is when we find out. When the attack has occurred, which is not the same as when it actually occurred. Now, if we look at these kinds of of intervals, what could we do about this? Well, there's a time here you can see that is the mean time to identify what the problem is, according to Ponemon Institute's survey in 2022. The mean time to identify is on the order of 200 days. That's how long it takes to figure out that the bad guy is already in your system, and further still, there's this notion of meantime to contain, and the meantime to contain is on the order of 70 days. You put those two together, you're at about 270 days total between when boom occurred and when now, we finally got everything recovered. There clearly would be some advantage if we could go back in time and discover the boom and bring the interval between the alarm and the boom closer together, or even go back before boom and start to realize during the reconnaissance phase that something is amiss. Something is happening in this phase, and this timeline in this area is when we could be doing what we call threat hunting. I could do a hunt and we'll talk about what that involves after the hunt is when we start doing the investigation. And the investigation is reactive. The hunt is proactive. That's the big difference here. So as much as possible, it would be nice if we could go back and anticipate some of these things. Now, how does this work? Well, it turns out we're going to take if we're doing threat hunting, we're basically going to develop a hypothesis. You have someone that's acting as an investigator, but they're investigating things that maybe have not fully happened yet or in the process of happening. So no alarms have gone off. What are we going to use? What we're going to use things like indicators of compromise. That's information that we can gather from our systems that tell us that someone has done something here that has breached. Maybe it's a small hole here, a small hole there. There are other things like indicators of attack that may be in here as well. It says someone is knocking on the door. They might not have fully gotten in, but they did some things that we should be concerned about. Other sources of information would be security, intelligence, threat feeds. So intel feeds that tell us that there are certain types of vulnerabilities that are being exploited on the Internet these days and certain things that are happening maybe in a particular sector or in a particular geography. This is security intelligence. We like to use that kind of information and leverage it to our best ability as well. Then we could do some vulnerability scans on our environment and that would tell us what the bad guy is also seeing. And that is that there are vulnerabilities here. There are systems that are weak here. This is the soft underbelly. This is what the bad guys doing. Why wouldn't we do the same thing? Then an intelligent threat hunter who has some experience and some insight into this is going to use basically their experience and a little bit of intuition to connect the dots and say these things that seemed isolated, in fact, are not. They're all grouped together. We connect the dots and we realize before the boom has occurred that in fact, this is the setup that's about to result in a data breach. Or it could be in this range of time when some things maybe we've already been attacked. But the alarm hasn't gone off. But we're going to see the telltale signs, and those are the things that we're looking for. Now, what would an experienced threat hunter use in terms of the tools to gather all of this information? Well, they would use some tools like an XDR, extended detection and response capability. And I've talked about this in a previous video. Another thing that we'd use is a security information, an event management system, something that goes out and gathers information from all of my sources of security telemetry, all of the logs, all of the flow data, all of the different sources that might give me security intelligence in these two, in fact, can be related and feeding to each other. And then another related technology that often relates to these as well is a user behavior analytics capability. And the UBA would look for anomalous user activities. Here's a user that's performing differently than what their peer group is doing, and therefore they're drawing suspicion. And then we would ultimately want to take all of those technologies and infuse them with artificial intelligence so that we can get to the point, find the source, get to our investigation faster, and do the reporting, and hopefully uncover this issue before the boom, and if not at least very soon after the boom, because what we're trying to do is avoid two numbers. There's one number, 270 and another number 4 million. What do those mean? 270 is roughly the number of days between boom and containment, according to the Ponemon Institute. Also, according to that same survey, 4 million is the average cost of a data breach. That's what we're trying to avoid.

sound of the shot they instinctively drop their body to load up their back legs to quickly get out of the area and by doing so the deer change is where its vitals are at especially if you were aiming Center Mass on the deer it could result in a miss or even worse a high non-lethal wounding shot now inside of about 17 18 yards typically the deer can't drop far enough to make much of a difference early years of bow hunting it was behind the shoulder and halfway up however with more experience of shooting gear over the years I found that the spot that I want to hit rather instead of shooting behind the shoulder is straight up the leg one third for the way up and hitting a deer here will not only go through both lungs but it will hit the top of the heart and also possibly sever a major general rule of thumb now padir is beyond 20 yards I'm aiming for the heart low heart that way if the deer doesn't drop still going to hit it in the heart and if it does react that gives me the largest margin of error for the deer to duck into that shot and still end up with that Arrow going through the deer's vitals here's a perfect example of what I'm talking about this buck that I shot in
