this using it pretty much every deer hunting video and this is a tip that I wish I had known sooner because previous to finding out about milkweed we'd use either whatever the wind forecast was on the Weather Channel and just hunt in locations based off that and we'd use the puffer bottle and obviously those two things can have several flaws one with the weather channel it's not very specific they'll just give you a direction and a lot of times they're raw so you go out to a spot that and hunting it for a West Wind and you might get out there and it's an East Wind or a Northeast wind or something so we'll still use that for a reference but then once we get out there that's when we pull our milkweed out and then hunt based off what this is telling us other thing we used to use is a puffer bottle and I'm sure many of you have had experiences with those it's just got white powder in there and you flip it up and it'll just kind of drift off but then it'll disappear within a couple feet and you don't really get a good gauge on what that's doing other than the it went in that spot so what we've gone to now is milkweed and you flip this stuff out take a little bit out there and you just flip it up in the air and you can see a little bit better as to what the wind's doing and right now it's going straight up it's actually going straight behind Jake which is Nothing's Gonna smell you up there no nothing's gonna smell it and that's going to give you a lot better idea of where deer is going to be able to smell you a lot of times if you're hunting in hilly terrain the wind's going to be doing some weird stuff might be swirling and all that jazz and with milkweed you can see that you can see it as it's going down a ridge or going through a little Valley or something and see exactly where the wind is going so that way you have a better idea of where deer are going to be able to smell you another big advantage to that is if you can see exactly where that wind is going or wherever The Thermals are taking your scent you'll be able to know right where a deer is going to be able to smell you and if one comes in that you want to shoot you'll know where you got to stop them and shoot him before he gets to that wind so milkweed is a really great tool for all that and I think it helps you to actually understand how thermals work when you get out there took me a long time to actually figure out how thermals worked when the sun warms up the ground The Thermals are going to be rising and when the ground cools down thermals are going to be dropping down the hill it'll help speed up that learning process and then you in turn you'll be able to predict that a little bit better and know before you go into a spot what the wind's going to be doing and what The Thermals are going to be doing so and sometimes use it to your advantage to get closer to Deer than what you might think yeah exactly because like you just saw when I threw that piece of milkweed up it went straight up and behind Jake and the camera puffer bottle is not going to really show you that it's probably just gonna sit and fall straight down where when you have milkweed if you can see all those little movements that your scent is doing it'll help you know exactly what's going on out there and help you get a lot closer to deer in the future I over hang on just relax a minute sit please stay strong a couple decades ago the best you could do was leave a map with your loved ones and tell them when to deploy the search party if they hadn't heard from you now we have these simple fairly affordable devices that let you communicate with people no matter how many miles outside of cell range you are they also have the handy little sos feature that will put you directly in touch with search and rescue so yes generations of hunters before us just went out into the woods and when they came back they came back that was all the good people at home had to go on but there's no reason to put your family through torture while you're out there so if you're gonna solo hunts have some sort of satellite communication device i honestly believe it's on the necessity list if you're gonna head out there alone there's just no reason not to have it all right logistics aside let's talk about some of the deeper issues at play here there are worse and statistically much more probable things that could go wrong in your life than a mishap on a solo hunt i'm not going to presume to speak for every solo hunter out there but i can say with absolute confidence that solo hunting is something that keeps me sane i'm an introvert i'm not ashamed of that and i need a few extended periods of solitude over the course of a year to keep myself like leveled out i can feel it when i've been just constantly surrounded by people for months on end and i just need a couple days to be in the utter and marvelous quiet of the wilderness i've found no place more mentally refreshing than a lonely glassing point miles from anyone my wife and i are in total agreement that the slight risk of a wilderness accident is well worth it compared to the bigger risk of breaking my brain i feel like i'm taking crazy pills on a more meta scale let me make a point specifically aimed at the men out there if you've ever felt that like this is it kind of feeling you know you grew up and kind of got your life together you have a family a decent job like like you're getting a taste of the american dream and somehow it feels kind of hollow or like you thought it would scratch that restless itch killer band name by the way i believe one of the huge things creating that angst is this desire to get out and do something wild not crazy not irresponsible not buying a miata and running off with your 20 year old hairdresser but just something that involves some risk and adventure there's this primal man still buried underneath your polo shirt and americano that has nothing to do and wants to get out and play for me hunting has been a huge answer to solo hunting further increases that sense of adventure because there's there's just something primal about matching wits with the wilderness all on your own it's just you and nature don't screw it up i love that it restores my soul and at least for me it's worth the risk okay i i could preach about the inner benefits of solo hunting forever but one more practical thing before we wrap this up you have to be smart out there alright hunting solo is a different beast than hunting with your buddies you have to evaluate every potential decision through the lens of i am alone out here that's not to say you don't get after it you don't take some calculated risks to maximize your hunt but there should probably be at least one moment in every solo hunt where you stare at a steep ridge or an avalanche shoot and think you know if i was out here with somebody i'd go that way but i'm gonna pass right now because it's just too risky alone all i'm saying is you need to add just a bit of extra caution when you decide to go alone and let that modify how you might normally hunt just because the stakes are now higher so if you've been dreaming about solo hunting for a while absolutely get out there and try it take precautions get yourself an inreach or something like that hunt smarts but also just enjoy every moment of it if you've never been before prepare for the fact that it will get a little creepy and unnerving at night it doesn't matter how many nights you've slept in the dirt with people the first time you do it all alone messes with your head a little bit but power through that i'm a big fan of some sort of sleep supplement just to help you relax a bit more and get a decent night's sleep when every single noise sounds like a mountain lion coming to snack on your lonely self but aft after enough times you you get used to it it really becomes no big deal all right hopefully some part of this helped somebody if nothing else maybe it armed you with a more cohesive argument for your loved ones who think you're crazy for wanting to do this and if you have any other advice or tips for successful solo hunting drop them in the comments below let's get a conversation going thanks so much for watching if you haven't already please do me a favor subscribe right down here and if you want to see a solo hunt in action check this one out and if you want to hear the honest truth about my first night ever solo in the backcountry that embarrassing story is right over there thanks again now just go have some sort of awesome solo adventure

is to tell the story tell the story of your hunt film what's important to you what do you want to remember about your hunt what beyond just the kill is memorable to you what's meaningful to you things couple other things to think about as far as propelling your story forward is maybe at the beginning of the hunt or the beginning of the day film and intro talk about what the plans are for the day what you're hoping to accomplish and then at the end of the day or just at the end of the trip film some sort of conclusion and outro that you could put at the end of your video to kind of solidify things and bring everything back together or maybe just at the end of the hunt grab your hunting partner and film them ask them some questions about the hunt and hear their recollection of the story bring in a lot of unnecessary equipment you want to keep it light but there's a few things you can do without even needing any other sort of equipment hold the camera nice and tight to your body try to have multiple contact points with it it's gonna be less shaky than if you're just free handing it and there's micro jitter every step you take if you're trying to get a really smooth shot just bend your knees and hold your camera like it's a hot cup of coffee that you're trying not to spill a couple other options for keeping your footage would be using something like a monopod which is just a single pole that you can attach your camera to so if you stop you can just set it down and then that's gonna keep your footage super steady with also being still pretty lightweight and just a small footprint to carry around so get him on a pod or a tripod of any sort and that's gonna just help you keep things steadier when you're filming alright next tip is to keep your camera readily accessible interesting because what's gonna happen is you're gonna put it in your pack and then you're not gonna want to take it out or an Elk's gonna run by and you're gonna miss the shot because you're messing with it and carry that around your shoulder a better option is to use a strapping system that attaches directly to your backpack shoulder strap and then you can quickly release your camera within seconds and get it ready to shoot alright last tip for this video is shoot when you don't feel like it there's tons of times when you're gonna be tired and maybe the weather's miserable or whatever and you're not gonna feel like shooting but that's often times the best time to shoot maybe you're super super tired maybe it's a grueling pack out and you don't feel like lifting that camera up and filming cuz you're so exhausted you're mentally and physically exhausted it's gonna be fun to look at afterwards and remember how miserable it was to pack that animal out or maybe it's just the weather is so bad and you don't want to take your camera out but those are great times to consider film those ups and downs of the hunt well there's a lot of technical tips we could cover different things like that the biggest thing as far as storytelling and capturing what you want is it's up to you these are your videos for you to remember and for you to share with your friends and family so just get out there get a camera that fits your needs and start filming and learn as you go [Music]

- Why Gon's Fall Into Darkness Is So Controversial: Kind: captions Language: en Gon What makes Gon such a controversial protagonist? On the surface Hunter x Hunter, Yoshihiro Togashi’s long-running magnum opus is a standard Shounen story. Each story arc focuses on a well worn Shounen trope such as a training sequence or a tournament arc, things that Togashi has covered before in&nbsp;his series YuYuHakusho. But where&nbsp;Hunter x Hunter&nbsp;fundamentally differs from the status quo is in the way that it takes these formulas and breaks them apart in interesting ways before returning to its Shounen roots. In a 2018 interview with Weekly Shounen Jump Magazine Togashi revealed that with Gon he originally planned to create a sort of good-boy type of character that would, and I quote, win every ‘character I would want for a son’ competition. But as soon as he started writing his story, he immediately realised that this sort of character would not fit the story he had envisioned at all. After all, what good boy would lightheartedly and without so much as a second thought abandon his foster mother to become a hunter and search for the father who left him behind as a child just to follow his own selfish path? And so it was at that point back in 1998 when the series published its very first chapter, that he decided to try something a little bit different. He came to the conclusion that the traditional tropes of Shounen Manga would not work for the world he was about to create, and so he decided to twist the general perspective on the genre in a number of unimposing, but nonetheless highly impactful ways. As a result, Gon is not your classic all-good-all-powerful Shounen hero, but rather a realistic version of it, that has to face real consequences for his actions within the story world Togashi has thrown him in. Thus, he has become one of the most controversially discussed Shounen protagonists in history, and in this video I want to explore with you why I believe that it makes Gon one of the most well-written Shounen heroes of all time. //The theme of Hunter x Hunter Before we can dissect, how Togashi has designed Gon as a character and what makes his take on the trope of the Shounen protagonist so unique, we first have to understand the general theme of Hunter x Hunter as a series overall. You might have heard before that the main theme of Hunter x Hunter is morality and exploring the thin lines between good and evil, black and white. It’s undeniably true that morality is a central part of the story’s premise. However, I believe that the actual theme of the story goes even further than this. Because the theme, otherwise also known as the moral argument of a story, contrary to common believe, doesn’t refer to a general subject matter such as love, class, power or good versus evil. It much rather is the author’s view on how to act in the world and therefore encompasses a certain moral vision, that the author wants to explore through his story. Therefore, by definition I believe that morality as a concept cannot be the overall theme of Hunter x Hunter but much rather is a part of it. The actual moral argument that Togashi is exploring in the story, and that he also uses to explore Shounen as a genre overall is - ‘What does it mean to be human in a super-human world?’. And this overall theme then includes other important questions, such as ‘what does it mean to hold power?’ and ultimately also, ‘what does it mean to be moral?’. If you look closely enough, each character in the story is a variation of that theme and that’s exactly what I want to explore with you in this series of videos, and I hope you enjoy watching them, as much as I enjoyed making them. //Gon & the split hero So, why start with Gon? Well, Gon is of course the protagonist of the story. However, Hunter x Hunter takes a slightly different conceptual approach to their character web than other typical Shounen do. No matter if it’s Naruto, Bleach or One Piece, amidst a usually very well-crafted group of supporting main characters, the hero always stands out clearly as the central focus point of the story. Therefore, Naruto is about Naruto himself becoming Hokage. Bleach is about Ichigo following his destiny as a Shinigami and exploring the supernatural world. And One Piece is of course about Luffy striving to find the One Piece and becoming king of the pirates. But what about Hunter x Hunter? While Gon does have a central desire within his ambition to find his father Ging, that is driving the story forward, it is neither particularly intense nor is it very urgent. The story has no overarching big evil to defeat, no important object to be acquired and no final destination to be reached. Rather than following a straight line towards a major goal, Gon and his friends follow a meandering path without apparent direction. They cover a great deal of territory in a haphazard way and encounter a variety of characters from different levels of society. At the same time and in close relation to this, in Hunter x Hunter the lines between protagonist and ally become very very thin. So thin even, that it’s fair to say that the story has not one, but two protagonists - Gon and Killua, with Gon being only a tad more important. This trope of the buddy story, is quite common in Hollywood movies, from stories like the lord of the rings over 21 Jump street all the way to pretty much any love story out there being a typical example. But it is very very rare in Shounen Anime and Manga. In a buddy story, in essence, the hero is split into 2 parts, with one being a bit more relevant than the other and both main characters having two different backgrounds, 2 different approaches to life and 2 different sets of values and talents. As a result, the whole becomes greater than the sum of its parts and is what makes the combination Gon and Killua such a strong leading duo. These two protagonists are allies throughout the course of the series, but at the same time also have an overarching conflict of values and actions, that gives character development to both of them. So, as you can see Gon is already fundamentally different from other typical Shounen protagonists, simply based on his role in the story. His desire to find his father is not particularly intense and he shares the role of the hero with his buddy Killua, who has his very own set of desires and weaknesses, and we will get further into their relationship in the next video. Now this insight, of the split hero, can be boiled down even further to one of Hunter x Hunter’s major strengths as a story: While many writers believe that all you need is a well-written hero that is the only person that matters, Hunter x Hunter doesn’t live from any individual character, but rather from an intricately crafted WEB of characters that interact with each other. As a result, the main group of protagonists move the plot along, but the don’t carry it all by themselves. And thus, every single character that Togashi introduces helps to define those around them. So now that we have looked at the general premise for Gon’s role in the story and how it differs from conventional Shounen heroes, let’s finally talk about his character arc. //Gon Freecss: A dark coming-of-age story At the beginning of the story, Gon is the stereotypical shounen poster boy — young, plucky, irrepressible, spiky hair — in the first 20 or so episodes, he’s basically Son Goku without the tail. He is seemingly nothing but good natured, and depending on your preferences as viewer his simple and pure attitude was probably either incredibly charming or almost intolerable. His main desire is to find his father Ging and prove himself in the world of hunters, both of which he strongly idealises. Equipped with immense talent, power and skill, he leaves the isolated island he was raised on to take the hunter exam and follow his dreams. Togashi makes us believe that Gon, just as so many other popular Shounen characters, will grow to become a warrior of justice, fighting for what is right. He gives us the impression that in this story it will be easy to distinguish who are the good guys and who are the baddies wearing capes, just as we have been trained to expect from so many other stories from that generation. However, it quickly becomes clear that the world of Hunter x Hunter is quite cruel and unforgiving. Actions and mistakes have real consequences here and Togashi basically used Gon as a sort of literary guinea pig to see what would happen to your typical Shounen Hero, if you were to throw them into a realistic world. And so slowly but steadily, as we pass from arc to arc and get to see more and more of the world and Gon’s interactions with it, it becomes clearer and clearer that what now separates Gon from most other Shounen protagonists is his increasingly skewed moral compass. The fundamental lie that Gon believes about the world is that everything is either black or white and that there is a clear line between good and evil. But since that is not how the real world works, where morality is mostly found on a spectrum of grey, Gon increasingly struggles to define what is good and what is bad, based on the childish naivety an 11 year old simply has. Since objectively judging people’s complex actions and behaviour makes it difficult to put them into one category or the other, Gon bases his morality on himself and his relationship with a person. People he is familiar with, such as Killua, Leorio or Kurapika are obviously good and are worthy of his protection, while unfamiliar people dying during the hunter exam are generally ignored and not considered as important. Meanwhile, people Gon is unfamiliar with, but that conduct acts of violence and cruelty must obviously be bad. This can strongly be seen when Gon gets captured by the phantom troupe as he despises them for taking innocent lives unrelated to them. At the same time Gon also categories people based on whether or not they are useful to him or his friends. Next to his naivety, this selfishness is Gon’s second major weakness as a character. As a result his morality appears to become increasingly skewed, where an objectively evil and twisted character like Binolt, who has massacred and eaten countless people, is actually thanked by Gon simply for helping him getfing stronger. To better understand this development in Gon and how his story ultimately reached the dark climax it did, I think the best thing is to look at the central relationship between hero and opponent. //Gon & Hisoka I think it is fair to say that Gon’s relationship with Hisoka is to Hunter x Hunter what unnecessarily dramatic music is to this channel. Without it, it just wouldn’t feel the same. So, when we first get introduced to Hisoka, Togashi promises us the typical Shounen good-guy-takes-on-bad-guy relationship between him and Gon. Hisoka is a psychopathic Clown and the apex of hedonism, who cuts off another contesters arms simply for bumping into him right at the start of the hunter exam. He will stop at nothing to get what he wants, no matter the damage it does to himself or others. As a result, at the beginning of their relationship Gon and Hisoka appear to be clear-cut opposites, right down to Hisoka’s appearance as a scary, blood-thirsty clown — which as we know is the mortal enemy of kids everywhere. Nonetheless, despite all their seeming differences, over the course of the story, both characters find themselves inextricably drawn to each other. What started off as our typical good-hero vs evil-opponent dynamic, slowly blurs into an uncomfortable similarity between the two. Their shared desire to better themselves through combat and their ecstatic enjoyment of battle is nothing unique to&nbsp;Hunter x hunter, however most Shounen tend to shy away from so blatantly highlighting this objectively problematic stance of their main characters. Togashi on the other hand, makes it easy to imagine a small misstep in Gon’s life pushing him into a position similar to Hisoka’s. And we can see this in the story in a number of ways. The best example however might be Hisoka’s fight in the Heaven’s Arena arc against the over-confident Kastro. Simply to prove his superiority and provide a show, Hisoka sacrifices both his arms to gain the upper hand (no pun intended), and to satisfy his desire to dominate and humiliate his opponent. Then, a little later during the Greed Island arc, Gon mirrors this morbid sacrifice against the bomber Genthru. He abandons a carefully thought out plan by taking an explosive attack from Genthru which results in him losing an arm, basically for no other reason but his desire to prove his superiority and humiliate his opponent. In a weird way, Gon and Hisoka become more and more similar throughout the story to the point where Hisoka almost functions as a sort of mentor for Gon, a dynamic that we will explore in more detail in another video. Now however, we can finally look at what Gon’s character arc looks like over all. //Gon’s Character Arc To summarise what we have discussed so far, Gon’s character arc shows us what happens to a naive 11 year old boy that holds enormous physical power, when he is increasingly confronted with the harsh & cruel reality of the real world. This is basically the coming-of-age story line we know from other Shounen, such as Naruto, only that Gon doesn’t have a positive arc, but a negative one. Instead of growing more emotionally and morally mature, his character is slowly crushed by the brutal reality of the Hunter x hunter world. This twist on the always-rising shounen protagonist, had not been explored in this much detail before and has lead to the very mature vibe hunter x hunter carries as a series. The premise for Gon’s development throughout the story, as we have already discussed, are his naivety and his selfishness. Both of these things don’t necessarily make him a bad person, but simply an inexperienced and overwhelmed child. Nonetheless, based on these weaknesses, Gon’s character is set up to learn about the complex moral fabric of society and form more a realistic view of the world. This need is also moral, since through his basic moral compass and his naivety, combined with his enormous power he starts to hurt people around him. However, it is not until the chimera ant arc, that Gon completely spirals into a complete loss of his morality and ultimately his humanity. There are two events in particular towards the beginning of the arc, that are representative for this change. The first is Gon’s first ever kill in the story. A low-ranked Armadillo chimaera ant that bluntly attacks him and that Gon then slowly crushes to death. While it is understandable that Gon defends himself and maybe even has to kill the chimera ant, the complete lack of reaction and emotional response to this act is what is truly shocking here. The pure Gon we got to know at the beginning of the story would never had been able to do such a thing, at least without being deeply affected by it. Yet, Gon has already been so consumed by power and violence that not only is he unshaken by his first kill, but doesn’t even give it a second thought. And even more impactful, is the death of his mentor Kite, who is killed by the royal guard Pitou. This first-ever loss of a loved person affects Gon deeply, way more than is common for typical Shounen characters. Much rather Gon has a very realistic and human response to this event and develops a rage and thirst for revenge against Pitou, that is honestly quite unsettling to watch. As a result, his final act in the arc is one of the most notorious events in shonen manga. He uses the heavily injured and innocent Komugi as mere a tool to blackmail Pitou, giving no second thought about the overall situation and threatening to kill her. Once he learns that Kite is beyond help, in order to satisfy his wrath against Pitou, he calls upon all of the potential energy he will ever have for a short bout of combat where he brutally kills his opponent without a single shred of remorse. In doing so, he trades the rest of his life for one short moment of violence, as the horrified Killua is unable to do anything but watch. It’s some of the most harrowing reading or watching in the genre, a perverse version of Goku’s Spirit Bomb. This is Gon’s moment of moral choice, but instead of having a self-revelation about what it means to be human and how to behave justly and morally towards others, he gives in to his worst instincts. As Killua abandons his merciless past by forging a relationship with Gon, his friend sacrifices all of his remaining humanity for the ultimate act of revenge — and becoming a monster in the process. The self-revelation meant for Gon in this moment of extreme moral and human complexity, is so obvious that we the readers have it instead and it makes the scene just the more dark and uncomfortable to watch. The reason, why this dark twist on the typical Shounen protagonist arc works so well with Gon, is because Togashi has followed one of the most important rules of writing when creating his character: Creating empathy - but not sympathy. Sympathy means that we approve of the heroes actions and completely identify with him as a person. If that were to be true however, we could never have morally challenging and interesting main characters and every single story would only serve a very limited and specific audience, that shares the exact same views and values as the hero. Empathy on the other hand means that while we don’t necessarily approve of the heroes actions, we do however understand them. We understand why the hero would make this decision and how he ultimately reached that moral conclusion. And as you might suspect, foreshadowing plays a crucial role in this. It makes the story cohesive and engaging. So when we apply this to Gon, while his brutal murder of Pitou and his failure to keep his humanity are devastating to watch, we do fully understand why Gon is behaving this way and thus feel like we are watching the natural climax to his character arc. His entire journey has slowly built towards this dramatic moment and we could unconsciously witness Gon’s creeping moral decline, without Togashi having to throw it into our faces. This is truly great writing. It is a great endpoint to the story, where Gon ultimately meets his father and is judged worthy of traveling with him. He has fulfilled his desire and going back to the traditional tropes of shounen, Togashi establishes a new equilibrium. And as the author stated himself, at this point in Hunter x Hunter - where Gon meets Ging - he has pretty much already completed the story once. Now, you may rightfully say: Doesn’t hunter x hunter keep going afterwards? And of course you’re right. However, as you might know if you follow the manga, Gon is not currently part of the story and should he ever appear in it again in the form of a leading character, he will need a completely fresh arc with a new desire and now possibly a positive character arc, where he re-evaluates his past actions and ultimately regains his humanity, ending up better and wiser as at the beginning of the story. The one who enabled Gon’s second chance and survival meanwhile was his best friend Killua, who started off as the coldblooded and inhuman assassin at the beginning of the story, who has now completely changed places with Gon. In many ways Killua is written just as fantastically as Gon and we will be looking at his character and role in the story in the next video of this series. So, stay tuned for that. Thanks, guys. Peace.

  13. @Ik0As: 3:26 I'm 5 years late for this video but I wanna add my grain of salt into the mix. I don't think the theme is "What does it mean being human in a superhuman world?". This is going to be a long one, so please bear with me.

Spoilers Ahead, but if you are reading this considering the subject of the video is under, I'm gonna guess you already watched Hunter X Hunter.

Before I watched Hunter X Hunter, I always put it on the back of my mind next to Dragon Ball Z. I've tried many times to watch DBZ but I was always ground up and spit out by the glaciar pace and the general "I'm naive and good and the story will never address that" vibe of Goku and when I saw Gon I thought "That's child Goku". I was never interested in watching HxH but was always curious why was it so highly rated on My Anime List. One day I stumbled upon the first minutes of Gon and Hisoka fight on Heaven's Arena on YouTube and I was blown away by the amount of detail and how you could tell there was a strategy and train of thought going on inside the characters' mind, all conveyed without words, after that I decided I had to watch HxH.

I started HxH and was instantly disappointed. I found the opening and the whole first episode really generic, but with Gon's and Hisoka's fight on my mind I decided I was going to keep watching until I got to see it. Before I knew it, I was crying at the death of Meruem and Komugi.

I loved HxH. I recommended it to everyone I could, but even with all the enthusiasm I had I didn't just watched it again back to back of my first time. I must have been a couple of years, my mind still digesting HxH story, that, somehow, I convinced my parents and my siblings to watch HxH all together. All this time I was still confused about why it was called "Hunter X Hunter", but it was on the middle of my second watch that I had the following thought "All Hunters are super selfish when it comes to what they want" and then it clicked.

Here is what I think HxH's theme is, "What does it mean to do what you want and how do we find what we want?". To me this is the idea around everything is built around and under this lens Gon could be said to be the perfect vehicle to explore it.

- 50 Acres: Better Hunting Than the Neighbors (610): Kind: captions Language: en-US >>DANIEL: We often receive questions from folks&nbsp; asking how to improve the habitat and hunting&nbsp;&nbsp; on small properties - those 10, 20, 30-acre&nbsp; sized properties - and I gotta tell you,&nbsp;&nbsp; if those small properties are managed and hunted&nbsp; appropriately, they can provide some great hunting&nbsp;&nbsp; opportunities. >>DANIEL: A great example of success year after year on a&nbsp; small property has been Pro Staffer Jeff Therrell.&nbsp;&nbsp; He’s tagged a bunch of venison and some great&nbsp; bucks on the nine acres behind his house.&nbsp; >>JEFF: Whew. ..DANIEL: The key to Jeff’s success&nbsp; on his nine acres in Kansas,&nbsp;&nbsp; well, it’s been planning a quality food plot and&nbsp; providing deer with a food source, especially&nbsp;&nbsp; during the hunting season. Once the production ag&nbsp; in the area has been harvested, Jeff’s property&nbsp;&nbsp; has the only attractive food, and the deer come&nbsp; in, and he’s experienced some great hunts. >>DANIEL: If a property of any&nbsp; size can provide a limited resource&nbsp;&nbsp; better than neighboring properties, that&nbsp; property becomes very attractive and when&nbsp;&nbsp; seeking that resource - food, cover, water, or&nbsp; even security - deer will want to spend more time&nbsp;&nbsp; on that property than on the neighbors.&nbsp;&nbsp; >>DANIEL: Clay, Micajah, and I recently traveled about&nbsp; 30 minutes northwest of The Proving Grounds&nbsp;&nbsp; and met with Cody to tour his 50 acres. >>DANIEL: When looking at the onX map of Cody’s property and the neighborhood, you can see&nbsp;that Cody is surrounded by mostly hardwood timber,&nbsp;&nbsp; which have most likely been high graded, which&nbsp; is typical in this area, and cattle pasture.&nbsp; >>DANIEL: Imagine that Cody’s property is in the&nbsp; center of a Rubik’s Cube, and each square is one&nbsp;&nbsp; square mile. This is a great illustration to show&nbsp; that for several miles around Cody’s property&nbsp;&nbsp; there’s not great habitat. Food and cover are very&nbsp; limited. Currently, Cody’s property doesn’t look&nbsp;&nbsp; much more attractive than the neighborhood. >>DANIEL: We want Cody to see more critters and harvest more venison and making his property the&nbsp;most attractive 50 acres within this Rubik’s Cube&nbsp;&nbsp; is gonna be key. >>DANIEL: Cody was off to a great start, and he had already&nbsp; begun felling cedars on one of the mountains. >>DANIEL: In the past, we’ve shared updates from&nbsp; similar projects. One was Tom Free’s property. >>DANIEL: When we visited Tom’s place, there was&nbsp; an area that was invaded by eastern red cedars.&nbsp;&nbsp; We recommended he fell those, let ‘em dry, and&nbsp; then use prescribed fire. After that fire moved&nbsp;&nbsp; through and those skeletons were cleaned up, I&nbsp; gotta tell you, once that sunlight hit the ground,&nbsp;&nbsp; there was a flush of native grasses and forbs. >>DANIEL: This type of habitat is extremely productive for wildlife. >>DANIEL: Cody would likely see similar results in this area. Once he used prescribed&nbsp; fire, those native grasses and forbs would add not&nbsp;&nbsp; only cover but native browse to his property. >> ANNOUNCER: GrowingDeer is brought to you by Bass Pro Shops and Cabela’s. Also by Reconyx,&nbsp;Winchester, Avian-X Decoys, LaCrosse Footwear, Thlete Outdoor Apparel, Morrell Targets, Hooyman, Hook’s Custom Calls, Summit Treestands,&nbsp;&nbsp; RTP Outdoors, Yamaha, Fourth Arrow, onX Hunt,&nbsp; Scorpion Venom Archery, Case IH Tractors,&nbsp;&nbsp; Burris Optics, Bloodsport Arrows, Code&nbsp; Blue, D/Code, G5 Broadheads, Prime Bows,&nbsp;&nbsp; and Redneck Hunting Blinds. >>DANIEL: Cody was obviously onboard for improving native habitat, and I&nbsp; recommended that he not stop at the cedars&nbsp;&nbsp; but carry that improvement into the hardwoods&nbsp; - use TSI and improve the native habitat&nbsp;&nbsp; throughout much of the property. >>DANIEL: Right now, you look through and there’s, there’s nothing - >>CODY: No, there’s some oaks up there at the top. >>DANIEL: - on the ground level. >>DANIEL: But if we open that up and, you&nbsp; know, you’re leaving some of those big oaks through there much more productive. >>DANIEL: The high-graded timber wasn’t providing a lot of quality habitat. It was a&nbsp; closed-canopy forest during the summer - not&nbsp;&nbsp; a lot of sunlight is reaching the ground.&nbsp; Now, there were some acorns being produced,&nbsp;&nbsp; but on that large scale Cody’s acorns when&nbsp; they’re on the ground, well the neighbor’s&nbsp;&nbsp; acorns are on the ground, and there’s not much&nbsp; difference between acorns, and patterning and&nbsp;&nbsp; hunting deer in that type of habitat is very&nbsp; difficult. Deer could be on Cody’s property,&nbsp;&nbsp; or they could very easily be on the neighbor’s. >>DANIEL: I recommended that Cody either use the hack-and-squirt method or the double-girdling&nbsp;technique to terminate the low-quality trees.&nbsp; >>DANIEL: I’d - I’d almost just say&nbsp; that’s - that’s the better tree and&nbsp;&nbsp; everything else around it is going. >>CODY: Okay. >>DANIEL: Because now - now you’re&nbsp;really releasing that tree. >>DANIEL: This would accomplish two missions.&nbsp;First, by opening up that canopy, more sunlight would reach the ground, and after Cody used&nbsp;prescribed fire to remove that leaf litter, natives would be able to come up and offer&nbsp; great cover and browse for wildlife.&nbsp; >>DANIEL: The quality oaks that Cody leaves&nbsp; standing, well they’re gonna receive more&nbsp;&nbsp; sunlight, and they’re gonna release their&nbsp; potential. They’re gonna photosynthesize&nbsp;&nbsp; more and won’t have as much competition for water&nbsp; and nutrients. They’ll be able to produce larger,&nbsp;&nbsp; more, and better-tasting acorns. >>DANIEL: If Cody’s oaks are producing better tasting acorns than his neighbors,&nbsp; that could be just the edge to tagging&nbsp;&nbsp; a deer when acorns are on the ground. >>DANIEL: Deer are very selective. They’re gonna eat the best and then go to the rest. We’ve&nbsp;experienced some great hunts hunting near large&nbsp;&nbsp; oaks that have been released, that don’t have&nbsp; competition and are photosynthesizing more. &nbsp; >>DANIEL: Even though there were a lot&nbsp; of oaks in the area dropping acorns,&nbsp;&nbsp; deer were coming to those specific trees to munch&nbsp; on acorns, and it resulted in fresh venison.&nbsp; >> RAE: Yes! >>DANIEL: Due to the terrain, Cody’s property was limited&nbsp; to areas that could be used as food plots,&nbsp;&nbsp; so much of our focus was on&nbsp; native browse and cover.&nbsp; >>DANIEL: However, we did find areas&nbsp; to expand an existing food plot&nbsp;&nbsp; and create several new plots. >>DANIEL: By maximizing food plot acreage and getting more tons of quality forage&nbsp; on Cody’s property and improving the native&nbsp;&nbsp; habitat - not only cover, but native browse&nbsp; - Cody’s property becomes very attractive.&nbsp; >>DANIEL: Going back to the Rubik’s&nbsp; Cube illustration, Cody’s property&nbsp;&nbsp; is now an island. He’s got the best cover and food&nbsp; in the neighborhood. And when deer are seeking&nbsp;&nbsp; those specific resources, they’re&nbsp; gonna want to spend more time&nbsp;&nbsp; within their home range around Cody’s property. >>DANIEL: As Cody begins implementing the plan, I’m confident he’ll have some great hunting&nbsp;opportunities, and I look forward to updates&nbsp;&nbsp; and seeing those grip and grin photos. >>DANIEL: As we travel across the whitetails’ range assisting landowners with their&nbsp; habitat and hunting improvement plans,&nbsp;&nbsp; we’ll be sharing updates and the&nbsp; techniques we’re sharing with them.&nbsp;&nbsp; So, stay tuned on our social media. >>DANIEL: Whether you’re out improving habitat, looking for sheds, or starting to scout&nbsp; for turkeys, I hope you slow down this&nbsp;&nbsp; week and enjoy Creation. As of September 1, 1996, I am retired from the Colorado Springs Police Department. and I signed it. And that was that

- How BOUNTY HUNTERS Tracked Down WANTED Outlaws in the Wild West?: Kind: captions Language: en The most fearless manhunt the American West has&nbsp; ever seen came to an end when Frank Reno was shot&nbsp;&nbsp; in the chest while running over a frozen cornfield&nbsp; close to Seymour, Indiana. In December of 1868,&nbsp;&nbsp; Alan Pinkerton had recently demonstrated that&nbsp; a private investigative firm was capable of&nbsp;&nbsp; achieving goals that sheriffs, marshals, and&nbsp; entire posies were unable to achieve. follow&nbsp;&nbsp; a group of railroers across two international&nbsp; borders and three states. However, Pinkerton's&nbsp;&nbsp; strategies for eliminating the Reno brothers would&nbsp; drastically alter America's approach to pursuing&nbsp;&nbsp; wanted men. Before the transcontinental railroad&nbsp; stitched America together in 1869, the geography&nbsp;&nbsp; of crime operated on a simple principle. A horse&nbsp; could carry you faster than news of your crime&nbsp;&nbsp; could travel. Sheriffs had jurisdiction only&nbsp; within county lines. Town marshals controlled&nbsp;&nbsp; a few dusty streets. Federal marshals existed&nbsp; but remained desperately few. Scattered across&nbsp;&nbsp; territories larger than European nations. Into&nbsp; this vacuum stepped men who worked not for badges&nbsp;&nbsp; but for percentages. They were called thief&nbsp; takers, regulators, manhunters. But the term&nbsp;&nbsp; that stuck was bounty hunter and they operated&nbsp; in a legal twilight that made them simultaneously&nbsp;&nbsp; essential and terrifying to the communities that&nbsp; employed them. The economics drove everything.&nbsp;&nbsp; A county government might post $200 for a horse&nbsp; thief, $500 for a murderer, a thousand for a train&nbsp;&nbsp; robber. The bounty hunter received no salary,&nbsp; no pension, no support. Every meal, every horse,&nbsp;&nbsp; every informant bribe came from his own pocket,&nbsp; reimbursed only if he delivered the wanted man&nbsp;&nbsp; alive or produced a body with sufficient&nbsp; identification. This arrangement created a&nbsp;&nbsp; peculiar kind of professional, part detective,&nbsp; part entrepreneur, part gambler, betting weeks&nbsp;&nbsp; or months of work against the possibility of a&nbsp; single payday. Some were former law men who found&nbsp;&nbsp; the constraints of a badge too limiting. Others&nbsp; were former criminals who understood the outlaw&nbsp;&nbsp; mind because they had lived it. Most occupied a&nbsp; moral space somewhere between. If you're drawn to&nbsp;&nbsp; stories about the systems that actually shaped&nbsp; the frontier, the mechanisms hiding behind the&nbsp;&nbsp; mythology, then subscribe now because this channel&nbsp; exists to show you the documented history that&nbsp;&nbsp; Hollywood left out. Patrick Floyd Garrett stands&nbsp; as perhaps the clearest example of how a man could&nbsp;&nbsp; slip between the worlds of law and lawlessness.&nbsp; Born in Alabama in 1850, he drifted west as a&nbsp;&nbsp; buffalo hunter, then a cowboy, then a bartender&nbsp; who ran with rustlers in the New Mexico territory.&nbsp;&nbsp; Before the citizens of Lincoln County elected him&nbsp; sheriff in 1880, they did not elect him for his&nbsp;&nbsp; moral clarity. They elected him because he knew&nbsp; Billy the kid personally, had drunk with him,&nbsp;&nbsp; gambled with him, and understood how the kid&nbsp; thought. that knowledge was a commodity more&nbsp;&nbsp; valuable than honesty. Garrett's pursuit of Billy&nbsp; the Kid across the staked plains in the winter of&nbsp;&nbsp; 1880 into 1881 illustrates the tracking methods&nbsp; that defined the era. Methods that had nothing&nbsp;&nbsp; to do with the dramatic showdowns later invented&nbsp; for dime novels. Garrett did not track footprints&nbsp;&nbsp; across open desert. He tracked information across&nbsp; a network of informants paying cash for sightings&nbsp;&nbsp; for rumors for the names of sympathizers who&nbsp; might be sheltering the outlaw. In December 1880,&nbsp;&nbsp; Garrett learned that the kid had been seen near&nbsp; Fort Sumner. He rode there with a posi, surrounded&nbsp;&nbsp; a house in the dark, and waited. When the kid&nbsp; emerged, Garrett called for surrender. The kid&nbsp;&nbsp; ran. Garrett's men opened fire, killing Charlie&nbsp; Bodri, one of the kid's companions. But the kid&nbsp;&nbsp; escaped in the chaos. This was not failure. This&nbsp; was how manhunts actually worked. You gathered&nbsp;&nbsp; intelligence. You made attempts. You failed.&nbsp; You gathered more intelligence. You tried again.&nbsp;&nbsp; Patience was the weapon, not marksmanship. After&nbsp; Billy the Kid escaped from jail in April 1881,&nbsp;&nbsp; killing two deputies in the process, Garrett faced&nbsp; a professional crisis. The escape had humiliated&nbsp;&nbsp; him. The territorial governor wanted results.&nbsp; Garrett's authority depended on his ability&nbsp;&nbsp; to deliver the one outlaw he had personally&nbsp; guaranteed to bring in. He returned to his&nbsp;&nbsp; informant network and in July, a source told him&nbsp; the kid was staying with Pete Maxwell at Fort&nbsp;&nbsp; Sumner. Garrett arrived after midnight on July&nbsp; 14th, entered Maxwell's darkened bedroom, and sat&nbsp;&nbsp; beside Maxwell's bed, questioning him in whispers.&nbsp; Billy the kid walked into that same room, could&nbsp;&nbsp; not see clearly in the darkness, asked in Spanish&nbsp; who was there, and Garrett fired twice. The kid&nbsp;&nbsp; died instantly. Garrett had won through superior&nbsp; intelligence, not superior gunfighting, and&nbsp;&nbsp; this pattern would repeat across countless other&nbsp; pursuits. But Garrett operated within a relatively&nbsp;&nbsp; confined geography, chasing a single man across a&nbsp; known territory. The truly transformative bounty&nbsp;&nbsp; hunting operations required something more. The&nbsp; infrastructure of modern surveillance imported&nbsp;&nbsp; into a landscape that still thought in terms&nbsp; of frontier justice. This was the innovation&nbsp;&nbsp; Alan Pinkerton brought to the profession and the&nbsp; Reno brothers gave him the opportunity to prove&nbsp;&nbsp; it would work. The Reno gang committed the first&nbsp; peacetime train robbery in American history on&nbsp;&nbsp; October 6th, 1866 when they stopped an Ohio and&nbsp; Mississippi railroad train near Seymour, Indiana,&nbsp;&nbsp; broke into the express car and stole $10,000.&nbsp; Trains were supposed to be invulnerable, protected&nbsp;&nbsp; by speed and schedules. The Reno brothers, Frank,&nbsp; John, and Simeon, along with their associate,&nbsp;&nbsp; Franklin Sparks, proved otherwise. And in doing&nbsp; so, they created a new category of criminal&nbsp;&nbsp; that terrified railroad executives more than any&nbsp; other threat. Trains carried payroll for mines,&nbsp;&nbsp; profits from grain harvests, cash for banks. If&nbsp; trains could be robbed with impunity, the entire&nbsp;&nbsp; financial circulatory system of westward expansion&nbsp; faced paralysis. Local sheriffs were useless. The&nbsp;&nbsp; Reno brothers had grown up in Jackson County,&nbsp; Indiana. They had family, friends, people who&nbsp;&nbsp; owed them favors or feared their retribution. The&nbsp; gang would strike, scatter, and vanish back into&nbsp;&nbsp; their home territory where witnesses remembered&nbsp; nothing and law men found only locked doors. After&nbsp;&nbsp; the gang robbed another train in 1867, stealing&nbsp; $13,000, the railroad companies made a decision&nbsp;&nbsp; that would echo for decades. They hired Alan&nbsp; Pinkerton. In March 1868, Pinkerton's operatives&nbsp;&nbsp; tracked John Reno and Frank Sparks to Indianapolis&nbsp; and arrested them. But here, the limitations of&nbsp;&nbsp; private law enforcement became brutally apparent.&nbsp; That night, a vigilante mob stormed the jail and&nbsp;&nbsp; attempted to lynch both men. Police fought off&nbsp; the mob, but the message was clear. The community&nbsp;&nbsp; around Seymour wanted blood, not trials. Pinkerton&nbsp; had demonstrated he could find anyone anywhere,&nbsp;&nbsp; but he could not control what happened afterward.&nbsp; The surviving Reno brothers fled to Canada,&nbsp;&nbsp; calculating correctly that extradition treaties&nbsp; moved slowly and that distance still offered&nbsp;&nbsp; protection. They were wrong about Pinkerton's&nbsp; reach. He sent operatives to Windsor, Ontario,&nbsp;&nbsp; where the brothers were hiding and worked with&nbsp; Canadian authorities and the US State Department&nbsp;&nbsp; to begin extradition proceedings. Frank Reno&nbsp; fought extradition for 8 months, hiring lawyers,&nbsp;&nbsp; filing appeals, but Pinkerton had resources that&nbsp; matched his own. The detective agency was not&nbsp;&nbsp; constrained by jurisdictional boundaries or budget&nbsp; limitations because the railroads were paying and&nbsp;&nbsp; the railroads had effectively unlimited funds when&nbsp; it came to protecting their shipments. In October&nbsp;&nbsp; 1868, Canadian authorities handed Frank Reno over&nbsp; to Pinkerton operatives, who transported him by&nbsp;&nbsp; train back to Indiana. He never made it to trial.&nbsp; On the night of December 11th, 1868, vigilantes&nbsp;&nbsp; wearing masks stormed the New Albany jail where&nbsp; Frank Reno was being held along with two other&nbsp;&nbsp; gang members. They overpowered the guards, dragged&nbsp; all three men from their cells, and hanged them&nbsp;&nbsp; from the same beam. Pinkerton had delivered his&nbsp; targets, but the community had delivered its own&nbsp;&nbsp; verdict, and the line between law enforcement and&nbsp; lynch law had dissolved completely. This outcome&nbsp;&nbsp; revealed the central paradox of bounty hunting&nbsp; in the Old West. The profession existed because&nbsp;&nbsp; formal legal institutions were weak, but its&nbsp; success often strengthened extralegal violence&nbsp;&nbsp; rather than legitimate courts. Bounty hunters&nbsp; brought outlaws back to communities that were&nbsp;&nbsp; tired of waiting for slow trials and unreliable&nbsp; juries. The hunters provided the capture. The&nbsp;&nbsp; mobs provided the punishment. This cycle repeated&nbsp; across the frontier with a frequency that official&nbsp;&nbsp; records often obscured because no one in authority&nbsp; wanted to document their own irrelevance.&nbsp;&nbsp; Texas offered the most extreme version of this&nbsp; dynamic because of its unique legal structure.&nbsp;&nbsp; After reconstruction ended in 1877, the Texas&nbsp; Rangers were reorganized as a state level law&nbsp;&nbsp; enforcement body, but vast stretches of West Texas&nbsp; remained effectively ungoverned. Cattle rustling&nbsp;&nbsp; plagued ranchers, and the distances involved made&nbsp; traditional pursuit nearly impossible. A rustler&nbsp;&nbsp; could steal cattle in one county, drive them 200&nbsp; miles to another, sell them, and disappear before&nbsp;&nbsp; the original owner even noticed the theft. Large&nbsp; ranching syndicates responded by hiring stock&nbsp;&nbsp; detective associations, which were essentially&nbsp; bounty hunting organizations with polite names.&nbsp;&nbsp; The most notorious was the Anti-Horse Thief&nbsp; Association, which operated across Kansas, Texas,&nbsp;&nbsp; and Oklahoma territory in the 1880s and 1890s.&nbsp; Members paid dues and in exchange received the&nbsp;&nbsp; association's services, tracking stolen livestock,&nbsp; identifying thieves, and recovering property.&nbsp;&nbsp; The association employed full-time detectives&nbsp; who maintained lists of known rustlers,&nbsp;&nbsp; monitored livestock sales, and coordinated&nbsp; with railroad agents to track suspicious&nbsp;&nbsp; shipments. When they identified a thief, they&nbsp; had three options. Turn him over to local law&nbsp;&nbsp; enforcement, recover the stolen property through&nbsp; intimidation, or kill him and claim he resisted.&nbsp;&nbsp; Records are incomplete regarding how&nbsp; often the third option was chosen,&nbsp;&nbsp; but evidence from court cases and newspaper&nbsp; accounts suggests it was not rare. In 1888,&nbsp;&nbsp; a stock detective named Frank Canton shot and&nbsp; killed two suspected rustlers in Johnson County,&nbsp;&nbsp; Wyoming, claiming self-defense. No charges were&nbsp; filed. Canton was on the payroll of the Wyoming&nbsp;&nbsp; Stock Growers Association, which included some&nbsp; of the wealthiest cattlemen in the territory.&nbsp;&nbsp; His employers considered his actions justified&nbsp; because the legal system had failed to protect&nbsp;&nbsp; their property. Canton would later participate in&nbsp; the Johnson County War of 1892, an armed invasion&nbsp;&nbsp; by cattlemen and hired gunmen intended to&nbsp; eliminate rustlers through mass execution.&nbsp;&nbsp; Federal troops intervened before the killing&nbsp; escalated beyond two deaths. But the incident&nbsp;&nbsp; demonstrated how bounty hunting could metastasize&nbsp; into private warfare when communities lost faith&nbsp;&nbsp; in formal justice. The federal government's&nbsp; response to this chaos was belated and inadequate.&nbsp;&nbsp; In 1865, there were fewer than 20 US marshals&nbsp; responsible for the entire Transmissippi West.&nbsp;&nbsp; Each marshall could appoint deputies, but these&nbsp; positions were feebased. A deputy marshal earned&nbsp;&nbsp; money through specific services. 6 cents per&nbsp; mile traveled in pursuit of a fugitive, $2 for&nbsp;&nbsp; an arrest, 10 cents for each subpoena served. This&nbsp; incentive structure meant deputies focused on high&nbsp;&nbsp; volume, low-risk work like serving court documents&nbsp; rather than dangerous manhunts. The reward system&nbsp;&nbsp; encouraged bounty hunters to fill the gap&nbsp; because they worked on commission for results,&nbsp;&nbsp; not process. Judge Isaac Parker's court in Fort&nbsp; Smith, Arkansas, illustrated both the potential&nbsp;&nbsp; and the limits of federal law enforcement on&nbsp; the frontier. Between 1875 and 1896, Parker's&nbsp;&nbsp; jurisdiction covered 74,000 square miles of Indian&nbsp; territory, including what would become Oklahoma.&nbsp;&nbsp; He employed up to 200 deputy marshals&nbsp; at any given time, making his court the&nbsp;&nbsp; largest federal law enforcement operation in&nbsp; the West. These deputies were technically not&nbsp;&nbsp; bounty hunters because they received federal&nbsp; commissions, but they operated identically,&nbsp;&nbsp; tracking fugitives across vast distances, working&nbsp; alone or in small groups, gambling their lives&nbsp;&nbsp; against fee payments and occasional rewards. Bass&nbsp; Reeves stands as the most successful of Parker's&nbsp;&nbsp; deputies and perhaps the most effective&nbsp; manhunt specialist in frontier history.&nbsp;&nbsp; Born enslaved in Arkansas in 1838, Reeves escaped&nbsp; to Indian territory during the Civil War, lived&nbsp;&nbsp; among Creek and Seol communities, learned multiple&nbsp; indigenous languages, and developed tracking&nbsp;&nbsp; skills that combined traditional indigenous&nbsp; knowledge with his own innovations. After the war,&nbsp;&nbsp; he worked as a farmer and scout before Judge&nbsp; Parker hired him as a deputy marshal in 1875.&nbsp;&nbsp; Over the next 32 years, Reeves arrested more than&nbsp; 3,000 fugitives, killed 14 men in self-defense,&nbsp;&nbsp; and was never wounded. Despite working in&nbsp; the most dangerous jurisdiction in America,&nbsp;&nbsp; Reeves succeeded because he understood&nbsp; that tracking was primarily theatrical.&nbsp;&nbsp; He used disguises constantly, posing as a drifter,&nbsp; a cowboy, an outlaw on the run, gaining access to&nbsp;&nbsp; criminal networks by seeming to belong. In one&nbsp; documented case from 1884, Reeves was hunting&nbsp;&nbsp; two brothers wanted for murder who were hiding in&nbsp; the Creek Nation. He dressed in ragged clothes,&nbsp;&nbsp; handcuffed his own wrists, and walked into&nbsp; their camp, claiming to be an escaped prisoner.&nbsp;&nbsp; The brothers believed him, fed him, and fell&nbsp; asleep. Reeves waited until deep in the night,&nbsp;&nbsp; retrieved his hidden revolver, and arrested both&nbsp; men without firing a shot. The performance was the&nbsp;&nbsp; weapon. But even Reeves operated within a system&nbsp; that was breaking under its own contradictions.&nbsp;&nbsp; By the 1890s, as railroads connected the&nbsp; frontier and telegraph lines carried information&nbsp;&nbsp; instantaneously, the geographic advantages that&nbsp; had protected outlaws disappeared. Butch Cassidy&nbsp;&nbsp; and the Sundance Kid discovered this in 1901 when&nbsp; Pinkerton operatives tracked them from Wyoming to&nbsp;&nbsp; New York to Argentina using steamship passenger&nbsp; lists and banking records. The Wild Bunch had&nbsp;&nbsp; been among the most successful outlaw gangs&nbsp; of the 1890s precisely because they understood&nbsp;&nbsp; infrastructure. They robbed trains and banks near&nbsp; railroad junctions, used relay stations of fresh&nbsp;&nbsp; horses, and dispersed into remote wilderness&nbsp; areas where posies lost their trail. But the&nbsp;&nbsp; same infrastructure that enabled their robberies&nbsp; also enabled their pursuers. After a train robbery&nbsp;&nbsp; near Wagner, Montana in 1901, Pinkerton detectives&nbsp; identified Sundance Kid from photographs, tracked&nbsp;&nbsp; his girlfriend to New York, and followed the trio&nbsp; to South America. Pinkerton could not arrest them&nbsp;&nbsp; there, but he coordinated with South American&nbsp; authorities, distributed wanted posters, and made&nbsp;&nbsp; their lives as fugitives increasingly difficult.&nbsp; The West was closing not because of gunfights,&nbsp;&nbsp; but because of recordkeeping, photography,&nbsp; fingerprinting, and international communication&nbsp;&nbsp; networks. Bounty hunters were becoming obsolete&nbsp; because institutions were finally catching up.&nbsp;&nbsp; The social consequences of this transition were&nbsp; profound and remain visible today. Bounty hunting&nbsp;&nbsp; had emerged from institutional weakness, but&nbsp; it normalized extraleal violence, vigilantism,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the idea that justice was a private commodity&nbsp; available to those who could pay. Communities&nbsp;&nbsp; that had relied on bounty hunters and stock&nbsp; detective associations internalized the logic&nbsp;&nbsp; that law enforcement was something you purchased&nbsp; rather than something government provided.&nbsp;&nbsp; This ideology shaped political culture in the&nbsp; Mountain West and Southwest for generations,&nbsp;&nbsp; creating resistance to federal authority,&nbsp; skepticism towards centralized law enforcement,&nbsp;&nbsp; and a persistent belief that individuals had the&nbsp; right to use lethal force to protect property.&nbsp;&nbsp; The professionalization of law enforcement&nbsp; in the early 20th century did not eliminate&nbsp;&nbsp; bounty hunting, but drove it underground. States&nbsp; began requiring bail bondsmen to be licensed,&nbsp;&nbsp; regulating the conditions under which fugitives&nbsp; could be pursued and limiting the use of lethal&nbsp;&nbsp; force. But the legal structure remained. A person&nbsp; released on bail is technically in the custody of&nbsp;&nbsp; the bail bondsman. And if that person flees, the&nbsp; bondsman has extraordinary authority to recapture&nbsp;&nbsp; them, often employing bounty hunters who work on&nbsp; commission. The modern bail enforcement agent is a&nbsp;&nbsp; direct descendant of Pat Garrett and Bass Reeves,&nbsp; operating in the same legal gray zone, motivated&nbsp;&nbsp; by the same economic incentives, confronting the&nbsp; same moral ambiguities. This reality was buried&nbsp;&nbsp; by the mythology of the Old West, which replaced&nbsp; it with a more straightforward tale of lone heroes&nbsp;&nbsp; and dramatic shootouts. However, the real history&nbsp; shows something more pertinent and unsettling. The&nbsp;&nbsp; long lasting effects of institutional failure,&nbsp; the brittleleness of governmental power,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the ease with which violence is&nbsp; commercialized. Communities go to anyone&nbsp;&nbsp; who can produce results when they are unable to&nbsp; rely on official police enforcement. Furthermore,&nbsp;&nbsp; it is rare for those deliverers to arrive&nbsp; without expenses that outlive the initial issue.&nbsp;&nbsp; They were not romantic characters, the bounty&nbsp; hunters who pursued outlaws throughout the&nbsp;&nbsp; frontier. They were signs of a legal&nbsp; system that was still learning how to&nbsp;&nbsp; operate over great distances. Additionally, the&nbsp; approaches they developed to address one issue&nbsp;&nbsp; gave rise to new issues that America&nbsp; has never been able to properly fix.

- How Can Out-of-state Hunters Reduce Hunting License Costs? - The Marksman's Journal: Kind: captions Language: en How can outofstate hunters reduce hunting license costs? Imagine planning a hunting trip to a new state and wondering how to keep costs manageable. Hunting licenses can vary a lot from state to state, but there are ways to save money even if you're coming from out of state. First, many states offer short-term licenses like 3-day or 7-day options, which are perfect if you're only visiting for a quick trip. These licenses usually cost less than full season licenses and are designed for visitors who want to hunt for a limited time. For example, some states sell 3-day non-resident licenses for small game or big game at a fraction of the full price license. Next, consider combo licenses that cover multiple species or activities. If you plan to hunt both deer and turkey or fish as well, buying a package license can save you money compared to purchasing separate permits. These combo licenses often come at a discounted rate, making your trip more affordable. Also, check if you qualify for youth or military discounts. Many states offer reduced fees for young hunters, sometimes as low as $10 for a license. Active duty active duty military members and veterans might also get free or discounted licenses. So, it's worth looking into those options. In states with lottery or draw systems for certain tags, buying preference or bonus points can help you get a hunting permit without paying full price each year. These points can be accumulated over time, increasing your chances of drawing a tag in future seasons. It's a smart way to plan ahead and cut costs in the long run. If you have access to private land or can hunt on landowner permission, you might find lower cost or even free hunting opportunities. Some states offer special permits or reduced fees for private land hunting, which can be a big money saver. Another option is to use mentored or apprentice hunting licenses. These licenses are designed for new hunters or those trying out hunting without a full license. They tend to be less expensive and are a good way to learn and enjoy hunting without a large upfront investment. It's also important to research each state's specific licensing rules and fee structures because they can change. Some states have recently adjusted fees to better support conservation efforts. And staying informed helps you choose the most economical options. Finally, if you plan to hunt multiple seasons or trips, look into multi-year licenses or licenses that remain valid until used. Some states allow licenses to be valid for several years, which can reduce the need to buy a new license every time you hunt. Planning ahead and understanding these options can help you save money while respecting safety and legal regulations. Always make sure your license is valid for the species and methods you plan to use. Whether it's archery, firearms, or other techniques, following the rules and hunting ethically supports wildlife management and keeps hunting sustainable for everyone.

analogy kiss which stands for keep it simple stupid and in an era of flashy over complicated hunting strategies it can get kind of overwhelming especially if you're new into hunting so today we're technique that someone could realistically only use probably one percent of the time so be sure to like this video and be sure to share it with your buddies so you too can kiss while let's get into it the same applies for deer foreign we use e-scouting and boots on the ground scouting interchangeably in a method we call the funnel method and simply put think of this whole scouting portion as a funnel it starts large and it gets smaller and smaller so to start we will e-scout and then put boots on the ground scouting and hang cameras in spots where we probably won't hunt during the season it sounds a little weird but I'll explain before we dive right into the interesting stuff like killing big giant bucks first we gotta know where to find them and to do that we got to start from scratch hunt so which one would be the best to start scouting well the answer we came up with is the one that has the most diversity around it and this is where we include the phrase x marks the spot this is a phrase I heard from Parker McDonald from Southern ground hunting and it just made so much sense to me and to put it in a super simple sentence that's a pretty good tongue twister look for a place where different types of habitat or terrain meet because the more diversity you have in one spot the better the odds the deer will probably be using that area so as we all know deer are creatures of edge habitat so what we want to do x marks the spot is fill Each corner of the X with a different piece of diversity for example I'm pretty big on hunting near water so that checks off one corner of the X and since where I primarily Hunt is nothing but Pines Oaks and Hills I would follow a creek and look for a spot where maybe there is an oak flat within the pines and let's just say not too far away there is an old clear cut that now has head high grass so with the water Pines Oaks and the tall grass field you now have all corners of your eggs filled with a lot of diversity in one area and from there just put boots on the ground and look for sign or anything that looks Deary within that X and then slap a camera through there so that's where I'd first start looking for that hit list or In this episode, we're going to be tackling one of the most common questions I get and one of the hardest for newer hunters to answer, especially coming into the new year. Where do I even begin with elk hunting? For a lot of people, elk hunting feels like staring up at a massive mountain. Overwhelming, intimidating, and almost impossible to know where to take the first step. You've got gear lists that seem endless, maps with more drainages than you could ever cover, and endless opinions online about calling strategies, tags, or the best units. But those mistakes taught me something important. Success in elk hunting doesn't start with having it all figured out. It starts with building a foundation. And that's what this episode is all about.
