Elk hunting is hard, no question about it. But it's also one of the most rewarding things you can ever take on. It'll test you, it'll break you, and it'll build you back up stronger than before. The key is knowing how to start. So, if you've ever wondered how to take that first step into the elk woods, or if you've been spinning your wheels trying to figure out the right way to prepare, this episode is for you. In chapter one, we're going to define your why. Before you ever buy a tag, download a map app, or lace up your boots, you need to stop and answer one question. Why elimin? That might sound like some motivational fluff, but I promise you it's not. Your why is the fuel that's going to get you through the grind of the mountains. Elk hunting isn't something you dabble in like a Saturday morning golf game. It's one of the hardest things you'll ever take on in the outdoors. You'll deal with long miles, brutal climbs, unpredictable weather, aching legs, and days where the elk just don't cooperate. If you don't have a deep reason for being out there, those days are going to break you. And the shallow why, it doesn't hold. A lot of hunters step into elk hunting for the wrong reasons. Maybe it's a picture of a monster 6x6 bull they saw online. Maybe it's the bragging rights of having a rack hanging in the garage. Or maybe it's just the idea that elk hunting looks cool. Here's the truth. Those reasons won't hold up when you're on day six of a hunt. You haven't heard a bugle and your body is wrecked. You're going to pack it up, head to town, and convince yourself it just wasn't meant to be. Elk hunting has a way of testing your resolve like nothing else. And a shallow why will crumble fast. The deeper why is what carries you. The guys who keep going, the ones who fill tags are out there for something bigger. Their why might be adventure, testing themselves against the mountain and pushing limits they didn't know they had. Providing filling the freezer with some of the healthiest, wildest meat you can eat. Heritage, carrying on a tradition or building one for their kids. growth, wanting to prove something to themselves to get better, stronger, tougher. And that's why that why keeps you moving forward when your lungs are on fire and your legs want to quit. It's the why that gets you up at 4:00 a.m. day after day to climb another ridge, even when yesterday was a bust. My personal why for me, elk hunting has always been about more than antlers. Don't get me wrong, I love a he heavy set of horns as much as the next guy, but my true why has always been the challenge and the growth that comes from it. Elk hunting strips away your excuses. It forces you to deal with discomfort, to figure out problems, and to endure when it would be easy to stop. That's what's kept me coming back for more than 34 years. Every hunt teaches me something new about the mountains, about elk, and about myself. And why this matters for you as a beginner is that you might be tempted to skip this step. You might think, "Ah, that's cheesy. I'll figure it out when I'm out there." But I can tell you from experience, if you don't nail this down now, you're going to struggle later. Your why becomes your anchor. When you're glassing for the third straight day without spotting an elk, you remind yourself, "I'm here because I wanted to prove to myself that I could do hard things." When your shoulders are screaming under a 90 lb pack, you remind provide clean meat for my family." That mindset shift turns suffering into purpose. And when your suffering has purpose, you keep going. [snorts] Here's some practical steps to help you define your why. How do you actually do this? Right? Here's the process. Write it down. Don't just think about it. Put it on paper. Again, might sound cheesy to you, but words become real when you commit them to ink. Be specific. I want to hunt elk isn't enough. Try something like, I want to hunt elk because I want to test my limits and put clean wild food on my table. Check it against reality and ask yourself, will this why hold up when things get tough? If it feels shallow, you got to go deeper. [snorts] Keep it visible. Put it somewhere you're going to see it while you're training or planning and remind yourself of it often. This is the foundation of success. I know this might not feel as exciting as gearless or calling strategies, but trust me, this is where elk hunting really begins. Without a strong why, all the best tactics in the world won't matter. Because the first time things get tough, you're going to fold. But with a strong why, you'll have a backbone that carries you through every setback. And in the elkwoods, setbacks are guaranteed. Success isn't guaranteed, but persistence, that's in your control. Defining your why gives you a reason to persist. It's the first and most important step in your elk hunting journey. Chapter two, we're going to talk about understanding elk behavior. When you're starting out, just starting out, the single biggest mistake you can make is thinking elk are just oversized deer. And they're not. If you go into elk hunting with that mindset, you'll spend more time confused and frustrated than you will learning. Elk are a completely different animal. They're bigger, louder, more social, and far more mobile. And if you don't understand how they live, move, and survive, you're going to spend a lot of time chasing shadows. Elk are herd animals. At the most basic level, elk are herd animals, and that means that they think move and survive as a group. Where one goes, more will follow. And that can be both a blessing and a curse. It means that if you find one elk, you're often in the right neighborhood to find more. But it also means when one cow catches your wind, the whole group is gone in an instant. In the fall, bulls gather her and cows, right? Competing with each other for dominance. You're not hunting individuals as much as you are hunting group dynamics. And that's why you'll often see a single bull with 15 to 20 cows. And why sometimes bugling at one elk suddenly brings in others you didn't know were there. Let's talk about their seasonal movements. Understanding elk behavior starts with knowing how they move throughout the seasons. In summer, elk are up high in the alpine meadows and cooler timber. They're feeding heavy on grasses and forbes, building strength for the rut. In early September, bulls start separating from bachelor groups, moving towards cows. Elk are still focused on feed and water, but social dynamics are shifting. During the peak rut, which is mid to late September, this is the chaos in the mountains. Everything everybody dreams about. Bugling, fighting, hurting, constant movement. It's the most exciting time to be in the woods. but also one of the hardest to predict because bulls are running themselves ragged. The post rut, we're going to call that October on. Bulls break off and seek recovery zones. Isolated, more quiet country with feed and security. Cows herd up, focused on survival. During the late season, we're going to say November on deep into December. Elk are going to drop lower following food sources like agricultural fields, winter ranges, and south facing slopes where snow melts first. When you know where elk should be in a given month, you stop wasting time in the wrong country. You've got to focus on their daily needs: food, water, cover, and security. Elk are slaves to those four basic needs. their food, their grasses, forbes, shrubs, and whatever else is available. They'll feed in open meadows at night and bed and cover during the day. Water. Elk need daily access to water, springs, creeks, seeps, wallows. Pay attention to how close that is to feed and bedding areas. Cover. Elk love north facing slopes, benches, and thick timber for bedding. They want shade, cool temps, and security. They want that security more than anything. Elk avoid pressure. Hunters, predators, even hikers can push them into new drainages. Once pressured, they don't usually come back quickly. Every elk movement you'll ever see revolves around those four needs. Learn to identify them on the map and confirm them in person, and you're going to already be 10 steps ahead. You've got to understand also that elk respond to pressure. This might be the hardest lesson for beginners to grasp. Elk don't tolerate human pressure at all. If they smell you, hear you, or sense repeated disturbance, they're gone. And not just 200 yards gone, often a whole drainage away. Elk have massive home ranges, and they'll happily move if an area feels unsafe. And that's why some hunters say the elk just disappeared overnight. They didn't disappear, they just shifted to where they felt safer. This is why things like wind, calling discipline, and access routes matter so much. Elk can survive wolves and mountain lions, but sloppy hunters spooked them faster than anything. I've learned this lesson the tough way. Early in my hunting years, I thought if I just found fresh sign and called enough, eventually a bull would show up. Instead, I educated more elk than I care to admit. I'd blow a bugle into a drainage, get an answer, charge in without thinking about thermals, and suddenly the woods would go dead. It wasn't until I slowed down, studied their behavior, and respected how sensitive they are at a presence that I started consistently finding elk, right? Once you understand that their survival depends on detecting threats before threats get close, you realize you're not just hunting elk, you're hunting elk behavior. So, how do you apply this as a beginner? Start by observing. Don't feel like you need to call or charge in every time you find sign. Sit back, glass, and watch. Think like an elk. Ask, "Where would I feed? Where would I water? Where would I bed to stay cool and hidden? Where would I run if there was pressure?" Try to keep a journal, especially in the beginning. I still do. Every encounter you have, good or bad, write down what happened and why. Over time, patterns are going to emerge for you. And here's the truth. Elk hunting is really just pattern recognition. The more you understand how elk behave, the easier it gets to predict where they're going to be. And when you can predict where they're going to be, you stop relying on luck and start relying on knowledge. If you want to begin elk hunting the right way, don't start with calls or gadgets. Start by learning elk. Because when you understand the animal, every decision you make in the field becomes sharper, more intentional, and more successful. In chapter 3, we're going to talk about choosing a state and a season. One of the first big roadblocks new elk hunters run into is figuring out where to hunt. You jump online and suddenly you're buried in regulations, tag systems, point creep, and more opinions than you can sort through. It feels like you need a law degree just to buy a tag. And a lot of new hunters get stuck here. They spend so much time researching the perfect hunt that they never actually step into the elkwoods. And here's the truth, though. The best elk hunt for a beginner is the one you can actually go on. That might be over- thecounter or draw tags. When we talk about elk tags, there are two main categories. Those over- the-counter OTC and limited entry or draw tags. OTC tags are available to anybody every year without waiting. You buy it, you hunt. The tradeoff is they're usually more pressure because everyone else has the same access. But for a new hunter, if you can find OTC tags, they are gold. They're your classroom. They're going to be what helps you learn because you're out there. Now, your draw tags, those are limited entry permits you apply for and may or may not draw depending on the point system and odds. The quality of hunting is often higher. Fewer hunters, more animals, better trophy potential. But here's the problem. If you're new, waiting a lot of years to build up points for a tag means you're missing out on decades of learning. If you're just starting out, don't chase the dream tag. Buy an OTC tag and get boots in the dirt. Experience will always be more valuable than points. Let's talk about a few states to consider. Every western state has elk hunting, but here's a quick breakdown. Colorado has some of the most opportunity and the largest elk herd in the country. Lots of easily gained archery and rifle hunts available. The pressure is high, but so is the chance to learn. In Idaho, you've got solid elk numbers, right? Pretty easy tags to draw in some places. Rugged country. Tags sell out fast, so you've got to plan ahead. Montana has general tags that can be drawn with relatively low points and offers both archery and rifle opportunities. Oregon has good over-the-c counter opportunities, but elk numbers aren't as high as some of the other states. Still a great classroom. Utah and Arizona, famous for trophy potential, but tags are hard to draw. great long-term goals, but not where you should begin. Wyoming also offers great opportunity. Slightly tougher to access for non-residents, but high quality opportunities when you do. The bottom line, Colorado, Idaho, Montana, probably your three breath starting points. They have elk, they have tags, and they give you the chance to build experience every single year. But I want to caution you on the trap of overplanning. I've seen too many new hunters get stuck in what I call tag paralysis. They think if they don't have the right tag in the right state, it's just not worth going. And that mindset robs you of years of experience. The reality is, every year you don't hunt, you're missing a year of learning. And no amount of YouTube, podcast, or ecouting are going to replace the time in the elkwoods. Even pressured over the counter elk are going to teach you more than sitting at home researching a better hunt. I've spent my whole life hunting higher pressured areas, over-the-counter public land tags across Colorado and Wyoming. The pressure is everywhere. It's tough country. Elk that acted like ghosts. At the time, I thought I was wasting my effort. But looking back, those hunts taught me lessons I couldn't have learned anywhere else. How elk respond to pressure, how to adjust on the fly, and how to stay relentless when the odds look slim. later when I drew a couple of quality tags, those skills paid off in a big way. If I just waited for the perfect hunt, I'd still be behind and learning a lot of the stuff that I've already been through. Action steps for beginners include picking a state you can hunt this year. Don't overthink it. Decide whether you're going to hunt archery or rifle. Pick one based on your strengths and interests. You've got to commit. Buy the tag. Mark it on your calendar. Start training and preparing for it. Why all of this matters? Elk hunting isn't about waiting for opportunity. It's about creating it. Every day you spend in the elk woods sharpens your instincts, builds your skills, and brings you one step closer to success. The worst elk hunt you ever go on will still teach you more than the best hunt you never went on. In chapter 4, we're going to talk about ecouting and ground truthing. Technology has changed the game in elk hunting. When I started out, we didn't have satellite maps or mobile GPS or 3D terrain tools. We had paper maps, compasses, and a lot of guesswork. Today, with apps like OnX and Google Earth, you can virtually scout an entire unit from your couch. And that's an amazing advantage if you know how to use it. But here's the trap. Too many hunters spend hundreds of hours escouting and never actually set foot in elk country. They drop a thousand way points, color code every drainage, and convince themselves they've got the mountain figured out. Then they show up and realize maps don't kill elk. The truth is, escouting is a tool. It gives you a starting point, but the real answers come from ground truthing. Putting boots on the dirt, [snorts] smelling the air, feeling the wind, and reading the country with your own eyes. Here's what you want to look for on the map. When you're e scouting, don't over complicate it. Start with the elk's four needs. food, water, cover, and security. For food, look for meadows, benches, burns, and open pockets where elk can feed. In the early season, that means high elevation meadows. Later in the year, south facing slopes and edges where grass holds longer. For water, you're looking for springs, seeps, wallows, creeks. Elk need water daily, and the closer they are to feed and bedding, the better the area is going to be. They're looking for cover. Thick north-facing timber, benches halfway up slopes, ridges with dark timber, elk bed where they feel safe and cool. They're looking for security. This one's harder to see on a map, but ask yourself, where can elk go to get away from hunters? Look for the roadless areas, the steep, dark drainages and pockets that are tough to access. If you can identify places where those needs overlap, maybe feed close to bedding, water nearby, cover for security, you found a triangle worth checking out in person. It's not just about where elk could be. It's about where they will be once the pressure hits. And that's where the escouting really shines. Look for roads and trails. Mark every access point and assume another hunter will be there. Then look for terrain features that put distance or obstacles between elk and people. Trail heads and camp spots. If it looks easy to get to, it's going to get pressure. Escape terrain. Identify adjacent drainages, ridges, and basins where elk might run once hunters move in. Pressure is one of the most important factors in modern elk hunting. The map can show you where pressure is likely to pile up, and you can plan accordingly. and make sure that you don't fall in love with a spot because of your escouting. One of the most common rookie mistakes is getting emotionally attached to a spot you found on the map. You mark it, you dream about it, you convince yourself it's the honey hole, then you hike in and you find boot tracks or another hunter or there's just old sign or no elk at all and your whole hunt feels crushed. Here's the fix. Don't fall in love with that single spot. Build multiple options. Have a plan A, B, C, D. The mountain rarely matches the map perfectly and elk behavior changes daily. Flexibility is what keeps you in the game. Now, ground truthing. This is where it all comes together. Boots on the ground reveal what maps can't. Fresh sign, tracks, scat, rubs, wallows, wind patterns, how thermals actually move in that drainage, not just how you think they should. Hunter pressure, where camps are set up or likely to be set up, how many trucks are going to be at the trail head, how beat up a trail looks, elk presence. You're never going to know for sure until you glass them, hear them, or bump them. Ground truthing is humbling, but it's where the learning happens. Every time you step into the country, you're going to be adding a new piece to the puzzle. Now, I remember a hunt where I'd marked the perfect meadow on my maps. food nearby, water close, north facing bedding above. I thought for sure it would be loaded with elk. I hiked six miles in, climbed a ridge, and nothing. Not a track, not a rub, not even an old piece of poop. Two days later, while moving to plan B, I found elk stacked in a small overlooked pocket just a half mile from a road, a place I ignored because I thought it was too close to pressure. That hunt reminded me that maps are starting points, not guarantees. Here are some practical steps for beginners and the escouting. Pick two to three areas per hunt. Not 10, not 20. You've got to keep it manageable. Mark access and pressure zones. Know where hunters will likely be before you arrive. Identify the triangles, right? The food, the water, the cover, all close together. Scout with your feet. The first day or two of your hunt, focus on learning where the elk actually are, not just where you thought they'd be. [snorts] Elk hunting is a moving target. They shift with pressure, weather, and seasons. If you only rely on maps, you're going to consistently feel like you're chasing ghosts. But if you pair smart ecouting with on the ground learning, you're going to adapt more quickly. And adapting is the difference between going home empty and going home with meat. You got to remember maps don't kill elk. Hunters who know how to use maps then confirm with bootleg do. In chapter 5, we're going to talk about the physical preparation for the mountains. If you want to know the truth, here it is. Elk hunting is hard. Not just I'm a little sore hard. It's the kind of hard that chews people up, spits them out, and sends them back to camp with their tail between their legs. And nine times out of 10, it's not because they didn't know where to go or how to call. It's because their body wasn't ready for the mountains. That's the reality of elk country. The mountains don't care how badly you want the elk. They don't care how nice your gear is or how tuned your bow shoots. Every step in elk country is earned. You're going to climb thousands of vertical feet, drop into drainages that feel bottomless, and crawl out of them with a pack that suddenly weighs twice what it did on the way in. I've guided hunted with and trained a lot of people over the years. And I can tell you the number one factor that separates hunters who last from hunters who quit is physical preparation. If your body breaks down, the hunt's over. Plain and simple. Forget the idea that elk hunting is just about cardio as well. Yes, you're going to be sucking wind at 9,000 ft, but what will break you faster than anything is a lack of strength. Your legs, you've got to be powerful. Enduring legs that climb steep ridges descend with control and carry weight. Squats, lunges, step-ups, and loaded hikes should be staples in your training. Your core, a strong core keeps you balanced under a heavy pack. planks, carries, rotational work. Can't skip any of it. Upper body, while not as critical as legs or core, pulling, pushing, and carrying strength all help when you're maneuvering gear, shouldering a pack, or quartering an elk. Think of strength as armor. The stronger you are, the more resilient you become against fatigue and injury. Let's talk about cardio and endurance. It's not something to downplay, right? I'm not trying to downplay the cardio. Elk Country demands lungs. If you can't climb 1,000 vertical feet without stopping every 50 yards, you're going to struggle to keep up with the hunt. But here's the nuance. It's not just about being able to jog three miles on flat ground. You need mountain endurance, the ability to hike steep, uneven terrain with weight for hours, day after day. That means training like you hunt. Hike hills with a weighted pack. Mix long, steady efforts with short, highintensity climbs. Train your lungs and legs to handle both sustained effort and sudden bursts. The goal isn't to look like an endurance athlete. The goal is to move efficiently and recover quickly in the mountains. And you've got to train with a pack. This is the piece that a lot of new hunters miss. They train in the gym, they run on the road, but they never practice under a loaded pack. Then the first time they throw 60 to 90 pounds of meat on their back, their body goes into shock. Don't make that mistake. Train with your pack before season. Load it with sandbags, water jugs, or even just gear. And hit local trails. Learn how it rides, where it rubs, and how your body reacts to weight. By the time you put quarters in that pack, it should feel familiar, not like a new kind of torture. Mental toughness through the physical prep is one of the secret underlying components of the training. It's something that I've learned after decades in this game. It's that physical preparation isn't just about muscles and lungs. It's about what happens in your mental toughness while you're doing those things. When you've spent months training hard, pushing yourself through workouts you don't feel like doing, grinding through weighted hikes in the rain, you build a bank of mental resilience. On the mountain, when things get tough, you'll draw from that bank. You'll know you've done hard things before, and you'll trust yourself to do them again. Hunters who skip this step often hit a wall, not because their body can't, but because their mind hasn't been tested ahead of time. Physical prep gives you the confidence, and confidence is what cures you when doubt creeps in. I'll never forget one pack out early in my hunting years. I wasn't nearly as prepared as I should have been. The bull was down deep in a canyon, several miles back in, and the climb out felt endless. Every step was pain, and honestly, I wasn't sure I was going to make it. I learned the hard way that my lack of conditioning wasn't just uncomfortable, it was dangerous. Fast forward to later years when I built training into my life year round. Same kind of packout, even heavier, but the difference was night and day. My body could handle it. My mind was sharper. Instead of misery, it became a challenge I could take pride in. That's the power of preparation. So, here are some practical steps for beginners. If you're wondering where to start, here's a simple plan. Strength training at least two to three times a week focused on squats, lunges, step-ups, deadlifts, core work. Building durable legs and strong trunk. Cardio training again a minimum of two to three times a week. Mix in long hikes, hill sprints, stair climbs, interval work. Train both slow grind and explosive effort. Pack training all the time. As many chances as you get, right? It's just out hiking around. What is the big deal? Get out there and do it. Load your pack and hike. Start light, build up gradually, and consistency is what matters here. Don't crash train two months before season. Make this a lifestyle. Elk hunting is too demanding for shortcuts. You can buy gear, study maps, and learn calling strategies, but if your body quits on you, none of it matters. Elk hunting rewards the prepared, and physical preparation is the foundation that supports everything else. When you're gasping for air at 10,000 feet, chasing bugles at dawn, or grinding out a heavy packout, your training will be the one thing that keeps you moving forward. Don't skip it. Don't cut corners. Start now. Because in Elk Country, you don't rise to the occasion. You fall to the level of your preparation. In chapter six, we're going to talk real quick about a gear system that works. And here's a truth that a lot of new elk hunters need to hear. Gear doesn't kill elk. Hunters do. And your preparation does. But gear absolutely does matter. It's the difference between staying in the mountains when things get tough or tapping out early because you're wet, blistered, or overloaded. [snorts] The problem is too many beginners think gear is the whole equation. They fall into what I call gear fever, buying every gadget and camo pattern on the market, then still failing in the field because they don't know how to use half of it. What you need isn't a giant collection of stuff. You need a gear system. The essentials that work together keep you in the hunt and support the mission. Boots are your foundation. If you only take one piece of advice from this chapter, let it be this. Invest in quality boots that you've tried on ahead of time. Your feet are your lifeline in elk country. You'll put more miles on them than you think possible through terrain that chews up cheap footwear and leaves you crippled with blisters. The wrong boots will ruin your hunt faster than anything else. You want to look for quality leather, synthetic boots designed for mountain hunting, right? Not back east, not treeand hunting. Proper ankle support for steep climbs and sidehilling. A sole with grip that can handle loose rock, mud, and deadfall. And most importantly, break them in ahead of time. Don't wait until the hunt to put miles on them. A $400 pair of boots is cheap compared to a $2,000 hunt lost because you couldn't walk. Your pack is your meat hauler, not just your backpack. Every elk hunter dreams of the pack out, but very few are actually ready for it. You're not just carrying gear. You're carrying hundreds of pounds of meat, antlers, and hide. If you're successful, if your pack isn't up to that job, you are in for a nightmare. Your pack should fit you properly. Adjustable torso length, solid hip belt, snug shoulder straps, and load liters are a must. You've got to haul heavy loads comfortably. A good frame that keeps weight on your hips and not pulling down in your shoulders. You've got it's got to be able to expand and compress enough space for multi-day hunts, but able to cinch down for day trips. It's got to be extremely durable. Cheap packs break under elk quarters. We've seen it a lot. Don't gamble there. Test it. Train with it. Learn how to load it. A good pack makes the hardest part of elk hunting survivable. Your clothing layers over fashion. Here's another place beginners waste money. Chasing the latest camo patterns. Elk don't care if your jacket is last year's model or if you spent $800 on the newest pattern. What matters is layering. Your clothing system should do three things. Keep you dry. Wet equals cold. Cold equals misery. Invest in a good waterproof outer shell. It's got to keep you warm. Marino or synthetic base layers. mid- layer insulation like a puffier fleece and outer shell. It's got to be able to adapt to conditions. Elk hunting can swing from 80° in the afternoon to 20° before sunrise. Layers let you adjust quickly. Buy quality where it counts, but don't get caught up in the fashion game. Function beats flash. Your weapon is an extension of your body. Whether you hunt with a bow or a rifle, your weapon is the most personal piece of gear you'll carry. It has to become an extension of you. So many guys don't do what's necessary with those weapons ahead of time. Bow hunters, you got to pick a bow you can draw smoothly under stress, not just one that maxes out poundage. Shoot broadheads that fly like your field points. If you're having to do a ton of re-calibrating and tuning, you're not set up properly, right? Practice in every position, kneeling, sitting, uphill, downhill. Rifle hunters, invest in a rifle that shoots consistently and a scope that holds zero and has a big enough field of view for you to find animals, not just one with a huge bell and a and an extreme magnification. Practice at real hunting distances, 200, 300, 400 yards if you're comfortable and know your limits. All the gear in the world won't save you if you can't make the shot when it counts. Your kill kit, one of the most overlooked essentials. A lot of new hunters over obsess about optics, camo, calls, and forget the one kit that actually matters when success comes. Your kill kit, at minimum, it should include sharp, reliable knife and replaceable blades. Extra blades and sharpeners, right? If you need them, game bags, quality, breathable, reusable. Nitrial gloves to keep things a little cleaner, right? Keep your fingers and hands cleaner, less slippage. paracorder rope, tying quarters up if you need to for for stability if you're alone or getting them up in a tree if you need to cool them out and a headlamp with fresh batteries. All mandatory parts when you're standing over your first elk, heart pounding, daylight fading, that kit is what lets you break that animal down and protect your meat. So, don't cut corners there. Your optics, nice to have, not always need to have, right? Good binoculars and a spotting scope can be a huge asset, especially for rifle hunters covering big country. But if your budget is tight, don't feel like you need to drop two grand on glass right away. A solid pair of mid-tier binoculars and knowing how to use them well will take you a long way. And don't try to buy your way out of inexperience. Here's something important to understand for all of us, right? No piece of gear will make up for lack of knowledge or toughness. I've watched guys show up in the most expensive camo with the latest rangefinder, $3,000 packs filled with all the latest and greatest equipment, bows tuned to perfection, and they still fail because they didn't put in the work ahead of time. I've also watched hunters in hand-me-down gear, secondhand rifles and bows, and boots held together with duct tape punch tags because they knew elk, respected the wind, kept grinding, they were prepared for the hunt. Gear is a tool. Tools don't win hunts, hunters do. So, to recap this section, here are a few practical steps for beginners. Prioritize boots. Build a clothing system with layers, not just camo. Dial in your weapon, no matter what it is. Make it second nature. Prepare a kill kit. Don't leave home without it. Add the extras as you learn. The optics, the gadgets, and the luxuries can wait. The wrong gear won't just make you uncomfortable. It'll end your hunt. blisters, wet clothing, and a broken pack strap or a dead headlamp can be the difference between tagging an elk out and heading home and in safety and comfort or a miserable ending to all of it. But the right system, it keeps you in the mountains longer, hunting harder and ready when the opportunity finally comes. Because in elk hunting, success doesn't go to the guy with the most gear. It goes to the one whose gear keeps him in the game when others quit. In chapter 7, we're going to talk about wind and thermals because it's one of the most important laws out there. It's the invisible enemy. You can have the best tag in the state. You can have the finest gear money can buy and you can have all the preparation in the world, but a bull screaming his head off at 200 yards away and the wind isn't right, you might as well pack it up and head back to camp. That's how important this chapter is. The number one thing that ruins more elk hunts than anything else, not bears, not wolves, not other hunters, it's the wind. Elk [snorts] live and die by their nose. Their sense of smell is their number one defense, far sharper than their eyes or ears. A bull might ignore a twig snapping or a faint silhouette in the shadows. But if he catches one whiff of human scent, he's gone and he's taken the whole herd with him. I've seen bulls bugling hard, raking trees, worked up into a frenzy, and the second my wind shifted, silence, gone. No sound, no tracks, no second chance. If you don't learn to understand and respect wind, you'll never consistently kill elk. Thermals are the mountains breath. And you've got to understand these. Here's where it gets tricky. Wind in the mountains doesn't act like wind on the prairie. It doesn't just blow in one direction. It changes constantly with the terrain, the weather, and the sun. In the morning, as the sun warms the slope and the air on that slope, the thermals are going to start to rise, right? But not until that happens. Before that happens, you're going to have sinking thermals, right? And so, you've got to understand that [snorts] happening in the morning. Cool air dropping. Once it starts to warm, then you've got rising thermals the rest of the day until evening. And as the sun sets and the air cools, thermals start to fall again and air is going to be moving downhill. midday, as long as there's sun on the slope, thermals are going to be rising. But if there's shade, if there's cloud cover, they can swirl unpredictably, especially in steep drainages, right? And this is why you'll hear seasoned, experienced hunters say, "Hun up first thing in the morning, right, as the thermals are coming down and down later in the day as the thermals are rising." It's not just a saying. It's the thermals in action. Now, let's add another layer. Terrain. Mountains create micro winds that don't always match the general thermal pattern. Side drainages, air can get sucked down smaller canyons, even while the main slope is pulling air uphill. Benches and saddles, those spots often swirl. One moment the wind is in your face, the next it's at your back. Timber versus open slopes, thick timber cools slower or faster than open ground, shifting air movement unexpectedly. This is why elk country feels like it has a mind of its own. You can't just assume. You have to test it constantly with your wind puffer. Now, carrying a wind checker like fine powder or bottles or even milkweed seeds, it's unnegotiable. You can't guess. You have to be checking constantly. Plan your approach. Don't just run toward a bugle. Circle, drop, or climb to keep the wind in your favor. Set up with wind in mind. Whether calling or still hunting, always ask, "If elk come from that direction, where will my scent go?" You got to be patient. Sometimes the wind just isn't right. Wait it out. A bull that doesn't know you're there today might still be there tomorrow. Blow him out with bad wind and he's gone. I've had hunts where everything lined up perfectly. Fresh sign talking the setup just right and then a small swirl gave me away and it was over. One hunt that sticks with me was a bull I'd worked all morning. He was bugling hard, pushing cows, and I managed to sneak within 70 yards. I thought I had him until the wind shifted just slightly. He caught it, rounded up his cows, and trotted a mile into the next drainage and never saw him again that season. On the flip side, the bulls I've killed almost always came from setups where the wind was bulletproof. I circled further than I wanted, climbed higher than I thought I needed, and sometimes waited hours until thermal stabilized. The difference between success and failure was discipline with the wind. Now, here's the mistake a lot of new hunters make. They think they can sneak in just a little closer with the wind kind of in their favor. Or they figure the bull is so fired up he won't care and that's wrong. Elk always care. If the wind isn't right, they're going to figure it out. And when they know, it's over. Patience here pays more dividends than any other skill you're going to develop. So, here are some practical skills to build your wind awareness. Practice at home. When you're hiking or training, carry a wind checker. Notice how air moves differently in valleys, ridges, or open versus wooded areas. Check constantly in the field. Don't just check once. Elk can change position and so can the wind. Learn from mistakes. When elk bust, ask yourself, "What did the wind just do?" Build that awareness every time. You can outwalk fatigue. You can outlearn mistakes. You can't beat an elk's nose. The only way to consistently stay in the game is to make wind your ally instead of your enemy. When you master thermals and micro winds, the entire mountain is going to open up for you. Suddenly, stocks that used to fail are going to start working. Setups that felt like a gamble are going to start producing encounters, and you're going to stop hoping and start hunting with more intention because in elk hunting, the invisible enemy isn't luck, it's the wind. Learn it, respect it, and you're going to already be ahead of most hunters in the elk woods. In chapter 8, we're going to talk about the calling learning curve. Ask a new hunter what excites them most about September and nine times out of ten they're going to say the bugles. And who can blame them? Hearing a bull rip a bugle across a canyon is one of the most electrifying moments you can experience in the mountains. It makes your hair stand up. It makes your heart race. It's what most people picture when they dream of elk hunting. But here's the truth. Calling is a double-edged sword. Done right, it brings elk into bow range and creates encounters you'll never forget. done wrong. It educates elk, blows up drainages, and leaves you wondering why the woods went silent. For beginners, the key is this. Start simple. A lot of new hunters believe if they just learn the right sequence, the magic combination of bugles and cow calls, bulls are going to come charging in every time, and that's not how it works. Elk are wild animals with their own moods, pressures, and personalities. Some days they're going to be vocal and aggressive. Other days, they're going to be silent and cautious. No two bulls respond the exact same way. And no two herds are alike. And that's why calling isn't about memorizing a script. It's about understanding the context and applying the right call at the right time. You've got to start with the cow call. If you're brand new, your best tool is the cow call. Cow sounds are less threatening than bugles. They're social, common, and generally safe to throw out in most situations. Even pressured elk are used to hearing cows. A simple muse says, "I'm here." A few soft chirps can calm a herd. Drawn out lost cow calls can sometimes pull curious elk in. Practice with a diaphragm, external read, or even a push button call if that's where you need to start. Don't worry about sounding perfect. Real elk don't always sound perfect either. What matters is tone, rhythm, and not overusing it. Once you're comfortable with a cow call, the next step is learning the location bugle. This isn't the chest thumping, challenging everything bugle you hear in hunting videos. It's a simple, long, high-pitched note bulls use to say, "Hey, I'm over here." The location bugle is one of the best tools for finding elk, especially in the mornings and evenings. Use it sparingly, and when you get a response, shut up and move. The goal isn't to call the bull in from where you locate him, unless he's, you know, less than 100 yards. It's to pinpoint his location and go after him. The beginner's biggest downfall, overalling. They hear a bugle and they think, "If I bugle back every 30 seconds, he's going to come charging in." Or they cow call non-stop on the hike in thinking they're being natural. What they're really doing is screaming, "I'm a human with a call to every elk that's within earshot because elk don't move and and talk like that." Remember, elk live in herds. Herds make noise, but they don't sound like a broken record. They move. They pause. They interact naturally. Calling should be part of a conversation, not a lecture. And you got to be aware of situational calling. So, here's how I keep it simple for beginners. Scouting and locating. Use a location bugle to find out or cow calls. Don't keep hammering. Move to cut the distance. Close range with cows. Use soft cow calls to blend in and calm them and make bulls circle and check you out. Hot rod action with a fired up bull. Once you're experienced, you can add challenge bugles in and raking. But for now, observe less is more, right? I'll I'll admit to it. I used to call way too much. I'd bugle at every drainage, cow call while walking, and basically advertise my presence like a used car salesman, and I blew more elk than I cared to count. Over time, I learned that silence often kills more bulls than sound. I can't tell you how many times I've been sitting quietly just watching and waiting when elk walked right into me because they had no idea I was there. The best callers I know aren't the loudest. They're the most disciplined. So, you got to work on building your calling skills up a little at a time. Calling [snorts] is a skill just like shooting your bow or rifle. It takes a lot of practice. Start now, not in September. Daily reps. Spend 5 to 10 minutes a day minimum practicing. Build that muscle memory. Listen to real elk talk. Watch videos. Listen to recordings and mimic their cadence. Record yourself. What feels right in your mouth might not sound right to your ears once you hear it played back to you. Learn restraint. Sometimes the best call is no call. Calling is what makes elk hunting so thrilling in September, right? It's interactive. It's a conversation with a wild animal. But it's also the most abused skill in the elkwoods. If you learn discipline early, you'll set yourself apart from a lot of beginners. So, start simple, master the cow sounds, use location bugle strategically, and above all else, call less than you think you should. Because calling isn't about showing off what you can do. It's about saying just enough to make elk believe you belong. In chapter [snorts] nine, I want to talk to you about mental toughness in the field. One of the most important chapters we're going to talk about here. If there's one truth I wish every new elk hunter understood before their first season, it's this. Elk hunting is a mental game as much as it is a physical one. Yes, you need to be in shape. Yes, you need solid gear. Yes, you need to learn elk behavior. But when the days stretch long, the miles add up, and the elk stay quiet. The only thing that will keep you moving forward is what's between your ears. Elk hunting will humble you. I don't care who you are. The mountains will test you. There will be mornings when your alarm goes off and every muscle in your body screams to stay in the bag. There will be days when you cover 12 miles and don't see a single elk track. There will be encounters where you do everything right and still the bull hangs up at 80 yards and vanishes. And there will be moments when you ask yourself, "Why am I out here? Maybe I'll come back later with the rifle." [snorts] That's not weakness. That's normal. I've hunted elk for more than 34 years, and I still have hunts where the mountains make me feel like a rookie. The question isn't whether elk hunting will humble you, it's whether you'll keep going when it does. Let's talk about comfort versus discomfort. We live in a world that teaches us to avoid discomfort. But elk hunting requires the opposite. You have to embrace it. Cold mornings, crawling out of a warm bag at 4:00 a.m. into freezing air. Long climbs, legs burning, lungs on fire, elevation grinding you down, frustration, days of silence, blown stocks, and close calls that don't pan out. If you expect elk hunting to be comfortable, you're going to be disappointed. If you accept that discomfort is part of the deal, you're going to thrive. Mental toughness isn't about pretending it's easy. It's about choosing to keep going when it's not and expecting that it's not going to be. Remember back in chapter one when we talked about defining your why. This is where it pays off. When you're worn out, frustrated, and tempted to quit, your why is the anchor that holds you in place. If your why is shallow, you're going to fold. If it's deep, you'll dig in. For me, it's about challenge and growth. When the mountains break me down or remind myself that this is exactly why I came to test myself, to get better, to learn, that reframing turns misery into meaning. So, here are some practical ways to build and protect your mental toughness. Break the hunt into chunks. Don't think about 10 days in the back country. Think about making it to the next ridge, the next glassing knob, the next day. Small winds keep you moving. Control what you can. You can't control elk behavior, weather, or pressure. You can control your effort, your attitude, and your preparation. You've got to find positives daily. Even if you don't see an elk, celebrate something. You learned a drainage, improved your glassing, or built endurance. Stay flexible. Rigid plans break under pressure. Adaptation keeps you in the game. Lean on your community. If you're struggling, remember others have been there. A hunting partner or even journaling your experience can reset your mindset. I've been on haunts where it felt like the mountains had me beat. One in particular, day after day of silence, hot weather, no sign of elk, every step felt pointless. I remember sitting under a tree, pack off, bow on the ground, ready to call it in. The only reason I didn't was because I promised myself that I wouldn't leave until the last day was over. That simple mental rule, no quitting until the hunt's done, carried me through. And wouldn't you know it, on day nine, I finally got into elk and had one of the best encounters of my life. If id left early, I'd have missed it. That's the lesson. The breakthrough often comes right after the breakdown. You don't have to wait until September comes to build this mental toughness. Every workout, every long hike, every time you choose discipline over comfort, you're training your mind as much as your body. Push yourself when you want to quit. Train in bad weather. Take the long way instead of the easy way. All of it adds up. By the time you've hit the mountains, you've already proven to yourself that you don't fold when things get hard. Elk hunting isn't just about finding elk. It's about lasting long enough to find elk. And that requires grit. The hunters who succeed aren't the ones with the fanciest gear or the deepest pockets. They're the ones who simply don't quit. They put in the time. They stay in the game day after day, mile after mile, setback after setback. If you want to begin your elk hunting journey the right way, commit now to being that hunter, accept that it will be hard. Expect to get knocked down and promise yourself you'll get back up every single time because in the elk woods, success belongs to the relentless. In chapter 10, we're going to talk about just starting because experience is the teacher. We've covered a lot of ground in this episode from defining your why to learning elk behavior, picking a state, scouting and preparing your body, dialing in your gear, mastering when starting with calling and building mental toughness. But at the end of the day, all this boils down to one truth. The best way to learn elk hunting is to go elk hunting. You can read all the articles, binge all the YouTube videos, and listen to every podcast out there. And while that knowledge helps, it is never going to replace the lessons you'll learn in the field. Don't [snorts] wait for perfect. Too many beginners hold themselves back by waiting. They want the perfect tag, the perfect gear, the perfect level of fitness before they head out. But here's the reality. Elk hunting will never be perfect. The conditions will always be tough. The terrain always unforgiving. And your preparation will always feel like it could have been better. If you wait until everything lines up, you'll never go. And every year you don't go is a year you lose the most valuable teacher of all, and that's experience. You're going to make mistakes. That's not optional. It's guaranteed. You're going to blow stocks. You're going to call at the wrong times. You're going to misread the wind. You're going to set up in the wrong spot. But every one of those mistakes is tuition. You're paying in time, sweat, and frustration. And in return, you're learning lessons no book or podcast could ever teach you. The elkwoods have a way of engraving those lessons deep. Once you've blown a herd with bad wind, you're never going to forget to check your thermals again. Once you've packed a bull out of a hell hole, you're never going to forget to consider packout routes during scouting. Those hard-earned lessons are what eventually turn beginners into killers. Here's what usually happens. Your first year, you're just trying to survive and not get lost. The second year, you start recognizing patterns. By the third or fourth year, you've got enough encounters under your belt that you stop doubting whether you belong. That confidence doesn't come from watching someone else. It comes from being there year after year stacking small wins until one day you realize, you know what, you're doing it. My experience is to learn by doing. I'll be honest, my first seasons were rough. I failed more than I succeeded. I came home empty-handed plenty of times, but each hunt taught me something. Over time, those lessons stacked up. And now after decades in the elk woods, I can look back and see how those failures were the foundation of every success that came later. [snorts] I wouldn't trade those early struggles for anything because I know they built the hunter I am today. And here's the thing, whether or not you kill an elk your first season, your second season, your third season, the experience itself will change you. You're going to climb mountains you never thought possible. You're going to hear bugles echo through dark timber and feel your chest shake from the sound. You're going to watch sunrises from ridges that most people will never see in their lifetime. And even if you go home empty, you're going to go home richer with stories, lessons, and a fire to get back out there. Action steps are to just start. So, here's my challenge to you. Pick a state. Don't overthink it. Buy the tag and commit to the hunt. Train your body. Start now, not later. Pack your bag. Use what you have and improve as you go. Go hunt. Step into the elk woods and let experience do the teaching. That's it. That's the formula. You can't become an elk hunter by wishing. You can only become one by hunting elk. The lessons will come. The success will come, but only if you start. Because in the elk hunting woods, there's no substitute for time in the mountains. The elk themselves are the best teachers you'll ever have. - How To Hunt Alone With Remi Warren: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] one of my favorite ways to hunt is hunting alone I do a lot of backcountry hunts alone and the reason that I like hunting alone is there's a type of instantaneous decision making that happens you don't have to talk to anyone you don't have to discuss a plan you just do whatever feels natural whatever feels right starting out solo hunting just take day trips take it easy go on a small hunt by yourself and get used to the fact of being alone and relying on your skills from there you can kind of graduate into longer trips maybe not necessarily Backcountry trips but short weeking getaways where you're hunting by yourself then take it a step further pack up your things and try a Backcountry hunt the more you graduate the more you do it the more comfortable you're going to be alone so it's the best way in my mind to advance your hunting skills when you're hunting alone there's a few problems that you don't run into when you're hunting with other people one of them is there isn't as many people to help glass and to be looking around often times when I'm hunting by myself I kind of feel like there's too much country for just me to look over so what I do is I end up looking here and looking there and not picking one spot and glassing it diligently so something you think about if you decide to go solo hunting or hunting by yourself just accept the fact that there may be too much country to Glass so you're better off picking a spot and glassing it and working that area diligently and trying to be all places at once if you're doing a backpack hunt alone you run into the problem that you're the the only person there to carry your gear so you really need to refine your gear setup and get a lightweight system that works for you something that you can manage and carry and still possibly be able to pack out an animal with most your gear for solo backpack hunts I like to find the lightest gear possible I also like to tweak my setup after every trip if I had something in my pack that I didn't use maybe next time I won't bring it I like to really look at my gear and think of multiple uses for each piece of equipment the more uses I have for something the more it deserves a spot in my pack another thing is if it feels lonely or you're out there and things aren't going right at night build a fire fires for some reason are extremely comforting when you're alone another thing is you just got to keep a positive mental attitude if things don't go your way you just got to shrug it off because you're by yourself you don't have anyone to communicate the struggles of the day with or laugh things off with so you just got to take things with a grain of salt also if you run into some kind of adversity think it out stay calm and Don't Panic it's easy to panic when you're alone because you don't have anyone else to help you so you got to really assess the situation rely on your skills and know that you have the ability to get yourself out of whatever you got into

  Top Comments:
  1. @coloradobackpackhunter2046 [75 likes]: Don't forget to tell someone where you are heading and what your general plan will be. You never know what can happen.
  2. @robertjensen1048 [19 likes]: It's OK to hunt solo, but be sure to think it out.
You have to ask yourself, every day, several times a day these questions:  "If I was severely injured or lost where I am right now, what would happen? Would anyone know where I am?  Would anyone be able to find me?  Would I be able to contact emergency help?   Am i able to administer myself first aid until help arrives?  Do I have enough water?"
Develop good habits. Every time you leave a paved road, pause and text your coordinates to your emergency contact.   Every time you start off on foot hiking or hunting, do the same.   When you arrive back at your car or campsite or hotel safely, text your contact again.  Carry an emergency GPS  Two Way Satellite Communicator, or even a satellite phone if you can afford it. Always have the ability to either send or tell someone your GPS coordinates in an emergency.      Keep track of the path you take when hiking.  Smartphones have apps that will show your trail.  Carry spare battery charger for your phone.   Hiking after dark is dumb: don't do it.    Carry a snakebite venom sucker kit: emergency help will not reach you in time to save you.
   There are some huge upsides to solo hunting.  Peace and quiet, you hunt at your own pace and schedule, you don't have to coordinate your hunt dates with another's schedule, and more. Furthermore, it is easier to focus on the hunt and spot animals, when you don't have to focus on your partner's chatter.
  3. @stevewest8420 [10 likes]: Always take a side arm for self defense.
  4. @skywalker844 [6 likes]: Dont hunt with someone who outta shape
  5. @tarasb.ukrainian1947 [5 likes]: should mention a PLB as mandatory essential gear for hunting alone
  6. @ericb.4358 [4 likes]: Good video and important for first time backpack hunters, especially on how to ease into it.
Having backpacked for decades and done weekend backpack deer hunts in Pennsylvania I thought I was ready when I moved to Nevada in 2004. NOPE! All my gear was too heavy. I realized this when I joined Backpacking Light as a  life member. I got SCHOOLED in UL backpacking. So gradually I replaced nearly every piece of my gear including buying a 6.5 PRC Browning X-Bolt Pro (6 lbs. 3 oz.)and a Bushnell LRTS 4.5 - 18 x 44 scope with illuminated G3 reticle.
Now, with a light Tarptent Moment DW solo tent, Trail Designs titanium wood or ESBIT fuel tablet stove, 20 F. Western Mountaineering Megalite factory overstuffed sleeping bag, REI FLASH Insulated All Season mattress., etc. etc. I have cut my pack weight drastically.
TIPS: 
1. Use a KIFARU Gun Bearer to comfortably carry your rifle with a backpack and have it ready instantly.
2. Use hiking poles WITH the new Quick stiX adaptors that clamp just below your handles and turn your hiking poles into shooting sticks. If you do not backpack with hiking poles your should be. Your knees will thank you in later years.
3. Never, EVER buy a tent that requires you to set up the inner tent & poles Before the fly. In a storm the inner tent will be soaked by the time you get the fly on. Same with taking it down in a storm. Be sure you inner tent can be attached to the fly while being packed.

  Top Comments:
  1. @sdpy5310 [7 likes]: In my early 20's I learned an invaluable lesson: comfort is critical. I spent thousands of hours mountaineering on a budget: cheap clothes, and cheap equipment. It got me to many summits, and I didn't give a second thought to upgrading my sub-par gear for the premium "gucci" lines. That was until on a 2 day mountaineering trip I pushed myself to a physical breaking point: cold weather clothes were too bulky and holding moisture, my backpack was chafing my shoulders, and my boots were creating terrible pressure points that resulted in open wounds over the duration of the trip. During the descent I couldn't appreciate the beautiful vistas, couldn't focus on the task at hand, all I could think about was getting down as quickly as possible and falling into my tent for a good nights rest. On part of the descent down a glacial chute my mind was focusing so much on the discomfort and not on getting sturdy footing that I broke out of a pigeon hole and went a$$ over tea kettle falling nearly 100 yards before piling up in a small band of rocks that separated me from a 1km vertical drop off the mountain. 

While i've given up mountaineering, I haven't forgotten the lesson. If you have the means to afford premium (not necessarily meaning the most expensive) equipment, your time in the field will be more enjoyable, comfortable, and most importantly safer. When it comes down to the wire and you have an animal in your crosshairs you don't want to remember that moment by all the negatives that could have been avoided resulting in a missed or injured animal. Rather make sure your kit and clothes support your activity and give you the upperhand in those intense moments that you will look back on in the future. 

With all this said, don't be a softie and expect every adventure to go perfectly. It won't no matter how much money you spend on gear. Thrive in the challenging moments and learn from adversity. But don't stack the deck against yourself.
