- What it's Like to be Every Type of Hunter in HunterXHunter: Kind: captions Language: en Hunter Hunter has one of the most insane and detailed worlds in all of anime, and a huge part of that is the fact that being a hunter means something completely different depending on who you ask. One guy is out here risking his life chasing down serial killers. Another one's traveling the globe just to eat weird food, and somehow they both have the same license. The world of hunters is so stupidly vast and varied that covering every type could take a whole day, but that's exactly what we're doing today because you deserve to know what your life would actually look like if you woke up one day and decided to become one. By the end of this, you either want to sign up for the Hunter Exam or thank every god you know that you don't have to. Let's start with Rookie Hunters. You passed the Hunter Exam, grabbed that shiny license, survived the secret Nen crash course, and boom, you're officially in. But until you pick a specialization, you're basically in a Hunter limbo, floating around without a clear direction. You can use Nen, you can take on jobs, and you got insane freedom, but you're still figuring out what kind of Hunter you want to be. Three out of the four main characters are still rookies, and that's because none of them have locked in a specialization yet. Gon wants to figure out why his dad chose the Hunter life over raising him. Leorio needs the license money to pay for medical school and eventually open a free clinic for people who can't afford treatment. And Killua just thought the whole thing sounded fun. So for them, the Hunter title is more like a golden key than a final destination. They're not rushing to become crime trackers or treasure experts because that was never the main objective. If you're in the same boat, you're probably using your license to chase your original dream first. That's the Rookie life. You jump from treasure hunting to arena battles to escort missions because you haven't committed yet. Blacklist Hunters. Blacklist Hunters are essentially the bounty hunters of the Hunter world, and their whole life revolves around tracking down and capturing dangerous wanted criminals, specifically the kind with Nen abilities and enough to make most people run the other direction. These are high-combat, high-stake hunters who work largely on their own and regularly go up against people who would genuinely love to eliminate them. Kurapika is the poster boy for this type, and his entire career as a blacklist hunter is built around one singular terrifying goal: hunting down every member of the Phantom Troupe, the notorious gang of murderous thieves who massacred his entire clan to steal the Kurta's scarlet eyes and sell them on the black market. Another blacklist hunter, Lippo, built his reputation by capturing some seriously notorious criminals, including Jonas the Dissector, the most mass wanted murderer in Zaban City, and that reputation was strong enough to land him an examiner job for the 287th Hunter Exam. And then there's Sequid, who decided the smartest career move was leading 100 henchmen to capture the Zoldyck family, only to get completely destroyed by a 10-year-old apprentice butler. As a blacklist hunter, you're constantly gathering intel on criminals, preparing for confrontations with Nen users who have had years to develop their abilities, and operating in environments where a single mistake can cost you your life. The pay and prestige are solid, and you get the satisfaction of doing work that actually matters to society, but you're always one bad mission away from a really unpleasant end. And the criminals you're hunting are usually hunting you right back. Beast Hunters. If you prefer a career out of chasing claws, fangs, and anything that leaves suspicious footprints in the mud, then becoming a beast hunter would be right up your alley. Beast hunters live for the thrill of tracking and studying rare and dangerous animals, and the job description can go in wildly different directions depending on which subcategory you fall under. Knuckle Bine is the most well-known beast hunter in the series, and while his exact reason for choosing this path is described as long and unhappy, what we do know is that the man has fought over 5,000 battles in his career. Then there's Pokkle, a hunter of fantastic beasts whose whole deal is venturing into uncharted territories to document animals that science hasn't officially recognized yet. And Shoot McMahon covers the unidentified beast hunter angle, cryptids, creatures with questioned existence, paranormal animals, basically everything that sounds made up until it tries to eat you. Pokkle's story is honestly one of the most tragic in the series and it perfectly captures both the wonder and the danger of being a beast hunter. He heads to the Neo Green Life Autonomous Region, a place where nature is completely untouched and technology is forbidden because it's a perfect hunting ground for rare undocumented creatures. What starts as an exciting expedition turns into a nightmare when his team runs directly into the Chimera Ant invasion. His entire team gets killed and devoured. Pokkle gets captured and then Neferpitou lobotomizes him to extract information about Nen from his brain before feeding him to the queen. It's a genuinely awful fate for someone who is just out there trying to catalog some weird animals. As a beast hunter, your best days involve discovering some insane creature no one's ever documented before or a species that straight-up shouldn't exist if biology is playing by the rules. But honestly, the reality is that the further you go looking for rare creatures, the more likely it is that those creatures or whatever else lives in those territories would love to have you for lunch. Gourmet hunters. Gourmet hunters dedicate their entire careers to food, but not the kind you grab from a street stall. They travel the world searching for rare ingredients that only grow in deadly swamps, frozen mountains, or places normal people avoid on purpose. They're experts in culinary culture, cooking techniques, and flavor balance and their palates are so refined that a slightly overcooked dish feels like a personal attack. At the same time, they need survival skills because the best ingredients are usually guarded by something with teeth. The most famous example is Menchi, a single-star gourmet hunter and widely considered the best in the world. Her achievements in culinary culture earn her a star, and her reputation lends her a spot as an examiner in the 287th Hunter Exam alongside Buhara. On the higher end, Linne Or d'Oeuvre holds a double-star status and once joins Isaac Netero and Zigg Zoldyck on a Dark Continent expedition, which tells you everything you need to know about how seriously accomplished she is. If you're a gourmet hunter, your day might start with trekking through a jungle to harvest a rare fruit that ripens once a year, then shift into chasing down some ridiculously dangerous wild animal for its premium meat while it's fully committed to making you the main course instead. After that, you head back to your kitchen and experiment with new recipes using ingredients you almost died getting. You gain status, connections, and the kind of lifestyle where people fly you out just to taste your cooking. You travel constantly, create world-class dishes, and build a name that can earn you stars within the Association. The pressure is intense though. Your standards are sky-high, and one bad dish can damage your reputation. Ruins Hunters. Ruins Hunters dedicate their lives to digging up the past and bringing it back to life. They uncover lost cities, restore ancient structures, and preserve historical sites so entire civilizations don't get erased by time. This job takes brains, patience, funding, and enough combat skill to survive whatever traps or guardians are still lurking underground. You're a part archaeologist, part explorer, and part problem-solver with a shovel and Nen. The biggest name in this field is Ging Freecss. After earning his license, he pours his time and fortune into excavating and restoring ruins across the world. He funds preservation projects so the public can actually see these ancient sites instead of letting them crumble. His work becomes the gold standard of archaeological management, and he ends up ridiculously wealthy and influential. Ging basically shows you what life as a ruins hunter looks like. Your daily routine might involve mapping out a buried city, negotiating with local governments for excavation rights, and carefully restoring murals that are centuries old. Your life involves a lot of traveling to places that civilizations left behind, piecing together history from whatever fragments survived, and making sure the world doesn't lose those stories forever. The challenge is that places civilization left behind often means places that are actively hostile to visitors. Extreme climates, unstable terrain, and a general sense that nature doesn't really want you there. At the same time, funding projects drains your wallet fast and one wrong excavation can destroy priceless artifacts. You gain influence and recognition, but you also accept that uncovering the past means constant travel, unpredictable dangers, and a lot of dirt under your nails. Sea Hunters. Sea Hunters make their living out on open water, chasing down massive sea creatures, or hauling up treasures from places most ships refuse to sail. You need insane stamina, sharp navigation skills, and the kind of combat ability that still works when everything around you is flooded. The ocean doesn't play fair, so you adapt or you sink. A top example is Morel Mackernasey, a single star sea hunter with lungs so ridiculous he can stay underwater longer than anyone has a right to. His apnea skills literally win him fights inside a flooded underground church. He sinks to the bottom, wasted out, then exhales enough carbon dioxide to poison his opponent before swimming through a maze-like structure without taking a breath. Morel's value extends well beyond just breathing, though. When the Zodiacs need someone to navigate the waters between the New Continent's border and the gate on the Dark Continent expedition, Morel personally takes on the navigation. The most elite hunters in the world trust a sea hunter to steer the ship because nobody else knows those waters the way he does. As a sea hunter, your day might involve diving into deep trenches to investigate a giant aquatic beast, then switching to charting dangerous sea routes for powerful clients. The freedom feels incredible and the pay can be huge. At the same time, storms, sea monsters, and oxygen limits are constant threats. Your life revolves around tides, pressure, and staying calm while everything around tries to drown you. Treasure hunters. Treasure hunters spend their lives hunting down rare, valuable items scattered across the world. That can mean ancient artifacts, legendary collectibles, or absurdly rare gems worth entire fortunes. You need sharp instincts, deep knowledge of history and markets, and enough combat skill to survive rival collectors who are more than willing to fight over a shiny object. A stone hunter falls under this category, focusing specifically on precise gems and jewelry. And Biscuit Krueger is the defining example here. A double star stone hunter who is objectively one of the most deceptively dangerous people in the entire series. During the Yorknew auction chaos, she gets hired by Battera to clear Greed Island, but her main goal inside the game is the Blue Planet, a perfectly spherical blue gemstone that reflects light like a living atmosphere. Along the way, she meets Gon and Killua and instantly sees them as unpolished gems waiting to be perfected. She decides to train the two boys to really unlock their potential. After helping them clear the game, she obtains the Blue Planet. If you're a treasure hunter like Biscuit, your day is swinging between high-end auctions and life-threatening expeditions. The money and reputation can skyrocket, especially when you land something as rare as Blue Planet. At the same time, competition is fierce and danger follows valuable objects like a shadow. Disease and virus hunters. Disease and virus hunters are the ones running towards the stuff most people run away from. Plagues, deadly viruses, and disease that would make your average doctor faint. You hunt for cures, research unknown pathogens, and develop medical protocols that can save entire populations. Sharp scientific knowledge, quick problem-solving, and nerves of steel are mandatory because exposure is always a risk, and mistakes can be catastrophic. Cheadle Yorkshire is a triple-star disease hunter, one of the elite few in the Hunter Association who's reached the absolute top. She joins the Zodiacs, becomes the 14th chairwoman of the Hunter Association, and gets assigned to the science team for the Dark Continent Expedition, ensuring the team stays healthy and setting up disease control procedures in one of the most dangerous uncharted environments on Earth. Then, there's Senritsu Norton, a single-star virus hunter and the Association's lead doctor. Her expertise spans diseases, viruses, and even poisons, and she's called on to create manuals for other hunters preparing for the perilous Dark Continent voyage. If you're a disease hunter, your days involve analyzing samples, designing treatments, and preparing for pathogens that mutate in unpredictable ways. You travel to remote, hostile locations, deal with sudden outbreaks, and constantly balance research with real-world danger. Recognition and respect come naturally when your work prevents epidemics, but the pressure is unrelenting, and even a minor miscalculation can have life-or-death consequences. Poison Hunters. Poison Hunters spend their day flirting with danger, handling toxins and venoms most people wouldn't touch with a 10-ft pole. Working with chemicals that can kill or cure on a whim takes grit and a brain that's always two steps ahead. Leading the charge in this deadly field is Gel, a Poison Hunter in the Zodiacs and part of the science team for the Dark Continent Expedition. She masters chemistry, biology, and medicine, turning lethal compounds into tools for survival and research. If you're walking in her shoes, your day involves testing antidotes, analyzing exotic poisons, and making sure the team doesn't accidentally self-destruct, proving that a sharp mind can be far deadlier than a sharp weapon. Botanical Hunters. Some people chase money, some chase fame, but botanical hunters hunt plants that can heal, harm, or completely rewrite what we know about nature. Mastering every leaf, root, and flower in the wild takes sharp instincts and a serious attention to detail. Cluck leads this green-focused mission as a Zodiac and Botanical Hunter. On the Dark Continent Expedition, she's part of the Flora-Fauna team, trekking and documenting unknown plants and animals. Working in her world means dodging venomous surprises, identifying bizarre flora, and cataloging discoveries while making sure nothing sneaks up on you. Out here, the jungle becomes your lab, and every plant could be a headline. Crime Hunters. Crime Hunters are the detectives of the Hunter world, and the job demands a mind that's constantly three steps ahead of everyone else. Their work demands razor-sharp deduction skills, a deep understanding of law, and the ability to manipulate information to get results. Mizaistom Nana gives you a solid look at what life as a Crime Hunter is really like. He is a double-star Crime Hunter and the Zodiac member whose intelligence is next level. He runs a private security company, oversees his team, and can predict outcomes of political moves. On the Dark Continent Expedition, he joins the Intelligence team with Kurapika, Pyon, and Sacho, handling background checks on Hunter Exam candidates, gathering intel on Beyond and Kakin, confirming passenger lists for the Black Whale 1, and even creating language analysis software in case the team contacts unknown civilizations. Working as a Crime Hunter means juggling investigation, strategy, and diplomacy while making sure your information doesn't fall into the wrong hands. Everyday life involves digging through endless records, cross-checking clues, running surveillance, and preparing contingency plans for problems you haven't even seen yet. Your decisions carry weight. Your predictions can shift entire political landscapes, and every case solved is proof that brains can outmatch brawn any day. Jackpot Hunters. If you ever wanted a career where your boss is a billionaire and your salary is measured in billions, Jackpot Hunters are living that dream, one contract at a time. Jackpot Hunters chase cold, hard cash like it owes them money. Their world revolves around contracts, high-stakes deals, and hunting down opportunities that pay big. Sugita is a single-star Jackpot Hunter who signs on with the billionaire Battera for a staggering 50 billion Jenny to clear Greed Island, a rare video game that Battera spent billions just to hoard every existing copy of. The goal is to find a card called Angel's Breath that can heal his comatose lover. Sugita takes the job purely for the money and the professional reputation that comes with completing a legendary challenge. When Battera's lover dies and the billionaire cancels the main 50 billion Jenny contract, Sugita still secures final wages and termination fees, proving that even when the big score falls through, your skills still earn respect and cash. With new threats like the Bomber emerging, he pivots, teaming up with Gon's group to clear the game, staying adaptable and keeping his eye on potential gains. Everyday involves negotiating contracts, analyzing risk, and balancing the promise of huge payoffs against the chaos of unpredictable clients and dangerous missions. Success means wealth, status, and bragging rights, but one misstep could leave you chasing empty purses. In the Jackpot Hunter world, your wallet dictates the adventure, and your sense of profit guides every decision. Head Hunters. Forget tracking beasts or hunting gems, headhunters collect people like rare Pokémon, spotting the ones worth training into legends. Headhunters chase people for their talents and potential. Teradein Neutral is a double star headhunter who scouts, grooms, and recruits new talent, turning promising rookies into elite operatives. As a human resource specialist and later leader of the Pure Paladin Squad, he uses charisma and leadership to inspire others. Though his trusting nature sometimes lands him in hot water. Life as a headhunter is managing personalities, spotting hidden potential, and occasionally dodging the deadly consequence of being too honest in a world full of dangerous hunters. Lost Hunters. Lost Hunters specialize in finding people who have gone missing. Specifically, people who have dropped off the radar and can't be reached. Lupe Highland is the only Lost Hunter shown in the series, handling cases involving lost members of the Hunter Association and serving as part of the Reform Pure Paladin Squad. As a Lost Hunter, your entire job is essentially running missing persons investigation at the professional Hunter level, which means the people you're searching for went missing in circumstances serious enough that regular search efforts wouldn't cut it. You're going into places and situations that swallowed people whole, which requires both the investigator skill to piece together what happened and the physical capability to survive wherever the trail leads. Terrorist Hunters. Terrorist Hunters track down and neutralize terrorism. And the level of expertise required means you're dealing with threats that could affect not just individuals, but entire countries or populations. Botobai Gigante is a triple star terrorist hunter, one of the rarest achievements in the Hunter world, and also a Zodiac and military analyst and a public prosecutor. His knowledge of security and anti-terrorist operations is extensive enough that for the Dark defense team responsible for guarding Beyond Netero, coordinating Nen-based defensive operations, and designing a strategy to prevent a man who is the son of the most powerful hunter who ever lived from escaping custody in the middle of an unprecedented voyage. As a terrorist hunter, your daily existence involves a constant surveillance of threats that most people don't even know exist. You're building intelligence networks, analyzing patterns, coordinating with other high-level officials, and preparing for scenarios that are designed to be catastrophic by people who are very good at designing catastrophic scenarios. Music Hunters. Music Hunters pursue rare musical compositions, rare instruments, and voice talents. And on the surface, that sounds like the most peaceful job in the Hunter world. Then, you hear about Melody and realize immediately that this field has a genuinely terrifying edge. Melody's life changed when her friend played a flute solo from something called the Sonata of Darkness. And listening to just one movement deformed Melody's body and killed her friend outright, mutating him so severely that he died from it. As a direct result of that horrific moment, Melody gained supernatural hearing so precise that she can detect heartbeats. Her entire career as a Music Hunter is now oriented around one goal, finding the complete sheet music for the Sonata of Darkness and destroying it so nobody else has to go what she and her friend experienced. As a Music Hunter, you're constantly globetrotting to track down rare tunes nobody else has heard with a musical knowledge that is basically obsessive level. For Melody, it also means knowing that some music out there is seriously dangerous in ways most people can't even grasp. And keeping people safe from it turns the job from just a career into a full-on mission. Paleograph Hunters. Paleograph Hunters specialize in ancient and lost languages. And their work sits at the intersection of linguistic history, archaeology, and cryptography. Piyon is the only Paleograph Hunter shown in the series and she's a Zodiac, multilingual, fluent in both ancient and modern languages, and skilled enough as a programmer to build language analysis software from scratch. Her expertise becomes essential for the Dark Continent expedition where the intelligence team might make contact with entirely unknown civilizations whose language no living person has ever encountered. As a Paleograph Hunter, your career is spent decoding scripts and languages that the rest of the world has forgotten, which sounds quiet and academic until you're sailing towards an uncharted continent and you're the only person on board who could potentially communicate with whatever lives there. Poacher Hunters. Poacher Hunters spend their careers protecting animals from illegal hunters, which makes them the wildlife conservation arm of the Hunter Association. Ginta is the only Poacher Hunter shown and he's a Zodiac, which means his commitment to animal protection has reached a level that the Association's most elite body considers worth having on their roster. For the Dark Continent expedition, he joins the Floor and Fauna team alongside Cluck gathering information about wildlife in unexplored territory. As a Poacher Hunter, your daily work involves tracking down people who are illegally hunting or trafficking protected animals, which requires investigation skills, physical capability in wild environments, and enough understanding of animal behavior to know what's being targeted and why. Hacker Hunters. Hacker Hunters are cybercrime specialists, basically deep computer and internet experts who deal with digital crimes in the Hunter world. The existence of this specialization tells you a lot about how developed and complex the Hunter world's infrastructure is. A Hunter license that specifically validates someone's cybercrime expertise means the Association recognizes digital threats as seriously as physical ones. As a Hacker Hunter, your work is entirely in the digital space, tracking criminal networks, dismantling cybercrime operations, and using technical skills that most hunters simply don't have. Cute Hunters. Cute Hunters exist to maintain the beauty and youth of their clients, essentially making them the aesthetic specialist of the Hunter world. And yes, someone took the Hunter exam and risked everything to make people look younger. To be fair, any world where wealth and power are everything, an expert in appearance management for the elite probably makes genuinely good money and works with extremely powerful clients who value their services enormously. As a Cute Hunter, your career is significantly less dangerous than most other types. You're unlikely to be chasing Chimera Ants or navigating the Dark Continent, but you're still operating at a professional level in your field that justifies a license. Temp Hunters. Temp Hunters basically handle government gigs that get funneled through the Hunter Association. They get paid a fixed rate depending on how dangerous and complicated the job is, and they are openly ridiculed by other professional hunters for making this their entire career. It's the fast food of Hunter specializations. Essentially, you show up, you do the job, you get paid, and you move on to the next one without any particular prestige attached. What makes Temp Hunters genuinely interesting from a story perspective is what Pariston Hill does with them. He cultivates roughly 200 of them as a personal political block, using their collective votes to manipulate Hunter Association elections, and consistently undermine Netero's policies. So, while Temp Hunters individually might not command respect, a large enough group of them in the right person's hands becomes a significant political force. During the Chimera Ant crisis, Temp Hunters get deployed to handle lower-level operations in the field, and many of them end up on Beyond Netero's team for the Dark Continent expedition. As a Temp Hunter, your life has a certain predictability. You'd know what you're getting paid and roughly what you'll be asked to do. The ceiling is low, the respect is minimal, and you'll spend a lot of your career doing jobs that specialized hunters won't look at twice. But, at the end of the day, you're still a hunter. That alone gives you access, privileges, and freedom that regular people can only dream about. Provisional Hunters Provisional hunters are a unique and temporary classification created specifically for the circumstances surrounding the Dark Continent Expedition. During the 289th Hunter Exam, around 150 Cockeyed Royal Guards made it through Kurapika's screening and get Provisional Hunter Licenses. They're basically brought in as backups, so the Hunter Association can stack up more allies before the voyage kicks off. To even pull this off, they had to tweak the second Hunter Bylaw, which is wild because these Provisional Hunters are allowed in even if they can't use Nen. Of course, there's a catch. Their license are temporary. Once the voyage ends, so does their Hunter status. As a Provisional Hunter, your entire career has a built-in expiration date, and your status is tied entirely to one specific mission. You have real access and real responsibilities during that window, but you're also working with the constant awareness that when the ship returns, so does your Hunter career.

- Sidestepping stereotypes of hunters. - Why the Brain Stereotypes: A Silent Cognitive Trap: Kind: captions Language: en stereotyping and above bird. During a hunting trip, my friend and I experienced something quite funny. Later, I was able to interpret what had happened in a scientific way. We were about 15 years old, full of excitement and ambition to catch something valuable, even though what we had was only a single petted gun. While walking along a narrow dirt road lined with cactus trees on both sides, we discovered an opening on the left side, like a gate between the cactus plants and decided to go through it. The moment we stepped in, we saw pomegranate trees arranged in a neat row. It was actually a fence separating two pieces of land. We walked a little further with the pomegranate trees on our right. Suddenly, I noticed the head of a bird between the leaves. I could not see its body because the foliage was too dense. We both started running. My friend stood behind me while I positioned myself behind an olive tree so that I was facing the pomegranate tree where I had seen the bird only a few meters away. When I changed my angle to aim, I suddenly noticed other birds moving their heads between the leaves. My friend whispered urgently for me to hurry and shoot at least one before the rest escaped. I told him, "No, not necessarily. Maybe these are Abuum birds. They might not even fly away." I aimed and fired at the first bird. Another bird stood next to it. It did not fly away. The foliage was extremely dense. In my mind, I felt lucky. These must be above my room birds, the stupid birds. And I would hunt all of them. I fired at the second bird. I missed. It did not move. It just kept turning its head left and right. Suddenly, I heard a voice from a distance. A man was shouting. His house was located at the end of the long pomegranate row. He stood on the roof, waving and shouting insults. I shouted back, "Forgive us. We did not come here to eat pomegranates, just to hunt birds." He yelled, "You are shooting my birds." I placed bird line traps and the birds get stuck one after another and you are shooting them with a gun. I will not go into what happened afterwards because the main point is to tell about the story of that bird Abun and how I got caught in the trap of assuming that all birds that do not fly away when shot at belong to this so-called type. By the way, this name is not a scientific classification. It is merely a local nickname. The story of Abun actually began about a month before the previous hunting incident. It happened when I borrowed a pellet gun and did not know how to adjust its sight. One day, my cousin came over and I told him, "Let's go hunting." He tried the pellet gun and said it needed sight adjustment. We went out onto the dirt road behind our house in the village. Before we even used the piece of cardboard we were supposed to shoot at repeatedly to calibrate the site, my cousin, the hunter, suddenly spotted a bird. He said, "What a coincidence. We can adjust the site using that bird." I asked how. He replied that it was stupid and would not fly away even if a pellet passed right next to its head. After several attempts of shooting and adjusting the sight, he eventually managed to hit it. I witnessed it myself. From that moment on, in my mind, there was a bird called Abu the stupid. Because of that generalization, stereotyping, and the absence of rational judgment, I later ended up in trouble when I tried to shoot birds that were actually stuck on glue traps placed between pomegranate leaves. How many false assumptions and dangerous stereotypes exist in our lives? Assumptions that lead us into problems with serious consequences. They can only be eliminated through questioning, investigation, and the use of reasoned judgment. Stereotyping is at its core a natural outcome of how the brain works, not merely a thinking error. The human brain constantly seeks to reduce effort, especially since the cerebral cortex responsible for deep analysis consumes a significant amount of energy. As a result, instead of processing every situation or individual independently, the brain relies on quick mental shortcuts based on similarity and past experience. These shortcuts allow us to form rapid generalizations, which is what we call stereotyping. Over time, this behavior becomes not just habitual, but biologically reinforced. When we rely on these shortcuts and feel successful or correct, the brain releases dopamine, reinforcing the pattern and making it more likely to repeat. Conversely, when we face new or complex situations, cortisol levels rise, pushing us away from cognitive effort and back toward quick judgments. Stereotyping does not arise because people refuse to think but because the brain is designed to choose the easier less energy consuming path. Therefore deep thinking and true analysis require conscious effort to resist this natural tendency and move from quick judgment to genuine understanding. Ultimately, stereotyping operates silently, gradually shaping how a person sees themselves and the world around them. With repetition, these judgments turn into internal beliefs that may weaken confidence, reduce motivation, and create imaginary limitations on what is possible. Over time, the impact of stereotyping extends beyond the individual mind into relationships, opportunities, and decisions. In this way, stereotyping is not just a passing thought, but a hidden force that reshapes our perception of ourselves and others, making awareness the first step toward freedom from it. Thanks for watching.

  6. @IngridTaylar [1 likes]: First, you hear this PR for hunting all of the time, but hunters were not the nation's first conservationists. There were many before who objected to hunting practices early on -- people like Harriet Hemenway who sounded alarms about bird hunting long before hunters had the consciousness to realize they were hunting species toward extinction. Second, hunters' involvement in conservation did come about for self-serving reasons as this video indicates, because they realized their practices would leave nothing left to shoot or trap. They, in fact, created the dastardly situation that required monumental conservation efforts. In these histories, important non-hunting figures like John Muir are ignored -- John Muir who was a primary influence on Theodore Roosevelt, whose conservation achievements can't be diminished but who is also known for such despicable practices as shooting an ostrich while she sat on a nest. If you take an overall survey of how much conservation is done by non-hunters and taxpayers, it's completely inaccurate to paint one group as ineffective, while aggrandizing the achievements of the other. Lastly, if you spend any time in the field as I do, you'll see all manner of hunting practice legal under the auspices of "conservation." But so many of those practices and actions are not motivated by conservation in the least, rather toward recreational enjoyment -- and so much of this treatment of wildlife is brutally executed (e.g. "helicoptering" geese after they've been shot, injury rates as high as 18 to 50 percent in some hunting). It's justified entirely by statistics rather than ethical and compassionate components which should guide our actions toward other species.

area… …and we fund it through very selective… …very sustainable trophy hunting I’ll be back Money from foreign hunters, like Dan… …enables Marina to keep this land for wildlife It’s a very nice, quiet morning. Little bit of wind I think it’s a nice morning for hunting Most people assume that trophy hunting is about killing… …but that’s not the part that I’m looking forward to I enjoy the stalk, I enjoy the challenge of it And yes you have to take the animal… …but that’s what funds this operation And the local community, where Marina lives, benefits too I have a deal with the local village elders… …that I will feed the 260-plus children in the school… …with meat from the hunt… …but in exchange we have no poaching… …or interference of any kind And it’s a system that works It’s all about involving local rural communities… …in benefiting from the wildlife If communities don’t benefit from the wildlife… …there’s little incentive to look after it Living on this landscape is hard There’s not much choices And this wildlife is wild People romanticise that these animals are beautiful They are beautiful animals, but they are also very dangerous And they can kill people or they can make our kids not go to school If wildlife becomes an economic asset… …people will want to protect it, rather than poach or kill it Critics of trophy hunting say… …that it only benefits the privileged in a country… …like Marina, who operates a private farm But in Namibia, and other countries, this is not always the case Nearly 20% of its land is run by local communities They choose how they want to use it The communities use the land… …as farmers, or they can be livestock owners They can be crop-growers The conservancies choose to have trophy hunting… …it’s also benefiting them financially And that is the only way that we can motivate these communities… …to be able to look after the lands for conservation values… …at the moment If trophy hunting is implemented well… …and critically with the buy-in of local and national… …communities and governments… …you really can see an increase in the wildlife populations According to international data… …the only African countries with increasing populations… …of wild lions… …use trophy hunting… …including of lions… …as part of their wildlife management And back in Namibia... …there were only 12 white rhinos in 1968… …since trophy hunting of them began in 1982… …their numbers have rebounded And valuable revenue has been raised for their conservation In order for countries to see these results… …trophy hunting has to be well-implemented As it is in Namibia They are downwind from us By law… …Dan has to be accompanied… …by a registered Namibian professional hunter His guide today is Johnny These are giraffe footprints You can see it’s very fresh It’s early from this morning Only a maximum of 2% of certain species… …can be killed in trophy hunting… …and in many cases even less are taken And there are strict government quotas… …on which species of animal can be hunted… …and for some species, which sex For trophy hunting we don’t shoot young animals… …because we use these younger males for breeding… …and big bulls they are not breeding big bulls They don’t breed anymore, they just eat and sleep They’ve been searching for an old oryx bull for hours I think we can get close to this oryx… …because we have zebra here on the right The zebra saw us Namibia is a peaceful and reasonably well-governed country… Rifle Load up …which is why trophy hunting can be well-managed here But in other countries, it’s not so simple Trophy hunting is by no means a panacea Done badly, it can negatively affect animal populations For instance, around Hwange National Park in Zimbabwe They had far too high quotas for male lions there It just was completely unsustainable Corruption can also be rife in some countries Some estimate that across just seven African countries… …trophy hunting brings in more than $200m a year But it’s often unclear… …how much reaches local stakeholders Yet in places where revenues do reach local people… …trophy-hunting money is a financial life-line For example, in Namibia… …the annual revenue from hunting is $28.5m And in Zimbabwe 770,000 households benefit in some way from it In many cases, revenue from trophy hunting… …can’t be replaced by other incomes There’s a real misconception that photo-tourism… …could easily be substituted for trophy hunting… …in most places and that’s just not the case For tourism you need stability… …you need low disease risk… …you need infrastructure… …you need scenic areas with lots of wildlife In many, many hunting areas that’s not the case In Namibia frequent droughts make it difficult… …for large tourist operations But it’s ideal terrain for hunters And Johnny has spotted some prey Hunting involves killing It’s just a necessary part of it Is that what I’m here for? Absolutely not Is it necessary? Absolutely so It’s a big old bull Get ready with the rifle Safety off, safety off You see it between the trees? Yes Just aim dead on his chest Congratulations He’s down I didn’t see it until the last moment… …because it was behind a tree, but Johnny knew it was there It’s a heart-lung shot He went right down with one shot I’ve seen trophy photos… …which are disrespectful of the animal and I don’t approve But that’s not the way we do it We respect the animal, we honour it… …we use every bit of it… …so I think you can do it tastefully… …and that’s what we strive for Calls for trophy-hunting bans are intensifying Britain appears to be pushing ahead with a ban… …on the import of trophies And some American states have also pushed for import bans But blanket bans and restrictions are not the answer There is no conservation model in the world that is perfect And I will also not claim that ours is perfect Over the years we have managed to bring wildlife… …back in areas in countries where it was nearly decimated I fail to understand why would you like to close down… …a really good model that work for southern Africa? In 2020, more than 50 African community leaders… …signed an open letter criticising online influencers… …who call for bans on trophy hunting It’s these local communities… …that have to foot the bill for conservation At the moment, all the costs are borne locally… …and all the benefits generally go internationally I think we do need a new model of conservation… …and one where the international community… …plays a far more active role in funding the costs of conservation We cannot expect the poorest people in the world… …to maintain something that largely is valued… …by the richest people in the world It is not a sustainable model And crucially, local communities and conservationists… …have to be listened to… …rather than ignored or vilified Africans were born with wildlife, they understand wildlife It is important that we listen… …to those silent community conservation voices The people that are impacted by wildlife Developing new solutions for conservation… …could take years—and 1m or more species… …are facing extinction now While trophy hunting is no silver bullet… …taking this conservation tool off the table… …with no viable alternative… …will place millions of acres of habitat… …and the species that rely on it, at risk Hi, my name is Katie Bailes… …I’m a producer here at The Economist If you’d like to learn more about the threats to African wildlife… …please click the link opposite Thanks for watching and don’t forget to subscribe

Addressing invasive plants/trees, maintaining trails, and trimming trees around the edges of food plots is a never ending task but it gets us outside and gives us some exercise. The last boss is a lot less cool, Princeldren. but he can drop the bracers of the eclipse. Now, I know this thing doesn't look very impressive, but as far as bracers go, this thing is actually the best we can get pre- raid, but of course, he didn't drop them anyway. And with that, I can officially say that I have done my very first run of Dire Mall. Now, there's actually three different versions of Dire Mall. That one was the West version, but what I needed was the Dire Mall East version. So, I stayed around and I waited for a group. And whilst I was, someone in the group told me that there was a railro mob in the middle that has the potential to drop some very expensive items. So me and the rogue jumped in. I put aspect of the cheetah on and I kited the mob around the arena whilst the rogue did his absolute best to not take aggro off of me. And well, after about 6 minutes of kiting, me and the rogue managed to get him down. And he dropped the Orb of Deception and the dream walker armor. combined, they would sell for about 45 gold, which definitely isn't nothing. And well, I won both of them. With that now done, though, we could actually get started. Now, the weird thing is we weren't going to be running the dungeon normally. We were going to be doing what is known as a jump run, which basically targets the last couple of bosses and skips most of the trash, which for me was a bit unfortunate because I was actually in here because I wanted to get the Dire Mall key, but you know, it's all for the experience, right? So, we sneakily made our way through all of the walking plants until we got to the first boss, hydro spawn. Very similar to Noxy in this one because he does basically nothing and sometimes spawns smaller versions of himself. Awesome. But he did drop some leather boots. The next boss is Ze Thornhof. Lootwise, we don't care. Now, I don't like this boss for one specific reason. He sacrificed Gerald, which did indeed kill him. But with my heart now broken because of Gerald's death, we moved all the way down to the final boss, Alzen the Wild Shaper, who once again doesn't drop anything we care about. The only real mechanic that he has is that when his health reaches 50%, he spawns a gazillion imps to come and attack us. And the rogue did almost die, but in the end, we were able to take him down. But we weren't done just yet because someone in the group then went and spawned in the tier boss, Iselian. Now, this would be pretty huge for us because of the weapon that could drop, the Huntsman Harpoon, offering a massive plus 35 agility, which obviously is the absolute best weapon for us pre- raid. But just like everything else in this game, it did not drop. Now, although we did just do a run of Dire Mall East, I still wanted to get myself the key. So, I once again stuck around. But this time, we were actually going to clear the dungeon normally. Now, the way we get the key is basically a quest, but it's not really a quest. We have to talk to the impusin multiple times, and each time we do, he would run away and we would have to chase him. So, that's what we did. We chased him all the way around the entrance room until he goes down a little hallway where we can speak to him for one last time and he transforms into a giant demonic imp who we have to then take down and then loot the key. Now, unfortunately, we were making our way through to the end boss where we accidentally pulled one too many packs of mobs and it wiped us and then the healer left. So, unfortunately, we had no choice but to abandon the run, which honestly I was okay with because it was getting pretty late. Anyway, when I logged on the next day, I can't lie, I really just wanted to do some questing, but looking at my map, we had been literally everywhere. And that's when I remembered Silhifice. I had totally forgotten that Silithis does actually have quests available for levelers. So, I wasted no time and began flying over there. Of course, we don't have the flight point yet. So, we were running from Onoro Crater, which is perfectly fine, honestly, because Angoro is just such a beautiful zone. After a short 5-minute trip, we had made it to Sinarian Hold, and I could begin picking up most quests. Not every quest, because some of them were level 60 storybased quests, and I just didn't want to do those just yet. Now, here's the thing about Silithus. Aesthetically, I would honestly say it's just a bit meh. The bug theme, in my opinion, has kind of worn off. But that might just be due to the massive amount of time that I had spent grinding out Earth Strike and running AQ40. However, that's not to say that I didn't enjoy our time in this zone, though, because if you look at the quests, you can see that every single one of them is a kill quest, which are of course my favorite. And to be honest, that was kind of me set for a couple hours. All the mobs we needed to kill spawned everywhere, so I could just chill for a bit and run around and kill as many things as I wanted. On the plus side, I actually made a solid bit of gold from this as well, primarily due to the sandworm meat because my main character has maxed out cooking and I could craft the meat into smoked desert dumplings and sell them for like four gold each. And everything was perfectly normal until I got an invite to join another Dire Mall run. And well, how could I possibly say no? So, this run started out being perfectly normal. We got down to tendrous wartwood and took him down. And you'll never believe he actually dropped the waist piece this time. Finally, a piece of gear for us. And the rest of run was nothing out of the ordinary. We moved upstairs where I was reunited with the quest giver from earlier and I could turn in the quest, which just so happens to push us over into level 59, which means only one more level to go. But we get down to Imolar and we take him down just like normal. And that's when the warlock started up a quest. A quest that was apparently for the epic warlock mount. And almost immediately, we were getting swarmed by imps and demons. And at first, it really wasn't that bad. But after literally 10 minutes of nothing but imps, everyone's health was low. Our mana was drained. And sadly, we wiped. But we respawned. And I think everyone was expecting to have to restart the entire encounter. But no, we were actually right at the end of it anyway. and we were able to finish off the last of the imps. But that's when the big scary demon guy and his mount spawned in to attack us. And to be honest, he was a lot easier to take down than the trash was, which is something I've come to notice about WoW classic. Sometimes the trash is actually harder than the bosses. But we are not done just yet because we then went to take down Prince toldren and claim my shiny new bracers, which never dropped. Now that we had gotten at least some kind of gear upgrade, I once again returned to Silith to finish up anything that we had left there. And again, it was just a whole lot of killing. Scorpions, worms, spiders, you name it. If it was in the zone, you best believe we were going to be killing it. And I also had to go up to the very northwest of the zone to collect some twilight tablets. And to be honest, I didn't think that this area would be as quiet as it was since usually there's hordes of people farming these elementals for elemental earth. I then decided to head south for a bit for another solo elite quest. What we had to do was pick up Nogle Satchel. And I think the idea behind this quest is that you're supposed to kill the elite mob death clasp, but again, we are playing the most broken class in the game and we were able to do it completely solo. We also had to pay a visit to all three of the Twilight Camps in order to kill three different Twilight Keepers in order to collect some book pages. I then turned that quest in and had to immediately return to the Twilight Camps to collect encrypted Twilight Text. Now, I am very familiar with farming these because a few months ago, these Twilight Texts were going for upwards of four gold each and for a couple months at least, farming these would be my main source of gold income. Sadly though, these days they have dropped to only about one gold each. I then returned to Ortel for a final time and that was it. We had done basically every single quest in Silith and I got to say it was a lot of fun. Like I said, aesthetically I thought it was pretty ugly, but the questing here is just so chilled out. We were now getting pretty close to level 60. We only needed about a quarter of a level. And initially I wanted to go to Upper Black Rock Spire because I had just picked up the final Enixia attunement quest, but I ended up going for a run of Lower Black Rock Spire instead, which I forgot to hit record on. And the unfortunate thing is whilst doing this run, one of the officers in my guild had offered to take me a level 59 into Zolar Rub, which would have been an insanely epic way to hit level 60. But I think I've made it pretty clear that I don't like deserters and I kind of felt the need to stay with this group. Anyway, we first made our way down to Hunt Master Voon. Now, if you remember way back on the Druid series, we actually got the gems for the UBRS key in two runs. However, this time around, we don't really need it to be honest. We then moved upstairs to Shadow Hunter Vosch. Now, this one is pretty big for us because of the crossbow black crow plus three agility and 1% hit. I believe this is one of, if not the best ranged weapon for us. Unfortunately though, it dropped Rip Hook, which actually isn't that good for us at all. We then dropped down and took on Mother Smolder Web. Pretty simple fight. As long as you kill the little spiderlings, you're good. And she dropped the Wild Heart Boots. Next up is Quartermaster Ziggress. Now, originally I thought that the Cloudrunner girdle that he could drop would be the best in slot belt for us, but turns out it would just be a straightup downgrade. And weirdly, he didn't drop a single item. Not even that weird foot locker thing that he usually drops. We then take down Hian. Not really much to say about this one to be honest, apart from the fact that I managed to get the quest here done. And lastly is of course Overlord Wormthalac. Now this one can actually be quite tough. I remember when I was tanking it, he can sometimes send you flying into the air, which resets threat, but apart from that, he does just hit incredibly hard. Now, he did actually drop the Beast Master's mantle, which I needed on. Now, I know this individually looks like it's just a straightup downgrade from our current ones, but I believe if you get the Beastalker's mantle, chest, and legs, you can then turn all three of those in to get the best in slot pair of boots. So, I thought I might as well take it just in case we decide to do that. Now, after our lower Black Rock Spire run, we were so incredibly close to hitting max level that I was just hoping that maybe, just maybe, our quest turnins would get us to level 60. But unfortunately, that was not the case. But not to worry because I randomly got a message asking if I wanted to come to Scholamance. And well, obviously, I said yes. So, we entered into the dungeon and started clearing our way through. And really only after about 5 minutes it happened. We hit level 60 and I can now officially say that I have successfully leveled four entire characters in World of Warcraft Classic. If you have made it this far into the video, then please let me know in the comments because seriously, it means the absolute world to me and I cannot thank you guys enough. call more of a wedge-shaped core as opposed to a prismatic core but you start by facing the piece and this is essentially going to turn into your Clovis point preform and if you look at the casts of artifacts of preforms what you'll find is once they start to buy face one side is typically fluted really really early just like this one that I have done and one of the reasons for that is because once you start the bi-facing process one side is typically going to have some longer overshot flaking and then the other side is going to be a little bit more lenticular a little bit more rounded and that offers a great opportunity to set up yet another blade core removal which is essentially the flute off of the preform then once you have this initial flute developed this is actually what you're going to Base building your Clovis point off of an early a very early initial flute now you may work on this piece and essentially erase this flute and then end up fluting it again so that's something that you'll absolutely see within Clovis points is they aren't necessarily just made and then fluted and never retouched they might be thinned further and then fluted multiple times removing those blade core style of flakes to use and then as the piece gets thinner and thinner and you've got down really to the thickness that you're looking for and that last fluid is run that's going to be again what you're going to build your first flute side of the point on more in a unifacial fashion then you can flip the point over and continue to remove material from either side but only going to about Center which again gives it a lot more of a rounded lenticular shape and then that's when you're going to see the other flute being removed and sometimes that results in one really clean flute like we would start off with here a really beautiful clean flute that may remain in the point and then as we flip it over if this point was in fact finished we might have a single good platform to remove one more really nice Channel flake or we may have to set up multiple platforms by removing two relief Flakes a one a two on the other side and then a third up the center and then sometimes that fluting can happen in one shot it may happen in two shots or three and sometimes even four individual small indirect percussion flutes removed off of the opposite side of the first initial flute so there really are multiple ways to go about making Clovis especially because there's start to be a lot of variances in different types of Clovis especially as they start to spread out so you will see some different tools and even techniques being used even within Clovis points made in the same area so we gave you that really quick rundown on Clovis manufacturer just kind of as an overall blanket but that need to be considered and really what we found in this hunt was not only were these Spears really not efficient enough for good accurate throws but they also had so much oscillation because of their giant size but then they also had a lot of drop off and elevation in the throw made them very hard to throw accurately and they had a tremendous amount of kinetic friction compared to very thin tapered four shafts on smaller Spears so I feel like we had a really good idea in taking this all the way on one side of the spectrum saying what are the heaviest Spears we could possibly throw issue there's really simple really too much friction with these giant Javelin sized spear shafts despite having really really finely Barrel tapered front ends and then these points that are contextually sized really just struggle to cut a large enough wound channel for that large spear to slide in behind it also keeping in mind that these Clovis points really don't have the very steep edge geometry that later transitional paleo or archaic points do such as Dalton's or Boland style of points that are very very sharp at the tip they're not rounded at all so that again decreases kinetic friction and promotes deeper penetration so even though we were able to essentially prove that you know my initial idea of getting these massive Spears and throwing it and we're going to see have been coming through in very very cold place to seeing temperatures and certainly would have had very sophisticated clothing and with sophisticated clothing also comes very sophisticated hunting implements and other toolage and one of the things also associated with these very primitive caveman which is quite funny also because Clovis people really didn't live in caves but in those same depictions of the scantily clad cavemen uh they're also carrying very robust and rudimentary Spears and they think uh that they're running up to mammoths and jabbing them with them and really that is not what the archaeological record shows and the reason we're really confident that they're not running up to megafauna just simply stabbing or throwing these like javelins is because these are not the points or the blades associated with megafauna archaeological sites what they're typically finding are these very very small projectiles which will talk a lot more about as we work through this documentary but these are certainly not well suited to either butchering animals or attaching to these big giant thrusting Spears so what good would these little points do put on these great big thrusting spear handles well they weren't using thrusting Spears to take down the megafauna or bison for that matter they would have brought with them the atlattle the atlattle uses mechanical leverage adds an extra joint into the arm and then also the leverage of the atlatal or the thrower component to impose significant velocity onto the spear shaft and the spear shafts can then become smaller using velocity rather than Mass allowing them to fly significantly further and also faster and also on a much thinner shaft much like this which decreases friction kinetic friction which is needed to penetrate very large animals so if we look at some of the casts of the very earliest people that were in North America fifteen thousand five hundred sixteen thousand years ago this is a cast from the freaking site in Texas and then this isn't a cast but this is a re production the cast is behind me a reproduction of the Dumbo Mammoth kill site and if you look at the size of these projectiles that are being found they're very small but they fit perfectly onto our atlatal Throne spear shafts meant for deep penetration and the atlattle is so old that nobody can truly put a date to it some experts say it says young as 20 000 years old and some say as old as 40 perhaps even a hundred thousand years old the use of this weapon and hunting Implement system has absolutely been used extensively worldwide in all the human inhabited continents and used extensively to hunt everything from the size of rabbits on up to mammoths so here at Hunt primitive we've done a phenomenal plausibly answer the reason for the loss of a lot of coastal artifacts as well as what looks to be a significant decrease in human population at the same time as the extinction of the megafauna so I and then it slowly starts to come back until thousand a couple thousand years later during Dalton culture when it explodes again so they were went through a massive Devastation somehow either with an impact or other climate catastrophe and then were depleted significantly in population slowly climbed back until populations came that people could work together and then the population exploded again and one of the other pieces that was really important that I took from Dr Christopher Morris lecture was his research search and talking about the blood residue analysis being done on projectile points and that is known as crossover immunoelectrophoresis and that's not checking for DNA on the points it's using the crossover proteins from live animals and then comparing that to blood residue not like blood stains on the outside of the point but say like blood that will have slid underneath some of these micro fractures in the stone and have been preserved that long where they can test that against say like a modern day bison and then ancient bison will pop positive on that same thing with modern day rabbit would pop positive modern day cats would pop positive deer all of that kind of stuff same of course with elephants and throughout not only in his research of submitting points to be tested but then also looking at other tests being done across the continent they're really finding everything that people were hunting including camels and horses and mammoths and mastodons the Bison was without a doubt the number one animal that Clovis people were hunting and that's also a clue as well because some of the later people like Folsom and Midland are almost exclusively associated with bison kills so this process of being able to look at these projectiles and testing for about Clovis been a very fun project and I'm and I'm glad that hunt primitive was able to take part and recreate and hunt and Butcher and research and do so so much work with Clovis that so many or climate change in one way or another and also at the same time as the extinction of the proboscilians the mammoths the mastodons and even the other megaphone even though there aren't a tremendous because this is such a long time ago that of course very few of these uh bones would even be preserved so the fact that we have as many as we do is really amazing within itself but I don't think Clovis is the height is the best of primitive hunting as has been thought previously they think well because that fluting maybe has a very high threshold for failure here that they obviously knew what they were doing so they built that specifically to be megafauna killing implements and that may be true to some extent but I don't believe that it was the height of Flint napping or primitive hunting I think it was more of a cultural fad if anything that this came into popularity probably more likely for easier hafting and for thinner rafting on those transitions that we've shown you earlier because those smooth transitions really tapered steeply allow for much better penetration into very thick woolly hair and hide so I really think that the fluting was nothing more than a hafting aid and it wasn't used previously but that it was an advancement and then even when it was used post Clovis and after the megafauna it was still used for a thousand or so so years even into some of the Dalton points had flutes maybe one side was fluted and the other side wasn't but after the transitional paleo stage really the fluting just disappears and it's not because the people weren't skilled you actually start seeing that the flake scars on the bifacial lateral flakes start to be significantly better than those found in the Clovis tools so they're simply getting better at thinning their biases by faces and therefore no longer really need the fluted technology to be able to make thin streamlined points for penetration so if you ask me as a professional primitive Builder and Hunter what do I think about Clovis I don't think that there's an inherently anything wrong about it I think the the Clovis fluting is really just to Aid in a much smoother transition to benefit penetrating of proboscidians very Woolly and very thick hides of woolly mammoths and even the thicker hides of of the larger Colombian mammoths and then the mastodons because penetration is really the name of the game as opposed to laceration which we start seeing at a much later archaic time so I don't necessarily think that Clovis was the height of Flint napping and primitive hunting technology I think it was uh it did have a utilitarian base but I think it was also very cultural the way that it was done and I think it was effective for doing that job even though the job could be done with other tools I think it fit exactly where it needed to fit and that's also one of the reasons that it fell out of favor and then was not used in later point typology one of the quick points I would like to bring up about the potential more rounded distal ends the the leading ends of projectiles in the Paleo time being slightly more rounded than we see especially in archaic time so in my previous studies of working with bow and arrow technology we find that very steep sharp tip geometry is is exceptionally important for penetration especially on a lighter projectile like an arrow but with the atlatel and the spear that is much heavier carrying more momentum it is important to have that very sharp tip if we're talking about pure penetration but we can get away with having a little bit more rounded of a tip as seen in a lot of paleo points especially if the points are much smaller because we're decreasing friction but the purpose really that I found of the more rounded point is that it's less likely to snap that little needle point off in the event of a Miss but more importantly we found that when you're carrying multiple Spears and especially if you're running those longer Spears unlike arrows that are much shorter and they don't have a lot of rattle you can carry a handful of arrows and they don't bounce and oscillate when you hold a handful of Spears they're significantly longer and as you're running they tend to bounce and that bouncing as those needle tips bounce together they can snap off and then you go to throw and now you essentially have a blunt ended projectile Point as opposed to that needle sharp tip so the way to avoid that in Paleolithic times would be to make projectiles with slightly more rounded distal ends as opposed to the needle sharp tips even though you really want that penetration you're going to be able to sharpen those tips a little bit especially like you would see in the Kim's Wick or the Dumbo points but they're not going to be those perfect needle tips that are going to get easily broken off if your points happen to clock together while you're running so here at Hunt primitive we do a lot of building and hunting and then also a - How Did Hunter-Gatherers Live?: Kind: captions Language: en Hunter Gathering is the oldest and longest lasting human survival strategy Hunter Gathering involves human beings foraging for plants and animals for food and this lifestyle dominated human history for over 90% of Humanity's existence what is Hunter Gathering why do human beings do it and why is it still practiced today this is a deep dive into one of Humanity's most profound survival behaviors you're watching all things humanities [Music] human beings are built to survive we get hungry when we don't eat we get thirsty when we don't drink and we strive for well-being and a sense of purpose Hunter Gathering fulfills these needs as human beings forage for wild plants and animals for food Hunter Gathering began roughly 1.8 million years ago with Homo erectus Homo erectus was an extinct species of human that lived around 2 Mill ion years ago they originated in Africa and began migrating into Europe Asia and as far as Australia this took place over hundreds of thousands of years on the other hand modern human beings which we call Homo sapiens adopted Hunter Gathering about 200,000 years ago when our species first appeared Hunter Gathering was Humanity's most successful adaptation to the natural world and stayed as the dominant form of human subsistence up until about 12,000 years ago this changed when Agriculture and animal domestication began in Southwest Asia and meso America which we now call Mexico and Central America a key quality of hunter gatherers is that they do not grow their own food instead they are nomadic meaning that they move around and therefore forage around within an area to gather supplies hunter gatherers use various tools including Stone blades to help them hunt for a particular animal these types of animals of course depend on the geography of the hunter gatherer if you are Hunter gathering in meso America for example you would look for animals in the region like armadillos and wild boes hunter gatherer societies typically consist of small mobile groups of 10 to 12 adults and children in some cases they can be as large as 100 individuals in a particular group group generally speaking hunter gatherer societies have low population densities typically raging from2 to 10 people per square mile to give you an idea of how small this is modern-day Paris in France has about 52,000 people per square mile that's a whopping 5,200 times more dense than Hunter gathery societies hunter gatherers don't grow their food because they follow an immediate return Behavior whereas resources are used immediately this is very different to agricultural societies where food is stored hunter gatherers also move around to follow food sources making it difficult to partake in agriculture this is because agriculture involves staying in a particular location to ensure the crops have enough time to grow and attend it to properly Hunter gather societies often lack formal leadership structures while some individuals can be recognized for their wisdom or skills there are usually no chiefs or Kings there are also no Central authorities that dictate how their society functions however in most hunter gatherer societies men are typically responsible for hunting larger game while women and children gather plant Foods small animals and other resources for the most part this is because men are physically bigger stronger and faster and Performing these tasks is much easier for men however the IDE a of men hunting for larger game is not true of all hunter gatherer societies we do have evidence that the actar women from the Philippines hunted with knives bows and arrows we also have evidence that the hadza women from northern Tanzania would even hunt for large game on the whole large game hunting is done by men because of their physical advantages nonetheless women mainly partook in gathering which involves collecting wild plant food such as fruits nuts and seeds and it also included fishing which incorporates using aquatic resources in coastal areas rivers and lakes a key feature of hunter gatherers is that they live a subsistent lifestyle this means that the purpose of Hunter Gathering is to ensure the survival of their community and not to produce a food surplus a food surplus is where a society produces more food than is needed and it allows human beings to specialize in producing a particular product or specializing in a particular practice like the military or craftsmanship this is the reason why hunter gatherers do not specialize because their primary focus of daily life is ensuring that their society can eat hunter gatherers are not interested in gathering large amounts of food and selling it to other groups they are primarily concerned with feeding their society at the same time there are sites like gockley tap in Turkey which shows that hunter gatherers had built a large settlement with megalithic structures as we said before most Hunter gather societies are often non-h hierarchical with a limited focus on Gathering wealth there is often a strong emphasis on sharing in hunter gatherer societies which ensures resources flow through the community this also happens because of the nomadic lifestyle of hunter gatherers and it makes it difficult for them to accumulate possession because they are difficult to transport there is some evidence that suggests that hunter gatherers had forms of religion the oldest and most fundamental religious belief among hunter gatherers was animism animism is the belief that all natural objects and the universe itself possess a spirit or a soul in animistic religion supernatural beings may appear As Natural objects like lightning or specific rocks and stones with this in mind hunter gatherers also practiced Shamanism where individuals would Enter Trans states to interact with the spiritual World Chans would also perform rituals including healing sorcery rain making and divination shamans aimed to enter Altered States Of Consciousness through drumming singing dancing and also physical exhaustion it's worth noting that Shamanism may have played a key role in the transition from hunting and Gathering to farming in Southwest Asia as a way to cope with the uncertainty of climate and economic changes hunter gatherer religions were often polytheistic featuring many gods that were often playful and would intervene in the lives of the gatherers it's important to remember that their religious life was not arranged in a power hierarchy like we see in contemporary religions today while shamans were present they did not hold the same power as saved the church for the most part hunter gatherers were generally practical people and did not Embrace too much magic or Superstition their religious ceremonies were often more about embracing reality than trying to change it there was also no real dogmas in Hunter gather a religion different groups could have varying stories and practices without offending one another a good example of this is Abra Australian hunter gatherers with their dream time stories dreaming stories would change depending on Clans language groups and geography we do have evidence that hunter gatherers created sculptures for religious purposes a good example of this is Li man an ivory statuette found in Germany that depicts a human figure with a lion's head when hunter gatherers create something that has part human and part animal features is often associated with beliefs and shamanic practices it's also worth noting that creating lion man would have taken roughly 400 hours to make using Stone Age tools the large time commitment implies that the sculpture had great importance for its creators and these hunter gatherers saw extreme value in it after all these hunter gatherers balanced their need to secure food with sculpting this particular figurine another key Behavior with the en Hunter gather community Comm unities is resource sharing this is where there is a mutual exchange and sharing of resources particularly meat from hunting this is a crucial aspect of Hunter Gathering communities as it is often described as a gift economy this is where the members of a community use goods and services that are given away instead of sold in a gift economy there's no expectation of immediate or future rewards instead hunted gatherers do this as the basis of spreading wealth within their society and looking out for one another this is vastly different to how capitalistic markets work where Goods exchanged voluntarily for something in return the ethos behind the gift economy is communitarian and not liberal and individualistic like contemporary Western economies when it comes to life expectancy roughly 57% of hunter gatherers reach the age of 15 with a life expectancy between 21 to 37 years for those who survive adolescence approximately 70% of deaths are due to diseases 20% from violence or evidence from 10% showing death from degenerative diseases once again the modern world does not face these problems because our food surplus means we can specialize and take time to produce medicines hun gatherers only need to work for about 2 to 3 hours a day to ensure sufficient food for every everyone this leaves substantial time for leisure activities it's interesting to consider that in the modern world people work between 7 to 8 hours per day on average which is very different to the hunter gatherer working life while hun gatherer societies have largely been replaced by Agricultural and industrial societies some groups still continue to practice this lifestyle however these remaining groups face ongoing pressure from expanding agricultural communities and the loss of traditional lands this is because capitalism and industrialization works as a force meaning that functioning in a society where technology and devices are needed to progress traditional Hunter gather societies are not very competitive and they often struggle to keep up hunter gatherer societies have provided valuable insights into Human Social organization diet and also our adaptation to diverse environments the study continues to inform our understanding of human evolution and how societies can develop hunter gatherers are very aware of the finite resources that they live off they also could survive in tough climates by intelligently allocating resources building shelters out of wood Stone and ice and using animal hides and plant fibers for clothes Hunter gather ERS would even use food preservation techniques like drying smoking and fermenting to prevent food shortages during laner Seasons at Star Car a Mesolithic site in North Yorkshire hunter gatherers maintain their way of life despite two significant climate events that cause temperature drops of 10° C when not hunting and Gathering hunter gatherers relax by playing games talking to one another
